contexts:
A Timeless Kitchen Design Sits at the Heart of This Renovated 19th-Century Stone House: Boasting a beautifully [timeless kitchen design](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/5-timeless-kitchen-design-ideas), Stone Crop in the rolling green countryside near Elora, Ontario, is a rare example of early [settler architecture](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/colonial-architecture-everything-you-need-to-know) that has been carefully updated. Built in 1833 by Thomas Dow, a Scottish mason who helped shape the town’s earliest buildings, the property has recently been renovated for a young couple working in tech who relocated to the area from the city to raise a family.



[#image: /photos/681f8bce9fbf11ed3f5f7277]

“This project was about finding a balance between respecting the building’s history and making it practical for everyday life,” says interior designer [Keri MacLellan](https://www.west-grove.co/about), founder of [Westgrove](https://www.west-grove.co/), the studio that led the renovation. “We wanted to keep what made the house special while opening it up and bringing in more light and flow.”



[#image: /photos/681f8bf4d0e469ea6da51cc7]

MacLellan chose to celebrate the bones of the building rather than mask them. “We exposed all the original stonework throughout the existing structure,” she says. “There’s a richness to those textures that tells the story of the house better than any object could.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c193f60d2e393b1cda1]

One of the more complex challenges lay in the connection between the original home and a newer extension, added in the early 2000s. “The [original stone walls](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/mallorcan-house-by-rock-and-villa-is-a-lesson-in-refined-mediterranean-luxury) are nearly two feet thick,” says MacLellan. “Rather than try to disguise that transition, we decided to expose it.” The team cut a dramatic passageway through the stone, creating an 18-inch-deep threshold that now forms a striking portal between the old cottage and the contemporary living spaces beyond. “It’s like stepping through time,” she adds.



[#image: /photos/681f8c329fbf11ed3f5f7279]



[#image: /photos/681f8c44d0e469ea6da51cc9]

The interiors reflect the refined sensibility of its owners – both passionate [collectors of ceramics](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/gallery/see-how-stunning-ceramics-can-transform-a-space), design and art. “They wanted something calm and grounded but not too minimal,” explains the designer. [Natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/sustainable-house-mallorca-ernest-bordoy) anchor the palette. Take, for example, the timeless kitchen design: pale plaster walls, oiled white oak, woven cane, and slubby linens complement the texture of the exposed historic stone walls.



[#image: /photos/681f8c59d0e469ea6da51ccb]

To add colour and visual interest, the couple commissioned a series of artworks from local painter [Alex Moore-Gibson](https://www.alexmooregibsonart.com/). Her layered canvases enliven the otherwise neutral interiors while remaining in keeping with their spirit. “We wanted the art to feel like part of the home, not something layered on top,” MacLellan notes.



[#image: /photos/681f8c76d0e469ea6da51ccd]

Bespoke ceramic lighting – pendants, sconces and mobiles – created in collaboration with [Sara Wood](https://www.sarawoodbyhand.com/), a local designer known for her organic forms and earthy glazes, is also peppered throughout the cottage. “The client is an accomplished ceramicist herself, so this partnership was particularly meaningful,” says the designer. “The ceramics added softness and helped tie everything together.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c919fbf11ed3f5f727b]

Built-in cabinetry was designed to [maximise storage](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/10-clever-kitchen-storage-ideas-to-maximise-your-space) without overwhelming the rooms. Underpinning the timeless kitchen design, a cane-fronted island blends traditional references with a clean, contemporary shape. “The idea was to use [natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-modern-cote-dazur-home-lays-down-the-blueprint-for-chic-indoor-outdoor-living) and simple forms that would last,” MacLellan explains. “Nothing here is about trends.”



[#image: /photos/681f8cb83f60d2e393b1cda3]

The renovation was completed in six months and, despite the challenges of working within the constraints of a heritage home, the result feels perfectly coherent. Original features like architraves and stone walls remain untouched, but they now sit alongside new interventions that add light and ease of use. “The owners were really happy with how the original and new elements came together,” says MacLellan, ““There’s a sense that this house has history but it’s also ready for the future.”

Timeless by Design: The Rise of Ōpaal Interiors: Across the Middle East, design is undergoing a shift, from statement-making glamour to interiors shaped by authenticity, calm and emotional depth. Increasingly, clients are seeking spaces that mirror their identity, elevate their daily rituals and honour a more responsible way of living.



[#image: /photos/693286ca73ea71f36e4c1d6f]|||Residential project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

Leading this evolution is Ōpaal Interiors, an Emirati design studio that has steadily become one of the region’s most thoughtful design success voices. As it approaches its 10-year anniversary in 2026, Ōpaal’s influence can be felt in some of the region’s most refined homes and hospitality projects, where restraint take precedence.

“Not all design makes noise. Some simply stay with you,” says Ōpaal Interiors CEO & Founding partner Noor Al Muhaideb. This sentiment forms the backbone of the studio’s creative ethos: creating interiors that balance serenity with strength, warmth with precision, and beauty with meaning.



[#image: /photos/69244ee2335d173ca68f81cf]|||Ōpaal Interiors CEO & Founding partner Noor Al Muhaideb|||

What began as Al Muhaideb’s bold vision has evolved into a practice with a global footprint, spanning the Middle East, Europe and other international markets. Its portfolio ranges from expansive private residences to boutique hospitality escapes, as well as commercial and office environments. Projects include Dubai’s Mandarin Oriental Jumeira and the Kempinski Sky Garden Rafal in Saudi Arabia.

The studio’s growing influence has been recognised by the industry’s foremost institutions, with accolades including Interior Design Firm of the Year at the Design Middle East Awards and honours from *Arabian Business* for innovation and leadership.

Ōpaal’s philosophy is anchored by three pillars: longevity, sustainability and personalisation. Instead of chasing the momentum of trends, Ōpaal has committed to permanence. Its interiors blend classical structure with contemporary nuance, resulting in spaces that maintain their relevance over decades.



[#image: /photos/6932843a897ab921547a90bc]|||Residential project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

Sustainability, too, has long formed part of Ōpaal’s design DNA. Long before environmental responsibility became a regional mandate, the studio embraced locally sourced materials, conscious detailing, and earth-sensitive strategies. For Ōpaal, ecological responsibility does not constrain luxury, it refines it.

Perhaps the practice’s most defining quality is its bespoke rigour. Each commission begins with a conversation that uncovers a client’s aspirations, rhythms and relationship to space. The result is interiors that are deeply individual. No two projects are alike, yet all share a language of calm, considered refinement.

Behind every space is a multidisciplinary team of architects, artisans, materials specialists and sustainability experts, united under Al Muhaideb’s direction. A shared commitment to materiality and intention lends Ōpaal spaces a quiet power. These are interiors that feel grounded from the moment they are completed.



[#image: /photos/693286c158519187a495834f]|||Hospitality project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

As it plans to enter its second decade, Ōpaal Interiors continues to help chart a more nuanced definition of luxury in the Middle East. For Al Muhaideb, the future of design lies in authenticity, craftsmanship and consciousness, principles that will guide the studio as it expands its creative horizons.

7 *AD*\-approved sofa designs that will still feel fresh in 2026: In a design [landscape](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/10-architects-and-designers-in-surat-reimagining-the-built-landscape-with-purpose/){: target="_blank"} obsessed with what’s new, the most modern move is restraint. These timeless sofa designs endure because they prioritise proportion, texture, and tactility over trend—proof that true modernity never rushes.

## The Mid-Century Modern Couch: An Icon Reimagined


[#image: /photos/68cbb361915b28381cd498f4]|||*Pro-Tip: Add one curve nearby, a round coffee table or arc floor lamp balances the sofa’s linear geometry, as Studio Espaazo does in this Ahmedabad home. Keep the legs visible—exposed teak or metal legs create visual air and maintain that signature mid-century lightness.*|||

Born in post-war America, the [mid-century](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-multi-generational-villa-by-muselab-gives-the-motifs-of-kutch-a-mid-century-makeover/){: target="_blank"} modern sofa embodied a new kind of optimism: light, democratic, and human-scaled, when renowned designers such as Charles and Ray Eames, Florence Knoll, and George Nelson replaced heavy, carved furniture with clean horizontals, organic curves, and slender wooden legs. Eight decades on, its appeal endures because it balances form and function with quiet confidence: compact proportions which fit urban apartments, honest materials like teak, wool, or linen which age gracefully, and a geometry that harmonises with almost any style—from Scandinavian calm to Indian tropical modernism.

## The Sectional Sofa: For Stylish Ease


[#image: /photos/6863a2fe01e928c8f7030977]|||*Pro-Tip: As Concepto Studio does in this Bengaluru home, position the sectional away from walls; it looks sculptural and frames open-plan zones beautifully.*|||



[#image: /photos/6908518fe0d04a711165639b]|||*Pro-Tip: Rather than bulky end tables, opt for nested surfaces or built-in side ledges to complement the piece. Keep the side and centre tables low height, as seen in art collector Karan Bhagat’s Mumbai home—originally designed as a show flat by Rajiv Saini and reimagined by architect Siddharth Garimella.*|||

The sectional sofa has evolved from its suburban origins into a symbol of intelligent design—adaptable, sculptural, and unapologetically generous. Conceived in the mid-20th century to suit open layouts and shifting lifestyles, its genius lies in its modularity: each segment can transform a space, from an [intimate](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-designers-home-in-juhu-is-an-intimate-portrait-of-heritage-and-crafted-modernity-interior-designer-tania-shah/){: target="_blank"} reading corner to a sprawling social zone. Contemporary designers treat it as architecture rather than furniture—anchoring living areas, defining circulation, and softening modern geometries. With low profiles, tonal upholstery, and deep, sink-in seats, the sectional embodies relaxed luxury that doesn’t age. It’s as much a place to gather as it is to pause.

*Also read: [5 best sofa colours to breathe life into a neutral home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-best-sofa-colours-to-breathe-life-into-a-neutral-home/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Modular Marvel: Where Form Meets Flexibility


[#image: /photos/683f428af160cc375552caa9]|||*Pro tip: With modular sofas, try to keep the palette monochrome to maintain visual calm. Let its sculptural form, not colour, create rhythm across the room.*|||

The modular sofa can be described as even a philosophy ofmodern living, beyond its life as furniture, and shares roots with the sectional sofa. Born in the late 1900s and popularised by icons like Mario Bellini’s *Camaleonda*, it challenged the static idea of a traditional couch by letting users compose their own configurations. Today, its appeal endures because it reflects how we actually inhabit our homes fluidly. Each unit can shift from intimate nook to social centrepiece with ease, embodying a kind of democratic design that feels both playful and poised. With soft edges, comfortable upholstery, and sculptural volume, the modular sofa is a versatile and [artistic](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/anish-kapoors-monte-santangelo-turns-everyday-commute-into-an-artistic-experience/){: target="_blank"} shape-shifter.

## The Bouclé Sofa: Elevating Texture


[#image: /photos/680f60f3d7037b1705713a37]|||*Pro-tip: Maintain the tones of the bouclé, as the texture works best in neutral, ivory or oat shades, as seen in this home by AT Studio. Pair it with tonal contrasts —dark wood, marble, or brushed metal—to ground its softness and keep the room from feeling too ethereal.*|||

The instantly recognizable bouclé sofa designs have achieved cult status for a reason—they turn simplicity into seduction. Once favoured by mid-century modernists for its soft tactility, *bouclé* (French for “looped yarn”) resurfaced in contemporary interiors as the textile of quiet luxury. Its tactile, cloud-like texture diffuses light, softens architectural lines, and invites touch—an antidote to the hard-edged [minimalism](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/in-this-5100-square-foot-bengaluru-penthouse-minimalism-gets-a-mediterranean-twist-pomtini-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} of recent decades. Designers now favour it not for novelty, but for its ability to balance sculptural form with comfort, making even the most minimal space feel warm and inhabited. Its versatility allows it to effortlessly find its place in all designs, from a pared-back Japandi apartment to a richly layered art deco interior. Whether curved, modular, or perched on slender legs, the bouclé sofa embodies modern, fluid comfort.

## The Reimagined Chesterfield: Tradition With A Twist


[#image: /photos/686e2c0bebca66233ec8ed90]|||*Pro-tip: A matte or suede finish modernises the traditional high-gloss look instantly, as seen in the library lounge of Goa’s Verve Estate, by Paul Rodrigues.*|||



[#image: /photos/65d44e14f3a9d1555ccb3647]|||*Pro-tip: Let the Chesterfield breathe—keep it off rugs with heavy patterns, and pair it with visually minimal pieces (such as the marble centre piece in this Chennai home by Shreya Krishnan Design Office) to offset its stateliness.*|||

The Chesterfield sofa has travelled centuries—from British drawing rooms to modern lofts—without losing its quiet command. Born in 18th-century England and once synonymous with men’s clubs and cigar smoke, it has since been redefined by designers who replaced excess with elegance. Today’s Chesterfields shed their ornate scrolls for slimmer arms, matte leather, or even linen and velvet, achieving heritage without heaviness. What makes it timeless is its architecture: deep tufting that adds texture, symmetry that calms the eye, and a sense of permanence rare in fast-turn design. It anchors contemporary spaces with [history](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/bombay-deco-is-the-ultimate-chronicle-of-mumbais-art-deco-history/){: target="_blank"}, bringing gravitas to the lightest rooms and restraint to the grandest ones.

*Also read: [Poliform's Owen collection blends couture finesse with modern Italian craftsmanship](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/poliforms-owen-collection-blends-couture-finesse-with-modern-italian-craftsmanship/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Built-In Sofa: Seating As Architecture


[#image: /photos/68c28ba871e6a8c79d088741]|||*Pro-tip: Layer fixed seating with loose cushions and textiles in varied tones; the contrast of softness against masonry keeps it human, not austere. You can also experiment with other couch style pairings to add variety, as seen in this Mumbai home by RAD co \+ lab.*|||



[#image: /photos/67a9c0a9a0d263d60280eda7]|||*Pro-tip: You can get creative with your seating. In this Mumbai home by STaND Design, the deck flooring of the balcony extends as seating into the living room, creating an enticing play of levels.*|||

The built-in sofa blurs the line between furniture and structure—part of the room, not merely placed in it. Emerging from Mediterranean and [Japanese](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-2000-square-foot-home-in-bandra-blends-indian-and-japanese-design-sensibilities-pill-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} traditions where space was both precious and poetic, it has become a hallmark of architectural minimalism today. Cast in lime plaster, stone, or tadelakt, it offers permanence in an age of the temporary. Its beauty lies in proportion and restraint: seamless planes that invite lounging without visual noise. Built-ins work best when continuity is key—extending from plinth to wall to window in a single gesture. They turn corners into contemplative nooks, steps into seating, and architecture into atmosphere.

## The Low-Slung Lounge: Grounding Grace


[#image: /photos/67cfe1a75f709ec2a12b9048]|||*Pro-tip: Orient the low-slung sofa towards windows or courtyards—the low height extends the landscape. The surrounding furniture should be low and the lighting diffused as well, to allow the sofa to fully express its horizontal emphasis, as seen in this Bengaluru home by Ashni Kumar.*|||

The low-slung sofa captures the easy elegance of spaces that breathe—homes where air, light, and pause carry as much weight as design. Drawing from Japanese tatami culture and India’s own tradition of floor seating, it sits closer to the ground, promoting a slower rhythm and visual calm. Its elongated proportions and deep cushions create an atmosphere of ease, while wide arms and open bases invite lounging that feels instinctive rather than staged. Designers love it for how it grounds a space—anchoring large [living rooms](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/9-living-room-decor-ideas-thatll-make-this-space-the-highlight-of-your-home/){: target="_blank"} without interrupting sightlines or scale.

In conclusion, across all silhouettes, the most timeless sofa isn’t defined by style but by spatial awareness and sensitivity—to proportion, texture, and light. Keep upholstery tactile, colour palettes grounded, and silhouettes human in scale. Let materials breathe, allow light to move, and balance every softness with structure. Above all, invest in craftsmanship and comfort; trends fade, but good design—like a well-made sofa—only gets better with time.

Moody Masculine Interiors Set the Scene at This Egyptian Architect’s Contemporary Cairo Penthouse: **[Dramatic, yet effortlessly liveable](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/comedian-mohanad-alhattab-serene-villa-in-dubai-brings-a-moody-masculine-style-to-home-decor){: target="_blank"}, this contemporary Cairo penthouse is a masterful composition of opposites: of light and dark, of moody, masculine interiors and earthy accents.**

For architects, designing their own homes represents the purest distillation of their design philosophy. It’s an opportunity and a challenge: a chance to experiment boldly without client feedback or market considerations, while creating a personal sanctuary. For Fathy Ibrahim, founder and lead architect of Egypt architecture studio Insignia Design Group, that challenge called for deliberate theatricality.



[#image: /photos/681586f1aa67175ec681b514]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6815870e883cad4e07ddc045]

[#image: /photos/68158712883cad4e07ddc047]

+++

It’s a quality you can feel immediately upon entering his contemporary Cairo penthouse in the Sodic Villette compound where a dramatic entrance – clad entirely in textured black tiles and a mirrored ceiling – create an infinity-like effect. It’s a bold statement that sets the tone for what lies beyond.



[#image: /photos/6815871b883cad4e07ddc049]

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/681586fbaa67175ec681b516]

+++

“When I first stepped into the empty apartment, I saw endless possibilities,” recalls Ibrahim. “The lack of finishing meant I could design freely, without having to be controlled by existing layouts or styles.” This blank canvas offered Ibrahim, an architect accustomed to bringing others’ visions to life, the rare opportunity to explore his own design vocabulary without compromise.

Unlike typical Egyptian residences that often favour bright, open entrances, Ibrahim’s choice of a moody, theatrical threshold creates a compelling transition. “The entrance serves as a powerful statement,” he explains. “The dark tiles on the walls create a sophisticated backdrop that contrasts beautifully with the warm tones of the interiors.”

This interplay between shadow and light continues throughout the 240-square-meter residence. Moving from the dramatic entrance, the space opens up into an airy reception area where Spanish marble, rich walnut accents, and a carefully calibrated palette of earth tones create a warm, sophisticated atmosphere. The living area seamlessly connects to an open kitchen, establishing the heart of this Cairo penthouse as a space designed for connection and hospitality.



[#image: /photos/681586f0883cad4e07ddc041]

7 tendances de revêtement pour la cuisine en 2025: La cuisine reste le cœur de la maison en 2025, reflétant, notamment par son revêtement, des tendances axées sur l’authenticité et le bien-être. L’aménagement évolue avec des designs uniques, intégrant des finitions audacieuses et un mobilier remarquable. Cette transformation fait de la cuisine un espace où cuisiner, partager, socialiser, et prend ainsi une dimension toute particulière. **Porcelanosa** propose une multitude d'inspirations modernes pour sublimer votre intérieur, en mêlant esthétisme et fonctionnalité. Ces sept tendances de revêtement vous aideront à repenser votre cuisine avec des éléments décoratifs et pratiques, en phase avec les aspirations actuelles, pour un espace chaleureux et personnalisé.

+++button-group

[En savoir plus](https://www.porcelanosa.com/fr/ "En savoir plus"){: target="_blank"}

+++

## 1\. Le revêtement en all-over


[#image: /photos/67b5f4d6d82ae62fac99dfdc]|||Le revêtement *Taranto Marfil* de Porcelanosa, effet marbre.|||

En 2025, le revêtement se fait pluriel : sur les murs, les sols, il pare une cuisine dans son entièreté, donnant une impression d'unité et d'harmonie agréables à l'œil. Grâce aux technologies contemporaines, les revêtements combinent les fonctions. Plus résistants, plus versatiles, sans transiger sur le design, ils deviennent, comme le béton ciré ou la peinture, envisageables (et recommandés \!) en all-over dans la cuisine. *« Cette esthétique sans faille est idéale pour les cuisines qui nécessitent beaucoup d’entretien et de nettoyage,* explique Porcelanosa. *Moins il y a de joints, plus le résultat est hygiénique ».*

## 2\. Le papier-peint décoratif
Dans la même démarche de résistance aux exigences techniques de la cuisine, le papier-peint s'y intègre également. L'enjeu, apporter une touche décorative, sophistiquée ou minimaliste, pour insuffler davantage d'originalité dans sa cuisine. Une cuisine désormais personnalisable aux goûts de chacun, qui s'autorise la fantaisie des papiers-peints les plus divers, sortant du classique duo peinture-carrelage. *« Les tendances en matière de design pour les cuisines 2025 incluent des murs décorés de motifs luxuriants, de surfaces texturées, de combinaisons de couleurs organiques et de motifs géométriques »*, indique la marque.

## 3\. L'affirmation du dosseret de cuisine


[#image: /photos/67b5f4ebd82ae62fac99dfde]|||Ici, le dosseret de cuisine est marqué par la pose d'un revêtement *XTONE Navona* effet travertin.|||

Espace dévolu à la cuisson et à l'évier, le dosseret de cuisine devient la pièce maîtresse de la pièce. Pour cela, on le distingue visuellement du reste de la cuisine grâce aux matériaux : des placards en béton ciré ou en laque font ainsi ressortir le marbre, le travertin ou le carrelage d'un dosseret affirmé. La structuration de l'espace peut également s'effectuer par la couleur. *« Cette esthétique est plus frappante avec des étagères flottantes, évitant les casiers en hauteur, pour faire du tableau de bord un élément marquant du mur »,* conseillent les experts de Porcelanosa.

## 4\. Les textures naturelles


[#image: /photos/67b5f3a63b0f3607e830906c]|||Le revêtement *Xlight AGED CLAY* s'exprime en 120x270 au mur et 120x120 au sol.|||

Cette année, les matières organiques continuent leur percée dans les intérieurs, notamment dans les cuisines qui intègrent l'argile, le grès cérame ou le marbre pour davantage de naturalité. Des matériaux permettant, en outre, d'insuffler une délicate tactilité dans les cuisine, à rebours des surfaces lisses et hygiénistes peu sensorielles. *« De plus, l’utilisation de ces surfaces qui recréent des pierres naturelles telles que le travertin comme revêtement de sol les rend plus faciles à entretenir*, souligne Porcelanosa. *Les tendances de la cuisine pour 2025 incluront également des carreaux décoratifs avec des reliefs subtils qui ajouteront de la dimension et de l’intérêt visuel. »*

## 5\. Les finitions métalliques


[#image: /photos/67b5f504d82ae62fac99dfe0]|||Le revêtement *Xtone* terre greige de Porcelanosa se marie admirablement avec les touches métallisées.|||

Ultra contemporain, le métal s'intègre dans la cuisine. Une finition argentée pour un rendu high-tech, une touche de laiton pour un effet raffiné… ces détails peuvent prendre la forme d'un encadrement de mobilier, de poignées, de luminaires ou encore de notes métallisées purement décoratives. L'objectif ? Dépoussiérer la cuisine traditionnelle pour lui insuffler un vent de modernité. *« Actuellement, les métaux chauds tels que le cuivre, le laiton et le titane sont les plus courants,* précise la marque. *En général, deux ou trois métaux sont combinés dans le même espace, avec un matériau principal et les autres complémentaires ».*

## 6\. Les céramiques artisanales


[#image: /photos/67b5f4c6d82ae62fac99dfda]|||La mosaïque *Kronos Jade* de Porcelanosa, au vert raffiné, s'allie au carrelage *CUZCO* au sol.|||

À l'inverse, là encore, des cuisines en carrelage classique, Porcelanosa propose des revêtements en céramique à la main, à la fois élégants et singuliers. Ils apportent une note artisanale bienvenue, une ambiance légèrement plus rustique, qui confère à la pièce davantage de richesse matérielle. *« La cuisine comprendra des pièces telles que du zellige, des mosaïques et même des carreaux peints à la main »*, prévoit la marque.

## 7\. Couleurs : les tons organiques et nordiques


[#image: /photos/67b5f4f5ed2990ec470e5da4]|||Le revêtement *Xtone Amazonite* mêle les tons vert et gris.|||

En 2025, deux familles de couleurs sont privilégiées :

- **Les tonalités nordiques issues de la nature**, comme les verts forêt ou sauge, ici traduits dans un revêtement de cuisine en marbrures minérales, mêlant un vert intense à un gris très naturel. *« Le bois, surtout dans les tons moyens et foncés, sera de plus en plus visible. »*
- **Les nuances organiques chaleureuses**, comme le carrelage rose-brique chaud présenté ci-dessous. Un apport de lumière et de convivialité idéal dans la pièce du partage par excellence. *« L’augmentation de la combinaison des tons bruns et verts est due au fait qu’ils sont plus élégants et naturels dans la lumière de l’hémisphère nord »*, conclut Porcelanosa.



[#image: /photos/67b5f4e1ed2990ec470e5da2]|||Le carrelage de la collection *KENT* au ton enveloppant apporte de la chaleur dans la cuisine, en mélangeant deux types de carreaux de la collection : _Kent Warm Gloss_ et *Kent Deco Warm Gloss*.|||

+++button-group

[En savoir plus](https://www.porcelanosa.com/fr/ "En savoir plus"){: target="_blank"}

+++

Trendfarben 2026: Diese Farbtöne sind im kommenden Jahr angesagt – laut AD100-Designer:innen: **Trendfarben 2026: AD100-Designer:innen verraten ihre Favoriten für das kommende Jahr.**

Es gibt bekanntlich zwei Arten, wie mit der [Wandgestaltung](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/waende-streichen-tipps){: target="_blank"} umgegangen wird. Entweder, man hält es einfach und schlicht, und entscheidet sich für einen weißen Wandanstrich (und das meist in der gesamten Wohnung) oder aber, man ist wagemutig und taucht jeden Raum in eine andere Nuance. Nun lässt sich weder das eine, noch das andere, als „richtig“ kategorisieren. Nun mag Weiß mit großer Wahrscheinlichkeit der [Farbton](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/grundlagen-farbenlehre-fuer-inneneinrichtung){: target="_blank"} sein, der die meisten Wände deutscher Wohnräume bedeckt, doch lässt sich seit einigen Jahren ein mutigerer Umgang mit Wandfarben erkennen. Und das ist toll, schließlich kann die Wahl der Wandfarbe die Stimmung im Raum erheblich beeinflussen. Der Schweizer Architekt Le Corbusier ging sogar so weit und behauptete, dass Farben in der Architektur ein ebenso wichtiges Mittel seien wie der Grundriss oder der Schnitt. Dabei hört die Farbgestaltung noch lange nicht beim Kauf des Farbeimers auf. Genauso wichtig wie der Ton sind Muster, Maserungen und Oberflächen, die das Farbkonzept am Ende dynamisch machen. Doch auf welche Farben, Muster und Kombinationen sollten wir 2026 setzen? Das weiß wohl niemand besser als unsere [AD100-Interiordesigner:innen](https://www.ad-magazin.de/ad100){: target="_blank"} – lassen Sie sich inspirieren\!

+++inset-left

[#externallink: /externallinks/674da354c166cd4b99f8e392]

+++

## Diese Trendfarben und Muster bestimmen laut AD100-Designer:innen das Jahr 2026
Wir haben vier unserer frisch gekürten AD100-Designer:innen gefragt, welche Farbtöne auf dem Trendradar 2026 ganz oben liegen. Ihre Lieblingsnuancen verraten wir hier.

### Warme Juweltöne
In den vergangenen Jahren hat sich eine äußerst elegante Form des [Maximalismus](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/stil-trend-maximalismus){: target="_blank"} in unseren Interiors breit gemacht und Räume hervorgebracht, die geprägt sind von Farben, Extravaganz, Kühnheit und Humor. Gleichzeitig ließ sich eine Zuwendung zu warmen Erdtönen beobachten: Warme Brauntöne zeichnen die Saison ebenso wie die breite Palette an Grün und Rot. Für die AD100-Designerin Stephanie Thatenhorst tritt dieser Hang zur Sinnlichkeit 2026 noch stärker hervor. „Für mich bewegt sich 2026 ganz klar in Richtung tiefer, warmer Farbtöne: Burgund, intensives Rot bis hin zu leicht orangen Nuancen, kombiniert mit dunklen Lila- und Pflaumentönen – Farben mit einer spürbaren Wärme und Tiefe. Sie bringen eine Erdung in Räume, wirken aber gleichzeitig kraftvoll und emotional.“ Dazu empfiehlt Thatenhorst die Kombination mit natürlichen Materialien. „Das verleiht diesen Tönen eine besondere Präsenz.“



[#image: /photos/6931979b655cd3ef2f9bf6b5]|||Laut der AD100-Designerin Stephanie Thatenhorst sind warme Erdtöne 2026 auf dem Vormarsch. Im Zusammenspiel mit natürlichen Materialien entfalten sie ihr volles Potenzial.|||

### Goldenes Camouflage
Die Gestaltung mit Farbe ist das eine, doch lässt der Umgang durchaus Spielraum zu. Für den AD100-Designer [Fabian Freytag](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/altbau-berlin-charlottenburg-fabian-freytag){: target="_blank"} steht eines fest: „Ich bin überzeugt, dass wir künftig vermehrt Muster sehen werden, die sich an Camouflage anlehnen – allerdings nicht im Sinne klassischer olivgrüner Tarnfarben, sondern als monochrome Mélange aus drei aufeinander abgestimmten Tönen.“ Es muss also nicht immer nur klassisch einfarbig sein. „Gerade in Kombination mit glänzenden Oberflächen entsteht eine Atmosphäre, die Geborgenheit ausstrahlt. Vielleicht würde ich diesen Trend ‚Golden Camouflage‘ nennen: eine harmonisch orchestrierte Farbwelt – ein wenig spießig, aber durchaus stolz.“



[#image: /photos/6931974c655cd3ef2f9bf6b3]|||Laut Fabian Freytag feiert das Camouflage-Muster 2026 ein Comeback – und zwar ein schillerndes, glänzendes.|||

### Mineralische Blaugrün-Nuancen
Die AD100-Interiordesignerin [Sophie Ashby](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/sophie-ashby-wohnhaus-london){: target="_blank"} prognostiziert für 2026 eine Palette aus mineralischen Blau- und Grünnuancen: „In 2026 zieht es mich zu einer blau-grünen Farbfamilie aus satten Kobalt- und Kornblumenblau, die gleichermaßen präsent und beruhigend wirken. Sie besitzen eine juwelenartige Tiefe, lassen Räume lebendig erscheinen, und vermitteln das Gefühl von Klarheit und Heiterkeit – wie ein Abbild von Himmel und Wasser.“ Besonders raffiniert ist für die Interiordesignerin die Kombination mit tiefen Schokoladentönen. „[Brauntöne](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/trendfarben-beige-braun-wohnung-einrichten){: target="_blank"} schaffen in diesem Zusammenspiel mit diesen lebendigen, energiegeladenen Akzenten Wärme und Geborgenheit und runden das Farbkonzept harmonisch ab.“ Dazu kommen dunkle, fruchtige Rottöne, die für eine gewisse Prise Drama sorgen. „Das erzeugt eine stimmungsvolle Fülle – ideal für vielschichtige, atmosphärische Innenräume“, weiß Ashby. Den finalen Abschluss des eleganten Farbkonzeptes bildet ein zartes Lila, „das eine sanfte, helle und luftige Note einbringt und in Kontrast zu den satten, erdigen Nuancen nahezu verträumt wirkt.“



[#image: /photos/693198af655cd3ef2f9bf6b7]|||Die AD100-Interiordesignerin Sophie Ashby setzt auf mineralische Blau- und Grünnuancen, die im Zusammenspiel mit roten Akzenten eine wunderbar lebhafte Atmosphäre schaffen – so wie hier in diesem Londoner Schlafzimmer.|||

### Dynamische Farbwelten
„Wir haben das Gefühl, dass weniger eine Farbe an sich die Trends 2026 bestimmen wird, sondern vielmehr das Mixen von Farbstimmungen“, beobachtet das [AD100-Designerinnenduo Antonius Schimmelbusch](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/lokale-talente-ad100){: target="_blank"}. „Zumindest wünschen wir uns das.“ Zu einem übergreifenden Farbton gesellen sich also immer wieder kleine, unterschiedliche Akzente. „Bei einem aktuellen Projekt, ein Produzenten-Loft in Prenzlauer Berg, sieht man gut, was wir meinen: Wir haben einen sehr dunklen Farbkanon ausgewählt und immer wieder fast schon grelle Akzente eingebaut, die den *vibey* Farbkanon unterbrechen und damit noch verstärken. In der Küche das Arsenic Green, vor der Plattenwand leuchten blaue Samtsessel oder im Text-Zimmer eine fast neongelbe Mohairdecke.“ Weniger ist nicht automatisch mehr – so das Fazit.



[#image: /photos/69319707655cd3ef2f9bf6af]|||In diesem Produzenten-Loft in Prenzlauer Berg, gestaltet von Antonius Schimmelbusch, treffen leuchtend blaue Samtsessel auf eine terracottarote Plattenwand.|||

How A Small Home Office Can Still Be Charming, Even in a Tiny Apartment: A small home office could be the smartest [home makeover project](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/top-diy-trends-that-will-define-home-renovations-in-2025){: target="_blank"} you take on in 2025. Not only will it add to your home's potential resale value, it will make life so much better in the meanwhile with your work life not taking over your home life.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab570a6f2dfa698d480]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab66a663b2da95cc822]

+++

This [small apartment](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/small-apartment-ideas-this-tiny-studio-is-big-on-style){: target="_blank"} was completely redesigned by the interior design agency NOWW Studio. "The owners are young and have professions in the creative industry," confides the architects. So they appreciate ideas that defy convention, and they value their private lives as much as their professional lives: this apartment had to be like them."

### A tiny living room becomes a small home office
Here, a real bedroom has taken the place of the old kitchen, and only the small living room, which serves as an office, remains unchanged. It has, of course, been completely redesigned. Colour is everywhere in the apartment, and that's what makes this interior so interesting, both visually and graphically.

### A tip to remember
***Combine contemporary woodwork, retro inspiration and designer pieces. The large storage unit in burr wood veneer with its dark border replicates a vintage swivel desk chair produced by MIM, Mobili Italiani Moderni, in the 1960s.***



[#externallink: /externallinks/6503041abb917946d989f4c1]

***
## A dialogue of styles and eras


[#image: /photos/67b77ab670a6f2dfa698d481]

In Paris, this pied-à-terre is the work of Le Cann's interior designers. The interior is inspired by the owner's personality, his story - "a very elegant young man, very classical, Parisian but whose adopted country was Italy" – thus favouring cross-influences between Paris and Milan. After organising the layout around the sources of natural light provided by the bay windows and skydome, Le Cann sculpts the volumes with a rhythmic play of moldings and symmetries.

### Tips to remember
***Combine eras, materials, geometries and styles to create a unique charm. Here, on the brushed stainless steel desk, a Les Yeux Noirs sculpture in bone and black pearls, a Chandigarh hand in bronze, and Olivier Thomé's Absence, in gouache on cardboard. A wrought-iron chair completes the ensemble.***

***
## An abundance of storage space
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab770a6f2dfa698d482]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab770a6f2dfa698d483]

+++

The challenge of layout and optimisation was the compass for interior designer Alice Lacherez's project. "We're in Neuilly, so we had to be consistent with the local environment," she explains. We brought in some southern French codes, while preserving the building's 1970s style," she explains. Various species of wood are used in the furniture and decor, right down to the bamboo sconces, a nod to the Mediterranean.

### Tips to remember
***Incorporate clever, designer storage in the office area. Specially carved niches replace unused service doors. The multiplicity of storage units in no way detracts from the apartment's design. When done right you can make a [small space appear bigger](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/how-to-make-small-rooms-look-bigger){: target="_blank"}.***

***
## Separating spaces with a module
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab74e1507eef299189b]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab86a663b2da95cc823]

+++

To appreciate this small apartment's transformation, you have to understand what it was before. “You have to imagine yourself, in 24sqm, with old wallpaper, carpet on the floor, floral curtains...” From a dark, partitioned cube with a dubious mezzanine, the place has become a pleasant single apartment under the aegis of Aurélien Duny. For the architect, functionality means opening up the rooms. "All we kept of the existing space were the exposed ceiling beams. We knocked down the walls in favor of semi-high partitions that let light in everywhere."

### Tips to remember
***Separate spaces with a module that seems to float overhead, like a cloud with its rounded corners. We love its Mediterranean influences, reminiscent of the organic lines of a Menorcan finca.***

***
## A small office thanks to 100% made-to-measure furniture


[#image: /photos/67b77ab870a6f2dfa698d484]

When interior designers Elina and Diana Mussakulova were asked to design this small 55-square-meter apartment, they decided to make the most of its precious square meters. For this reason, many of the furnishings were custom-made, as in the bedroom, where the small desk fits in perfectly.

### A tip to remember
***Furniture designed entirely to measure – as in the case of the wardrobe with desk area and the bed.***

***
## An *"inhabited wall"* in plywood
+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/66cd641b6ec82447a1a7695f]

[#image: /photos/66cd641c0c92e7f11fddc0c0]

[#image: /photos/66cd641b0c92e7f11fddc0bf]

+++

"I found it very compartmentalised, with a small kitchen and storage units everywhere that made it hard to understand its high ceilings... in short, getting around this apartment was a journey full of pitfalls", says interior architect and designer Olivier Debin, referring to this bright 47 square-meter apartment. His intervention was therefore twofold: optimising the space and writing a unique story about the place.

### A tip to remember
***The large cabinet or "living wall". Plywood guarantees structural homogeneity and allows the interior designer to play with the effects of different levels, openings, solids, voids and heights between a work surface and a desk. The all-over birch veneer gives the impression of a cabin as much as a laboratory.***



[#externallink: /externallinks/65030450bb917946d989f4c2]

7 *AD*\-approved sofa designs that will still feel fresh in 2026: In a design [landscape](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/10-architects-and-designers-in-surat-reimagining-the-built-landscape-with-purpose/){: target="_blank"} obsessed with what’s new, the most modern move is restraint. These timeless sofa designs endure because they prioritise proportion, texture, and tactility over trend—proof that true modernity never rushes.

## The Mid-Century Modern Couch: An Icon Reimagined


[#image: /photos/68cbb361915b28381cd498f4]|||*Pro-Tip: Add one curve nearby, a round coffee table or arc floor lamp balances the sofa’s linear geometry, as Studio Espaazo does in this Ahmedabad home. Keep the legs visible—exposed teak or metal legs create visual air and maintain that signature mid-century lightness.*|||

Born in post-war America, the [mid-century](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-multi-generational-villa-by-muselab-gives-the-motifs-of-kutch-a-mid-century-makeover/){: target="_blank"} modern sofa embodied a new kind of optimism: light, democratic, and human-scaled, when renowned designers such as Charles and Ray Eames, Florence Knoll, and George Nelson replaced heavy, carved furniture with clean horizontals, organic curves, and slender wooden legs. Eight decades on, its appeal endures because it balances form and function with quiet confidence: compact proportions which fit urban apartments, honest materials like teak, wool, or linen which age gracefully, and a geometry that harmonises with almost any style—from Scandinavian calm to Indian tropical modernism.

## The Sectional Sofa: For Stylish Ease


[#image: /photos/6863a2fe01e928c8f7030977]|||*Pro-Tip: As Concepto Studio does in this Bengaluru home, position the sectional away from walls; it looks sculptural and frames open-plan zones beautifully.*|||



[#image: /photos/6908518fe0d04a711165639b]|||*Pro-Tip: Rather than bulky end tables, opt for nested surfaces or built-in side ledges to complement the piece. Keep the side and centre tables low height, as seen in art collector Karan Bhagat’s Mumbai home—originally designed as a show flat by Rajiv Saini and reimagined by architect Siddharth Garimella.*|||

The sectional sofa has evolved from its suburban origins into a symbol of intelligent design—adaptable, sculptural, and unapologetically generous. Conceived in the mid-20th century to suit open layouts and shifting lifestyles, its genius lies in its modularity: each segment can transform a space, from an [intimate](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-designers-home-in-juhu-is-an-intimate-portrait-of-heritage-and-crafted-modernity-interior-designer-tania-shah/){: target="_blank"} reading corner to a sprawling social zone. Contemporary designers treat it as architecture rather than furniture—anchoring living areas, defining circulation, and softening modern geometries. With low profiles, tonal upholstery, and deep, sink-in seats, the sectional embodies relaxed luxury that doesn’t age. It’s as much a place to gather as it is to pause.

*Also read: [5 best sofa colours to breathe life into a neutral home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-best-sofa-colours-to-breathe-life-into-a-neutral-home/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Modular Marvel: Where Form Meets Flexibility


[#image: /photos/683f428af160cc375552caa9]|||*Pro tip: With modular sofas, try to keep the palette monochrome to maintain visual calm. Let its sculptural form, not colour, create rhythm across the room.*|||

The modular sofa can be described as even a philosophy ofmodern living, beyond its life as furniture, and shares roots with the sectional sofa. Born in the late 1900s and popularised by icons like Mario Bellini’s *Camaleonda*, it challenged the static idea of a traditional couch by letting users compose their own configurations. Today, its appeal endures because it reflects how we actually inhabit our homes fluidly. Each unit can shift from intimate nook to social centrepiece with ease, embodying a kind of democratic design that feels both playful and poised. With soft edges, comfortable upholstery, and sculptural volume, the modular sofa is a versatile and [artistic](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/anish-kapoors-monte-santangelo-turns-everyday-commute-into-an-artistic-experience/){: target="_blank"} shape-shifter.

## The Bouclé Sofa: Elevating Texture


[#image: /photos/680f60f3d7037b1705713a37]|||*Pro-tip: Maintain the tones of the bouclé, as the texture works best in neutral, ivory or oat shades, as seen in this home by AT Studio. Pair it with tonal contrasts —dark wood, marble, or brushed metal—to ground its softness and keep the room from feeling too ethereal.*|||

The instantly recognizable bouclé sofa designs have achieved cult status for a reason—they turn simplicity into seduction. Once favoured by mid-century modernists for its soft tactility, *bouclé* (French for “looped yarn”) resurfaced in contemporary interiors as the textile of quiet luxury. Its tactile, cloud-like texture diffuses light, softens architectural lines, and invites touch—an antidote to the hard-edged [minimalism](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/in-this-5100-square-foot-bengaluru-penthouse-minimalism-gets-a-mediterranean-twist-pomtini-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} of recent decades. Designers now favour it not for novelty, but for its ability to balance sculptural form with comfort, making even the most minimal space feel warm and inhabited. Its versatility allows it to effortlessly find its place in all designs, from a pared-back Japandi apartment to a richly layered art deco interior. Whether curved, modular, or perched on slender legs, the bouclé sofa embodies modern, fluid comfort.

## The Reimagined Chesterfield: Tradition With A Twist


[#image: /photos/686e2c0bebca66233ec8ed90]|||*Pro-tip: A matte or suede finish modernises the traditional high-gloss look instantly, as seen in the library lounge of Goa’s Verve Estate, by Paul Rodrigues.*|||



[#image: /photos/65d44e14f3a9d1555ccb3647]|||*Pro-tip: Let the Chesterfield breathe—keep it off rugs with heavy patterns, and pair it with visually minimal pieces (such as the marble centre piece in this Chennai home by Shreya Krishnan Design Office) to offset its stateliness.*|||

The Chesterfield sofa has travelled centuries—from British drawing rooms to modern lofts—without losing its quiet command. Born in 18th-century England and once synonymous with men’s clubs and cigar smoke, it has since been redefined by designers who replaced excess with elegance. Today’s Chesterfields shed their ornate scrolls for slimmer arms, matte leather, or even linen and velvet, achieving heritage without heaviness. What makes it timeless is its architecture: deep tufting that adds texture, symmetry that calms the eye, and a sense of permanence rare in fast-turn design. It anchors contemporary spaces with [history](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/bombay-deco-is-the-ultimate-chronicle-of-mumbais-art-deco-history/){: target="_blank"}, bringing gravitas to the lightest rooms and restraint to the grandest ones.

*Also read: [Poliform's Owen collection blends couture finesse with modern Italian craftsmanship](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/poliforms-owen-collection-blends-couture-finesse-with-modern-italian-craftsmanship/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Built-In Sofa: Seating As Architecture


[#image: /photos/68c28ba871e6a8c79d088741]|||*Pro-tip: Layer fixed seating with loose cushions and textiles in varied tones; the contrast of softness against masonry keeps it human, not austere. You can also experiment with other couch style pairings to add variety, as seen in this Mumbai home by RAD co \+ lab.*|||



[#image: /photos/67a9c0a9a0d263d60280eda7]|||*Pro-tip: You can get creative with your seating. In this Mumbai home by STaND Design, the deck flooring of the balcony extends as seating into the living room, creating an enticing play of levels.*|||

The built-in sofa blurs the line between furniture and structure—part of the room, not merely placed in it. Emerging from Mediterranean and [Japanese](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-2000-square-foot-home-in-bandra-blends-indian-and-japanese-design-sensibilities-pill-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} traditions where space was both precious and poetic, it has become a hallmark of architectural minimalism today. Cast in lime plaster, stone, or tadelakt, it offers permanence in an age of the temporary. Its beauty lies in proportion and restraint: seamless planes that invite lounging without visual noise. Built-ins work best when continuity is key—extending from plinth to wall to window in a single gesture. They turn corners into contemplative nooks, steps into seating, and architecture into atmosphere.

## The Low-Slung Lounge: Grounding Grace


[#image: /photos/67cfe1a75f709ec2a12b9048]|||*Pro-tip: Orient the low-slung sofa towards windows or courtyards—the low height extends the landscape. The surrounding furniture should be low and the lighting diffused as well, to allow the sofa to fully express its horizontal emphasis, as seen in this Bengaluru home by Ashni Kumar.*|||

The low-slung sofa captures the easy elegance of spaces that breathe—homes where air, light, and pause carry as much weight as design. Drawing from Japanese tatami culture and India’s own tradition of floor seating, it sits closer to the ground, promoting a slower rhythm and visual calm. Its elongated proportions and deep cushions create an atmosphere of ease, while wide arms and open bases invite lounging that feels instinctive rather than staged. Designers love it for how it grounds a space—anchoring large [living rooms](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/9-living-room-decor-ideas-thatll-make-this-space-the-highlight-of-your-home/){: target="_blank"} without interrupting sightlines or scale.

In conclusion, across all silhouettes, the most timeless sofa isn’t defined by style but by spatial awareness and sensitivity—to proportion, texture, and light. Keep upholstery tactile, colour palettes grounded, and silhouettes human in scale. Let materials breathe, allow light to move, and balance every softness with structure. Above all, invest in craftsmanship and comfort; trends fade, but good design—like a well-made sofa—only gets better with time.

[#cneembed: script/video/65ef26588f191637a1f134a5.js]

Meet the architects and designers who design the homes of Indian celebrities: Beyond the red carpets and flashing lights, Indian celebrities retreat to sanctuaries designed by the country's (and beyond) most talented interior designers and architects. In this exclusive peek behind the velvet ropes, we'll meet the creative minds who transform dreams into a spectacular reality. Read on to uncover the design secrets that make India's popular stars shine even brighter at home.

## Gauri Khan, Gauri Khan Designs


[#image: /photos/6643a5d2265ca80b06be8e46]|||*Gauri Khan with Ananya Panday.*|||

Gauri Khan Designs was founded in 2013 in Mumbai. A leading name in the interior design industry, Gauri Khan has turned heads for her focus of crafting spaces boasting a luxurious and elegant design aesthetic. Khan has spearheaded the interior design of numerous celebrity homes, including [Ananya Panday’s new abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-ananya-pandays-first-home-in-mumbai-designed-by-gauri-khan/){: target="_blank"} and [Karan Johar’s home renovation](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/karan-johars-bachelor-pad-in-mumbai-is-as-glamorous-as-his-bollywood-films-celebrity-homes-gauri-khan-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“Design has always piqued my interest,” expresses the designer, “I have a background in graphic design and have worked with charcoal and oil painting, which has helped me refine my skills. But it wasn't until I was designing my house, Mannat, that I realised design is my calling. Striking the perfect balance between adding aesthetics and factoring in the clients' brief, while transforming spaces is Gauri Khan Designs' major USP. Additionally, we possess the ability to elevate any space into an imaginative, thoughtful and compelling environment that feels bespoke, and is a reflection of its people.”

Also read: [*6 famous homes and stores designed by AD100 designer Gauri Khan*](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/famous-homes-and-stores-designed-by-ad100-designer-gauri-khan-shah-rukh-khan-home/){: target="_blank"}

## Sarah Sham, Essajees Atelier


[#image: /photos/6643a659265ca80b06be8e48]|||*Sarah Sham with Ishan Khatter.*|||

Mumbai-based Sarah Sham is the founder of Essajees Atelier, which is renowned for crafting timeless homes that showcase the clients’ personality. In Sarah’s 15 years of work experience, she has crafted the interiors of several high-profile spaces such as [Ishan Khatter’s home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ishaan-khatter-mumbai-apartment-is-a-sunset-sanctuary-celebrity-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“My personal approach to work, which naturally influences all of Essajees Atelier’s endeavours, revolves around consistently aiming to provide top-notch décor solutions. This is achieved through close collaboration with our clients and by offering extensive customisation to fulfil their specific requirements. In terms of Essajees Atelier’s signature style, I think the one thing that we prioritise over all else is to maintain a sense of elegance and timelessness in every home we design. We take great pains to create spaces that go beyond passing design trends, ensuring longevity beyond the transient styles that may fade within a few years,” mentions Sarah Sham, interior designer and founder of Essajees Atelier.

## reD Architects


[#image: /photos/6643a6ae1825a14bc3f6df59]|||*Rajiv and Ekta Parekh with Sonakshi Sinha.*|||

reD Architects is a design and architecture studio that has been instrumental in creating the interiors of a number of celebrity homes such as that of [Sonakshi Sinha](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonakshi-sinhas-4000-square-foot-mumbai-home-is-an-embodiment-of-understated-luxury-red-architects-rajiv-ekta-parekh/){: target="_blank"}. At the heart of the ethos at reD Architects lies a philosophy that amalgamates contextual, vernacular, and contemporary elements. The Mumbai-based studio’s design language resonates with the surroundings, embracing local flavours while infusing modern sensibilities. Each project, be it residential or commercial unfolds as a narrative crafted from the fabric of its context.

“We view design as a collaborative symphony. Our team seamlessly collaborates with artists, sculptors, engineers, and various design experts. This convergence of diverse perspectives infuses each project with nuanced insights, enriching the design process and ensuring comprehensive solutions that cater to the diverse perceptions of our clients and users. Purpose-driven design isn’t just a concept for us, it’s a philosophy ingrained in every blueprint, every structure, and every interaction. We strive to achieve our clients’ goals through the process of research,” mention Rajiv Parekh, Ekta Parekh and Mithali Raut, founders of reD Architects.

## Quirk Studio


[#image: /photos/6643a72ea4d606164a1e0ac3]|||*Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera with Aditya Seal and and Anushka Ranjan.*|||

Quirk Studio is a boutique interior design firm based out of Mumbai. Founded in 2013 by Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, the studio finds its roots in the confluence of the design ideologies of the duo. Striving towards an ethos that shapes people-centric spaces and environments, the design firm has conceptualised the interiors of [Aditya Seal and Anushka Ranjan Seal’s Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-aditya-seal-and-anushka-ranjan-mumbai-home/){: target="_blank"}. Inspired and influenced by nature, Quirk Studio strives to establish a relationship with the surroundings, weaving a narrative befitting the physical context.

“Our design aesthetic sustains functionality while being classic in its appearance. Every project observes a distinct process of brainstorming that drives the design ideation, which is different for each project we undertake. Our approach is client-centric, process driven to learn and unlearn with every commission, resulting in an ever-growing and evolving practice,” remark Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, founders of Quirk Studio.

## Rupin Suchak


[#image: /photos/68008ee28854073fec1fac3b]

Interior designer Rupin Suchak designed actor [Aparshakti Khurana's Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-aparshakti-khuranas-mumbai-home-displays-drama-in-the-details-designed-by-rupin-suchak/){: target="_blank"}. Suchak is well-known for crafting unorthodox spaces that are dramatic, exhibit the resident’s personality, and push the boundaries of conventional interior design.

“I like to get really involved in everything I do, and give my hundred percent to any project that I am doing. I also have a penchant for exploring madness in design. I do not see things they stereotypically should be seen. For me, design is about having fun and being confident, I don’t want to get bored with something I create. I never want to feel a lack of ownership for what I have made. Hence, right from receiving the brief, for whatever kind of spaces I might be working with, I like to start thinking a little off-beat. I follow this path, hoping the result excites me. For me, each subsequent project has to be better than the other one, especially in terms of the design value and process. I am growing in my design sensibilities with time and I believe that should reflect in every project I do, and of course, it should keep becoming more ‘mad’, in every way possible\!” states interior designer, Rupin Suchak.

## Phillippe Fouché, SAOTA


[#image: /photos/6643a8134b9db58e3f91bc0e]|||*Virat Kohli at his Alibaug villa.*|||

[Virat Kohli's Alibaug home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/cricketer-virat-kohlis-holiday-home-in-alibag-is-the-embodiment-of-rustic-biophilic-luxury-celebrity-homes-vacation-homes-alibaug/){: target="_blank"} by Avās Living, created by globally acclaimed architects at SAOTA led by Phillippe Fouché, epitomises a biophilic design ethos, seamlessly blending modernity with nature-inspired elements. Nestled amidst the serene landscape of Alibaug, the overall design and style of the home exudes a modern yet contemporary vibe, characterised by natural light flowing in every corner, clean lines, sleek furnishings, and a calming colour palette.

## Shabnam Gupta, The Orange Lane
Over the last few decades, interior designer Shabnam Gupta has had several Indian celebrities for clients, including Aditya Chopra, Rajkumar Rao, Kangana Ranaut, Alia Bhatt and Parineeti Chopra. With a penchant for all things Indian, think bright colours, textures, and intricate aesthetics, Gupta started The Orange Lane, her design solution firm in 2003 that specialises in crafting exclusive home designs and furniture.

Shabnam’s design aesthetic is maximalist and eclectic, with a focus on incorporating traditional Indian artworks and natural elements. On how to design homes, Gupta advises homeowners to “be free to experiment\! Try to mix different cultures instead of sticking to a certain theme. If you do this, you will always end up giving a more diverse flavour to your home.”

## Kavita Singh, Kavita Singh Interiors


[#image: /photos/673c54dcc3ff95012fd5ab01]|||*Sonam Kapoor in the living room of her Mumbai home with her aunt and designer Kavita Singh.*|||

A true doyenne of design, AD100 designer Kavita Singh began her career three decades ago, when she founded her namesake brand of home decor accents in 1977. What began as a humble brand soon grew into a thriving practice—Singh’s tasteful eye was hard to miss, which was informed, over many years, by her deep interest in contemporary and decorative arts.

She collaborated with her niece, Sonam Kapoor, to bring the maximalist vision of [Kapoor's Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonam-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-a-maximalist-cocoon-of-indian-craftsmanship-and-heritage-celebrity-homes-ad100-interior-designer-kavita-singh/){: target="_blank"} to life. “Besides my nani, my aunt Kavita Singh has been the main voice within these walls, alongside my own,” says Kapoor. The pair threw themselves into transforming the contemporary blank canvas into, in Singh’s words, “the homage to Indian craftsmanship that my niece has been imagining for a long time”.

## Apoorva Shroff, Lyth Design


[#image: /photos/673c5ae8cd0c162a58f51b7b]|||*Madhuri Dixit with her husband, Dr Shriram Madhav Nene, and their Indie friend, Carmelo, in their Mumbai apartment.*|||

Madhuri Dixit and Dr Shriram Madhav Nene’s sea-facing apartment in Mumbai was designed by Apoorva Shroff of Lyth Design to be a union between art and technology. A clear brief helped Shroff create a home that oozes with understated elegance and simplicity. The couple requested "a sanctuary that blends contemporary aesthetics with a minimalist undertone, emphasising straight lines, clean forms, and a sober palette’’. This also convinced Shroff to use a “neutral interior palette that complements the homeowners' vibrant personalities.’’

## Annkur Khosla, Annkur Khosla Design Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f2b8854073fec1fac3d]|||*Mira in a printed co-ord set from Moonray in the formal living room. She is wearing heels from Aquazzura, earrings from Miss Jo, as well as rings from Misho and Miss Jo.*|||

The moment Mira Kapoor walked into the bare shell that is now her new [Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/mira-and-shahid-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-all-about-wholesome-family-fun-with-design-at-its-heart/){: target="_blank"}, she knew she had come home. It took 14 long months, but with the help of AD100 designer Annkur Khosla—a designer who Shahid Kapoor had worked with previously— she was able to bring her vision to life. “She’s a Virgo; I loved that. And she manages to blend Shahid’s and my aesthetic—that is often quite opposing—together.” Mira describes her husband’s taste as avant-garde while she looks for practicality. “Will it age well? How do we get the most storage space? How do we clean this?” she laughs. “Annkur was a fair referee and listener. We decided that in the kitchen and the kids’ room, I had a veto; upstairs, Shahid had a veto, and whatever was left was under negotiation\!”

*Original text by Priyanka Khanna, edited for context.*

## Kush Bhayani, KULx Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f6950c932f7d0e5f1b0]|||*Salaam Namaste (2005) and Dhamaal (2007) actor and television host, Jaaved Jaaferi in his living room in Bandra.*|||

For the design of his [Bandra home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-jaaved-jaaferis-sea-facing-mumbai-home-is-a-mediterranean-oasis-made-of-memories-celebrity-homes-designed-by-kush-bhayani-of-kulx-studio/){: target="_blank"}, Jaaved Jaaferi approached Kush Bhayani of KULx Studio. After hours of discussion and brainstorming, his brief was to create a space with minimal sophistication. “Warm, natural, eclectic.’’ And so, Kush, with the help of Polyfloors India, designed a home that hangs heavy with history…a mature, expansive twin of his family home with Mediterranean design ethos.

Open communication is key for Kush Bhayani. Regular meetings and constructive inputs from Jaaved resulted in a well-balanced, harmonious home. While merging functionality with aesthetics raises a challenge, at the heart of the design stands individuality. “Incorporating personal touches and meaningful elements is the cornerstone of our design approach.’’ Mementos, favourite colours and patterns, and characteristic details of home dwellers aren’t just a requirement but also a design vernacular that resonates with Kush. He says, “Jaaved was part of the construction, actively present on site, helping us navigate a place he calls home.’’

*Original text by Harleen Kalsi, edited for context.*

Les 10 projets les plus spectaculaires de Frank Gehry, de Paris à Los Angeles: [Frank Gehry](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/hommage-frank-gehry-architecte-96-ans), l'architecte américain légendaire né au Canada, dont l'œuvre a élargi le domaine esthétique du design et provoqué *« l'effet Bilbao »*, est décédé à Santa Monica, en Californie, ce vendredi 5 décembre. Il avait 96 ans.

Le travail de [Frank Gehry](https://www.admagazine.fr/adinspiration/article/frank-gehry-portrait-architecte-designer) a toujours été axé sur l'architecture en tant que sculpture ; devant le jury Pritzker en 1989, il a cité l'artiste Constantin Brâncuși comme une source d'inspiration plus importante que de nombreux architectes de l'époque. Cependant, il ne considérait pas que son travail était uniquement basé sur la forme et reconnaissait que l'architecture avait aussi des exigences fonctionnelles qui étaient intrinsèquement nécessaires. *« J'aborde chaque bâtiment comme un objet sculptural, un contenant spatial, un espace avec de la lumière et de l'air, une réponse au contexte et à l'adéquation du sentiment et de l’esprit »*, a-t-il déclaré un jour. *« Dans ce contenant, cette sculpture, l'utilisateur apporte son bagage, son programme, et interagit avec lui pour répondre à ses besoins. S'il ne peut pas le faire, j'ai échoué. »*

## Les 10 projets les plus spectaculaires de Frank Gehry entre Paris, Arles, Bilbao…

7 sustainable architecture insights for India from BIG's iconic projects: “In *Formgivi*ng. *An Architectural Future History*, Bjarke Ingels writes that as architects, we must give something back to society,” begins Kai-Uwe Bergmann, partner at the globally renowned Bjarke Ingels Group (BIG). The ethos of sustainable architecture (an overused and often abused encapsulation of a branch of architecture that leaves much to interpretation) is being delightfully reshaped by BIG—moving away from a paradigm of limitation to one of opportunity. From hybrid power plants to community-driven [public parks](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ahmedabads-parimal-garden-offers-a-poignant-reflection-of-the-city-since-the-1950s-prabhakar-bhagwat-landscape-architecture/){: target="_blank"}, the design studio has long championed the idea that sustainability should not only be functional—but also joyful, social, and deeply contextual.



[#image: /photos/67f4d7af2f0b5ef9e021a55e]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

In conversation with *AD* India, Bergmann elaborates on this understanding of the social and sustainable architecture that drives BIG’s iconic projects, each of them offering not only a design strategy but a philosophical framework that can be meaningfully applied across the globe, and particularly to India's diverse, rapidly urbanising context.

## Make Sustainability Stick
Before studying their projects, it is important to note how BIG’s understanding of social sustainability guides them. As Bergmann points out, the classical understanding of the term innately involves some sense of limiting oneself, or spelling out a reduction in the quality of life for the greater good. BIG’s approach of enhancing through sustainability is different, one they have aptly termed ‘Hedonistic Sustainability’, and it begins with a simple question: *In what world do we actually want to live?*



[#image: /photos/67f4d81462d1bebe58149915]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark.*|||

“There is a greater chance that people will change their habits if sustainability contributes to improving everyone’s lives. I believe this is also true in India, where the greater populace would rather move towards something that increases the quality of their daily life than limiting their choices,” Bergmann rightly points out. It is this sense of collective, communal joy that BIG seeks to channel through the power of design.

## CopenHill, Copenhagen: Infrastructure as an Educational Experience


[#image: /photos/67f4d64562d1bebe5814990f]|||*Copenhill in Copenhagen presents an industrial plant reimagined as a leisure hub.*|||

In the outskirts of Copenhagen, CopenHill is a power plant designed to incinerate waste and convert it into clean energy for 150,000 homes, while simultaneously offering a [lush green rooftop](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-vastu-compliant-garden-home-in-mumbai-with-a-2200-square-foot-terrace/){: target="_blank"} replete with community functions—presenting a hybrid industrial plant and leisure park. “The concept includes a didactic strand: once onsite, visitors find out how waste incinerator plants function, an important part of a functioning society,” Bergmann elaborates.



[#image: /photos/67f4d63e62d1bebe5814990d]|||*Copenhill, Copenhagen*|||

With India’s pace of growth, there exists an enormous need for future energy sources. While these should primarily come from renewables, Bergmann speaks about the possibility of using recycling, upcycling and new technologies to create additional energy loops. Urban India urgently needs to shift public perception around waste and energy and projects like CopenHill show that essential infrastructure doesn’t need to be hidden—it can be celebrated, raising environmental literacy through integrating learning and leisure opportunities.

## Google Bay View Campus: Harnessing Solar Power


[#image: /photos/67f4d69e0dddd9765880c120]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

The shimmering, dragon-scale roof of the sprawling Google Bay View Campus in Silicon Valley, California (designed by BIG—Bjarke Ingels Group and [Heatherwick Studios](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/you-wont-believe-work-gets-done-at-these-three-google-offices/){: target="_blank"} in close collaboration with Google) might resemble a sprawling piece of futuristic origami, but actually boasts a photovoltaic roof made of 50,000 solar modules that ripple across the surface, expected to produce 90% of the campus’ energy requirements. “When it comes to choosing a project’s structural material, we carefully examine in advance what makes the most structural sense onsite, precisely with regard to the energy concept or the building’s life cycle,” Bergmann explains.



[#image: /photos/67f4d69c0dddd9765880c11f]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

In India, the abundance of sunlight during the summer months or rainwater during the monsoon season opens doors to immense possibilities. With the still-scarce adoption of techniques that harness these elemental powers, projects like the [Google Campus](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/content/googles-new-1-billion-london-hq-massive-dubbed-landscraper/){: target="_blank"} serve as a prescient reminder that technology can be reframed not as a clunky afterthought, but as an aesthetic gesture.

## Superkilen: Creation through Public Participation


[#image: /photos/67f4d6f262d1bebe58149911]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark brought to life through public participation.*|||

“Architecture must not be despotic, but represent a dialogue with context, history, and tradition. It should be an osmotic process of exchange and reciprocal enrichment,” says Bergmann. A large proportion of BIG’s socially sustainable projects employ public participation as a driving force for change. In Superkilen, an urban landscape park in an ethnically diverse neighbourhoods in Denmark with 60 different nationalities, BIG invited citizens to nominate objects from their home countries—Moroccan fountains, Russian neon signs, Indian benches, etc—to incorporate into the park’s identity, lending a sense of ownership to this [communal space](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-indian-courtyards-that-truly-exemplify-indoor-outdoor-living/){: target="_blank"}.



[#image: /photos/67f4d6e12f0b5ef9e021a55c]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark*|||

“By transforming public procedure into proactive proposition, we curated a park for the people, by the people—peer-to-peer design,” he says. In Indian cities teeming with plural, informal voices left out of formal planning, this serves as a stark reminder that participatory design can reclaim ideas of true democratic urbanism.

## BIG U: Climactic Resilience to Cultural Richness
In response to the devastation of Hurricane Sandy, BIG proposed the BIG U: a 16-kilometre-long flood protection system wrapping around Lower Manhattan. But it doesn’t just block water; it creates greenways, markets, [amphitheatres](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-pune-design-studio-marries-art-food-music-and-mindful-living-experiences/){: target="_blank"}, and open spaces for residents to use year-round. “Every place has a soul, a genius loci that must be listened to, understood, and interpreted,” emphasises Bergmann, that goes beyond function in architecture to the culture of the city.

With coastal cities in India facing increased climatic threats as development continues, planning goals need to go beyond utilitarian protection to adaptable, human-centric urban systems. The BIG U model offers a compelling reframing: climate responses as an opportunity for cultural activation.

## Telosa: Building Future Cities Consciously


[#image: /photos/67f4d7af62d1bebe58149913]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

How does one go about building a city from scratch? [Telosa](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/step-into-a-dollar500-billion-city-of-the-future-dreamed-up-by-an-american-billionaire/){: target="_blank"}, a proposed city for five million residents in the American desert, designed with walkability, renewable energy, clean air, and shared equity as its founding principles serves as an example where even the economic model upon which the city is founded was rethought. The founder Marc Lore is laying the groundwork for an equitable urban environment, bolstered by BIG’s vision for accessible and healthy urban spaces.



[#image: /photos/67f4d8650dddd9765880c122]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

“In India, the sheer magnitude of city-making that is necessary to keep up with its population growth means that entire neighbourhoods and cities are being planned informally, and don’t always take into consideration the many systems needed to sustain them. This means that they grow at such a speed that mobility systems can break down,” Bergmann cautions. Clean water resources, public transportation systems, and [renewable energy](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/sponsored/story/discover-how-this-brands-advanced-technology-is-shaping-contemporary-spaces/){: target="_blank"} sources are at the root of Telosa’s thinking and should also be considered India - as any project, no matter what its size, will tax the country’s already exhausted systems.

## Drawing Lessons from the Past
Bergmann reminds us that sustainability was inherent to Indian architecture (shaded courtyards, generously laid-out [stepwells](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-stepwell-in-sudi-will-be-resorted-to-its-former-glory-by-the-deccan-heritage-foundation/){: target="_blank"}, evaporative cooling through public ponds of water benefiting the society, not just the patron who built them, and more). He also references dharamshalas in India. “I was always taken by these structures, which are simple, pragmatic, and well placed in the centres of towns to offer any passing traveler a place to rest their feet and meet local inhabitants,” he reveals. For India, where social sustainability was once such an intrinsic part of local architecture, it might just be a better matter of getting back to the roots.



[#image: /photos/67f4da230dddd9765880c124]|||*Bjarke Ingels Group’s monograph, Formgiving. An Architectural Future History.*|||

Bergmann’s observations and subsequent insights, from his research and time in India as a collaborator at [Conscious Collective](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/conscious-collective-by-godrej-and-boyce-heralds-innovations-in-sustainable-design/){: target="_blank"} by Godrej & Boyce, reveal that in the race to build bigger and better, there exists a prescient reminder for India that sustainable architecture isn’t limited to certifications and boxes to check—it needs to be a mindset that intersects imagination, empathy and equity.
