A Timeless Kitchen Design Sits at the Heart of This Renovated 19th-Century Stone House: Boasting a beautifully [timeless kitchen design](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/5-timeless-kitchen-design-ideas), Stone Crop in the rolling green countryside near Elora, Ontario, is a rare example of early [settler architecture](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/colonial-architecture-everything-you-need-to-know) that has been carefully updated. Built in 1833 by Thomas Dow, a Scottish mason who helped shape the town’s earliest buildings, the property has recently been renovated for a young couple working in tech who relocated to the area from the city to raise a family.



[#image: /photos/681f8bce9fbf11ed3f5f7277]

“This project was about finding a balance between respecting the building’s history and making it practical for everyday life,” says interior designer [Keri MacLellan](https://www.west-grove.co/about), founder of [Westgrove](https://www.west-grove.co/), the studio that led the renovation. “We wanted to keep what made the house special while opening it up and bringing in more light and flow.”



[#image: /photos/681f8bf4d0e469ea6da51cc7]

MacLellan chose to celebrate the bones of the building rather than mask them. “We exposed all the original stonework throughout the existing structure,” she says. “There’s a richness to those textures that tells the story of the house better than any object could.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c193f60d2e393b1cda1]

One of the more complex challenges lay in the connection between the original home and a newer extension, added in the early 2000s. “The [original stone walls](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/mallorcan-house-by-rock-and-villa-is-a-lesson-in-refined-mediterranean-luxury) are nearly two feet thick,” says MacLellan. “Rather than try to disguise that transition, we decided to expose it.” The team cut a dramatic passageway through the stone, creating an 18-inch-deep threshold that now forms a striking portal between the old cottage and the contemporary living spaces beyond. “It’s like stepping through time,” she adds.



[#image: /photos/681f8c329fbf11ed3f5f7279]



[#image: /photos/681f8c44d0e469ea6da51cc9]

The interiors reflect the refined sensibility of its owners – both passionate [collectors of ceramics](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/gallery/see-how-stunning-ceramics-can-transform-a-space), design and art. “They wanted something calm and grounded but not too minimal,” explains the designer. [Natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/sustainable-house-mallorca-ernest-bordoy) anchor the palette. Take, for example, the timeless kitchen design: pale plaster walls, oiled white oak, woven cane, and slubby linens complement the texture of the exposed historic stone walls.



[#image: /photos/681f8c59d0e469ea6da51ccb]

To add colour and visual interest, the couple commissioned a series of artworks from local painter [Alex Moore-Gibson](https://www.alexmooregibsonart.com/). Her layered canvases enliven the otherwise neutral interiors while remaining in keeping with their spirit. “We wanted the art to feel like part of the home, not something layered on top,” MacLellan notes.



[#image: /photos/681f8c76d0e469ea6da51ccd]

Bespoke ceramic lighting – pendants, sconces and mobiles – created in collaboration with [Sara Wood](https://www.sarawoodbyhand.com/), a local designer known for her organic forms and earthy glazes, is also peppered throughout the cottage. “The client is an accomplished ceramicist herself, so this partnership was particularly meaningful,” says the designer. “The ceramics added softness and helped tie everything together.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c919fbf11ed3f5f727b]

Built-in cabinetry was designed to [maximise storage](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/10-clever-kitchen-storage-ideas-to-maximise-your-space) without overwhelming the rooms. Underpinning the timeless kitchen design, a cane-fronted island blends traditional references with a clean, contemporary shape. “The idea was to use [natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-modern-cote-dazur-home-lays-down-the-blueprint-for-chic-indoor-outdoor-living) and simple forms that would last,” MacLellan explains. “Nothing here is about trends.”



[#image: /photos/681f8cb83f60d2e393b1cda3]

The renovation was completed in six months and, despite the challenges of working within the constraints of a heritage home, the result feels perfectly coherent. Original features like architraves and stone walls remain untouched, but they now sit alongside new interventions that add light and ease of use. “The owners were really happy with how the original and new elements came together,” says MacLellan, ““There’s a sense that this house has history but it’s also ready for the future.”

Meet the architects and designers who design the homes of Indian celebrities: Beyond the red carpets and flashing lights, Indian celebrities retreat to sanctuaries designed by the country's (and beyond) most talented interior designers and architects. In this exclusive peek behind the velvet ropes, we'll meet the creative minds who transform dreams into a spectacular reality. Read on to uncover the design secrets that make India's popular stars shine even brighter at home.

## Gauri Khan, Gauri Khan Designs


[#image: /photos/6643a5d2265ca80b06be8e46]|||*Gauri Khan with Ananya Panday.*|||

Gauri Khan Designs was founded in 2013 in Mumbai. A leading name in the interior design industry, Gauri Khan has turned heads for her focus of crafting spaces boasting a luxurious and elegant design aesthetic. Khan has spearheaded the interior design of numerous celebrity homes, including [Ananya Panday’s new abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-ananya-pandays-first-home-in-mumbai-designed-by-gauri-khan/){: target="_blank"} and [Karan Johar’s home renovation](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/karan-johars-bachelor-pad-in-mumbai-is-as-glamorous-as-his-bollywood-films-celebrity-homes-gauri-khan-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“Design has always piqued my interest,” expresses the designer, “I have a background in graphic design and have worked with charcoal and oil painting, which has helped me refine my skills. But it wasn't until I was designing my house, Mannat, that I realised design is my calling. Striking the perfect balance between adding aesthetics and factoring in the clients' brief, while transforming spaces is Gauri Khan Designs' major USP. Additionally, we possess the ability to elevate any space into an imaginative, thoughtful and compelling environment that feels bespoke, and is a reflection of its people.”

Also read: [*6 famous homes and stores designed by AD100 designer Gauri Khan*](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/famous-homes-and-stores-designed-by-ad100-designer-gauri-khan-shah-rukh-khan-home/){: target="_blank"}

## Sarah Sham, Essajees Atelier


[#image: /photos/6643a659265ca80b06be8e48]|||*Sarah Sham with Ishan Khatter.*|||

Mumbai-based Sarah Sham is the founder of Essajees Atelier, which is renowned for crafting timeless homes that showcase the clients’ personality. In Sarah’s 15 years of work experience, she has crafted the interiors of several high-profile spaces such as [Ishan Khatter’s home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ishaan-khatter-mumbai-apartment-is-a-sunset-sanctuary-celebrity-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“My personal approach to work, which naturally influences all of Essajees Atelier’s endeavours, revolves around consistently aiming to provide top-notch décor solutions. This is achieved through close collaboration with our clients and by offering extensive customisation to fulfil their specific requirements. In terms of Essajees Atelier’s signature style, I think the one thing that we prioritise over all else is to maintain a sense of elegance and timelessness in every home we design. We take great pains to create spaces that go beyond passing design trends, ensuring longevity beyond the transient styles that may fade within a few years,” mentions Sarah Sham, interior designer and founder of Essajees Atelier.

## reD Architects


[#image: /photos/6643a6ae1825a14bc3f6df59]|||*Rajiv and Ekta Parekh with Sonakshi Sinha.*|||

reD Architects is a design and architecture studio that has been instrumental in creating the interiors of a number of celebrity homes such as that of [Sonakshi Sinha](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonakshi-sinhas-4000-square-foot-mumbai-home-is-an-embodiment-of-understated-luxury-red-architects-rajiv-ekta-parekh/){: target="_blank"}. At the heart of the ethos at reD Architects lies a philosophy that amalgamates contextual, vernacular, and contemporary elements. The Mumbai-based studio’s design language resonates with the surroundings, embracing local flavours while infusing modern sensibilities. Each project, be it residential or commercial unfolds as a narrative crafted from the fabric of its context.

“We view design as a collaborative symphony. Our team seamlessly collaborates with artists, sculptors, engineers, and various design experts. This convergence of diverse perspectives infuses each project with nuanced insights, enriching the design process and ensuring comprehensive solutions that cater to the diverse perceptions of our clients and users. Purpose-driven design isn’t just a concept for us, it’s a philosophy ingrained in every blueprint, every structure, and every interaction. We strive to achieve our clients’ goals through the process of research,” mention Rajiv Parekh, Ekta Parekh and Mithali Raut, founders of reD Architects.

## Quirk Studio


[#image: /photos/6643a72ea4d606164a1e0ac3]|||*Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera with Aditya Seal and and Anushka Ranjan.*|||

Quirk Studio is a boutique interior design firm based out of Mumbai. Founded in 2013 by Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, the studio finds its roots in the confluence of the design ideologies of the duo. Striving towards an ethos that shapes people-centric spaces and environments, the design firm has conceptualised the interiors of [Aditya Seal and Anushka Ranjan Seal’s Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-aditya-seal-and-anushka-ranjan-mumbai-home/){: target="_blank"}. Inspired and influenced by nature, Quirk Studio strives to establish a relationship with the surroundings, weaving a narrative befitting the physical context.

“Our design aesthetic sustains functionality while being classic in its appearance. Every project observes a distinct process of brainstorming that drives the design ideation, which is different for each project we undertake. Our approach is client-centric, process driven to learn and unlearn with every commission, resulting in an ever-growing and evolving practice,” remark Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, founders of Quirk Studio.

## Rupin Suchak


[#image: /photos/68008ee28854073fec1fac3b]

Interior designer Rupin Suchak designed actor [Aparshakti Khurana's Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-aparshakti-khuranas-mumbai-home-displays-drama-in-the-details-designed-by-rupin-suchak/){: target="_blank"}. Suchak is well-known for crafting unorthodox spaces that are dramatic, exhibit the resident’s personality, and push the boundaries of conventional interior design.

“I like to get really involved in everything I do, and give my hundred percent to any project that I am doing. I also have a penchant for exploring madness in design. I do not see things they stereotypically should be seen. For me, design is about having fun and being confident, I don’t want to get bored with something I create. I never want to feel a lack of ownership for what I have made. Hence, right from receiving the brief, for whatever kind of spaces I might be working with, I like to start thinking a little off-beat. I follow this path, hoping the result excites me. For me, each subsequent project has to be better than the other one, especially in terms of the design value and process. I am growing in my design sensibilities with time and I believe that should reflect in every project I do, and of course, it should keep becoming more ‘mad’, in every way possible\!” states interior designer, Rupin Suchak.

## Phillippe Fouché, SAOTA


[#image: /photos/6643a8134b9db58e3f91bc0e]|||*Virat Kohli at his Alibaug villa.*|||

[Virat Kohli's Alibaug home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/cricketer-virat-kohlis-holiday-home-in-alibag-is-the-embodiment-of-rustic-biophilic-luxury-celebrity-homes-vacation-homes-alibaug/){: target="_blank"} by Avās Living, created by globally acclaimed architects at SAOTA led by Phillippe Fouché, epitomises a biophilic design ethos, seamlessly blending modernity with nature-inspired elements. Nestled amidst the serene landscape of Alibaug, the overall design and style of the home exudes a modern yet contemporary vibe, characterised by natural light flowing in every corner, clean lines, sleek furnishings, and a calming colour palette.

## Shabnam Gupta, The Orange Lane
Over the last few decades, interior designer Shabnam Gupta has had several Indian celebrities for clients, including Aditya Chopra, Rajkumar Rao, Kangana Ranaut, Alia Bhatt and Parineeti Chopra. With a penchant for all things Indian, think bright colours, textures, and intricate aesthetics, Gupta started The Orange Lane, her design solution firm in 2003 that specialises in crafting exclusive home designs and furniture.

Shabnam’s design aesthetic is maximalist and eclectic, with a focus on incorporating traditional Indian artworks and natural elements. On how to design homes, Gupta advises homeowners to “be free to experiment\! Try to mix different cultures instead of sticking to a certain theme. If you do this, you will always end up giving a more diverse flavour to your home.”

## Kavita Singh, Kavita Singh Interiors


[#image: /photos/673c54dcc3ff95012fd5ab01]|||*Sonam Kapoor in the living room of her Mumbai home with her aunt and designer Kavita Singh.*|||

A true doyenne of design, AD100 designer Kavita Singh began her career three decades ago, when she founded her namesake brand of home decor accents in 1977. What began as a humble brand soon grew into a thriving practice—Singh’s tasteful eye was hard to miss, which was informed, over many years, by her deep interest in contemporary and decorative arts.

She collaborated with her niece, Sonam Kapoor, to bring the maximalist vision of [Kapoor's Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonam-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-a-maximalist-cocoon-of-indian-craftsmanship-and-heritage-celebrity-homes-ad100-interior-designer-kavita-singh/){: target="_blank"} to life. “Besides my nani, my aunt Kavita Singh has been the main voice within these walls, alongside my own,” says Kapoor. The pair threw themselves into transforming the contemporary blank canvas into, in Singh’s words, “the homage to Indian craftsmanship that my niece has been imagining for a long time”.

## Apoorva Shroff, Lyth Design


[#image: /photos/673c5ae8cd0c162a58f51b7b]|||*Madhuri Dixit with her husband, Dr Shriram Madhav Nene, and their Indie friend, Carmelo, in their Mumbai apartment.*|||

Madhuri Dixit and Dr Shriram Madhav Nene’s sea-facing apartment in Mumbai was designed by Apoorva Shroff of Lyth Design to be a union between art and technology. A clear brief helped Shroff create a home that oozes with understated elegance and simplicity. The couple requested "a sanctuary that blends contemporary aesthetics with a minimalist undertone, emphasising straight lines, clean forms, and a sober palette’’. This also convinced Shroff to use a “neutral interior palette that complements the homeowners' vibrant personalities.’’

## Annkur Khosla, Annkur Khosla Design Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f2b8854073fec1fac3d]|||*Mira in a printed co-ord set from Moonray in the formal living room. She is wearing heels from Aquazzura, earrings from Miss Jo, as well as rings from Misho and Miss Jo.*|||

The moment Mira Kapoor walked into the bare shell that is now her new [Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/mira-and-shahid-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-all-about-wholesome-family-fun-with-design-at-its-heart/){: target="_blank"}, she knew she had come home. It took 14 long months, but with the help of AD100 designer Annkur Khosla—a designer who Shahid Kapoor had worked with previously— she was able to bring her vision to life. “She’s a Virgo; I loved that. And she manages to blend Shahid’s and my aesthetic—that is often quite opposing—together.” Mira describes her husband’s taste as avant-garde while she looks for practicality. “Will it age well? How do we get the most storage space? How do we clean this?” she laughs. “Annkur was a fair referee and listener. We decided that in the kitchen and the kids’ room, I had a veto; upstairs, Shahid had a veto, and whatever was left was under negotiation\!”

*Original text by Priyanka Khanna, edited for context.*

## Kush Bhayani, KULx Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f6950c932f7d0e5f1b0]|||*Salaam Namaste (2005) and Dhamaal (2007) actor and television host, Jaaved Jaaferi in his living room in Bandra.*|||

For the design of his [Bandra home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-jaaved-jaaferis-sea-facing-mumbai-home-is-a-mediterranean-oasis-made-of-memories-celebrity-homes-designed-by-kush-bhayani-of-kulx-studio/){: target="_blank"}, Jaaved Jaaferi approached Kush Bhayani of KULx Studio. After hours of discussion and brainstorming, his brief was to create a space with minimal sophistication. “Warm, natural, eclectic.’’ And so, Kush, with the help of Polyfloors India, designed a home that hangs heavy with history…a mature, expansive twin of his family home with Mediterranean design ethos.

Open communication is key for Kush Bhayani. Regular meetings and constructive inputs from Jaaved resulted in a well-balanced, harmonious home. While merging functionality with aesthetics raises a challenge, at the heart of the design stands individuality. “Incorporating personal touches and meaningful elements is the cornerstone of our design approach.’’ Mementos, favourite colours and patterns, and characteristic details of home dwellers aren’t just a requirement but also a design vernacular that resonates with Kush. He says, “Jaaved was part of the construction, actively present on site, helping us navigate a place he calls home.’’

*Original text by Harleen Kalsi, edited for context.*



[#cneembed: script/video/65ef2e8b4e96752b9088b685.js]

Muebles de Frank Lloyd Wright; una faceta brillante del arquitecto: Los **muebles de Frank Lloyd Wright** son casi un secreto, en este caso, una lámpara de **Frank Lloyd Wright** diseñada para la casa Dana-Thomas de Springfield (Illinois) acaba de batir un nuevo récord para el arquitecto, 66 años después de su muerte. Vendida por 7.5 millones de dólares en la Modern Evening Auction de Sotheby's, es el objeto de Wright más caro jamás subastado. Jodi Pollack, presidente de Sotheby's y codirector mundial de **diseño del siglo XX**, declaró en un comunicado que "esta venta récord no solo celebra una pieza extraordinaria del diseño estadounidense, sino un momento histórico en el legado de uno de los **arquitectos más visionarios de la historia**".

## Historia de la lámpara de doble pedestal de Frank Lloyd Wright
Wright diseñó la pieza, conocida como *Lámpara de doble pedestal*, en 1904 para Susan Lawrence Dana, una dama de la alta sociedad progresista de Springfield. Dana había encargado una casa (ahora conocida como la casa Dana-Thomas), y Wright, que no veía distinción entre decoración y arquitectura, **diseñó más de 100 piezas de mobiliario para el proyecto**, entre ellas dos lámparas de doble pedestal. Una de ellas se conserva en la casa Dana-Thomas, que ahora es una casa museo, mientras que la otra ha estado en colecciones privadas; quizá con algunos otros muebles de Frank Lloyd Wright.



[#image: /photos/682f4a0c60e441d96d6aaebe]|||Wright diseñó la lámpara de doble pedestal para la casa Dana-Thomas en 1904.|||

Con una base rectangular, dos finos pedestales rectangulares y una pantalla de cristal con forma similar a un tejado a cuatro aguas, [la lámpara muestra el famoso cristal artístico](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/lamparas-para-que-tu-casa-se-vea-lujosa) de Wright en un contexto único. "Cuando está iluminada, proyecta un cálido resplandor otoñal que **transforma la estancia en un poético juego de luces y formas**", se lee en el catálogo de la subasta. "A la luz del día, estos mismos paneles se transforman por completo, revelando una iridiscencia luminosa y efervescente ... Esta oscilación visual de lo otoñal a lo iridiscente confiere a la lámpara una doble identidad: al mismo tiempo es un **elemento integrado en su espacio arquitectónico** y se transforma de manera única en función del punto de vista de cada espectador".

Según Sotheby's, la lámpara fue "perseguida por un decidido grupo de pujadores en una animada batalla de casi 11 minutos". Al cerrarse en 7.5 millones de dólares, superó con creces la estimación, que se situaba entre 3 y 5 millones de dólares. La venta marca un nuevo récord de **subasta para Frank Lloyd Wright**, que había sido establecido previamente por la lámpara de techo *Francis W. Little House* de Wright, que se vendió en Sotheby's por 2.9 millones de dólares en 2023.

## El futuro de los objetos de diseño en las subastas
[El precio de la lámpara es significativo](https://www.admagazine.com/interiorismo/accesorios-arena-ideales-para-espacios-sofisticados-y-neutros-20190717-2854-galerias), ya que se vende por más que algunas de las residencias de Wright. Una de las primeras casas de Wright se vendió por 449 mil dólares en marzo de este año, mientras que la *Circular Sun House* del arquitecto, uno de sus mayores diseños, está actualmente a la venta por 8.9 millones de dólares. Según Sotheby's, **los objetos de diseño son cada vez más populares** entre los **coleccionistas de arte contemporáneo**.



[#image: /photos/682f4a0c60e441d96d6aaec0]|||La pantalla de la lámpara es similar a los aleros sobredimensionados que Wright empleó en muchos de sus diseños.|||

"Dadas las intersecciones que vemos entre el **diseño y el arte**, y el hecho de que cada vez más coleccionistas de arte importantes están [creando colecciones de diseño serias](https://www.admagazine.com/gran-diseno/lamparas-por-objeto-20160407-1206-galerias), intentamos aprovechar cualquier oportunidad para dar a conocer mejor nuestra oferta de diseño", dice Pollack *a AD.* Recientemente, la casa de subastas ha estado ofreciendo objetos de diseño excepcionales como parte de sus *Modern and Contemporary Evening Sales*, lo que ha impulsado la visibilidad entre los coleccionistas serios. "Lo hicimos con gran éxito en 2020 con una **mesa de comedor** única de Carlo Mollino y, más recientemente, con la ventana conmemorativa *Danner* de Tiffany y la lámpara de doble pedestal de Frank Lloyd Wright. En cada uno de estos casos vimos pujas competitivas de un público más amplio de coleccionistas de arte y nuevos récords mundiales logrados para cada artista", añade Pollack.

## Compraventa de objetos y muebles de Frank Lloyd Wright
Aunque la *Lámpara de doble pedestal* representa un hito en el **mercado de objetos de Wright**, los coleccionistas llevan mucho tiempo valorando sus piezas. Sin embargo, la compraventa de **muebles de Frqank Lloyd Wright** es a veces controvertida, ya que el arquitecto solía diseñar de forma holística, elaborando cada pieza para la casa en la que debía existir. Aun así, el deseo de poseer aunque sea una pequeña pieza del inmenso legado del **arquitecto y diseñador** mantiene vivo el interés.

*Artículo publicado originalmente en* **AD *US***.

The Best Hotels in Norway for a Scenic Stay Among the Fjords and Mountains: The best hotels in [Norway](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/best-places-to-visit-in-2025-according-to-ad-editors){: target="_blank"} don’t just frame the scenery – they’re part of the story. Each one reflects a different side of the country, from historic fishing warehouses and 19th-century hideaways to hillside cabins steeped in *friluftsliv*: the Norwegian love of open-air living.



[#image: /photos/68c3dccd7707303d2921061b]

There are some rather wonderful things about Norway that might be useful to know before I begin this piece. It has, for example, been voted the happiest country in the world; it is also credited with the invention of skiing and has the longest coastline in Europe. But it is, perhaps, best known for its outstanding landscapes, the deep blue fjords created during the Ice Age, mountains with cascading waterfalls and the perfect rust coloured cabins that sit along the rugged coastline.

## Also Read: [Northern Lights: The 10 Best Places to See the Magical Aurora Borealis](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/northern-lights-best-places-to-see-the-magical-aurora-borealis)


[#image: /photos/68c2cd6136ccf055d63c3bb3]

There are over 1,700 fjords in Norway, and it is on the West Coast where some of the most beautiful and dramatic landscapes can be found. This is primarily why 62 Nord has chosen this part of Norway to create three extraordinary hotels. The first of these hotels is in Alesund, reached from the airport via an extraordinary deep-sea tunnel. The town has a long history as a fishing port, but there is also a fascinating architectural twist to Alesund. After a fire devastated nearly half of the town (many of the buildings were wooden and the fire spread quickly), it was rebuilt in 1904. The craftspeople drafted in to rebuild it had studied abroad and were influenced by [the Art Nouveau style](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/discover-iconic-art-nouveau-architecture-in-vienna-walking-tour-guide-for-design-lovers){: target="_blank"}, which they combined with motifs from the Viking Age and Norse mythology.



[#externallink: /externallinks/650303f6bb917946d989f4c0]



[#image: /photos/68c2cdadfa4c7768c3149e17]

## The Hotel Brosundet


[#image: /photos/68c2cdf036ccf055d63c3bb5]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/UWxN5ZEu9Mcqjud7m3NKrqQiPk2F5fdBvejmRsDAQB42W4bSNTSsZmZKyxNidfALj3BvrJRnHsbueeEJ5CiXJXPM957RbYiHBq8jVez7aRHxJFKaqJY73QRGzAuFqR4XieR1qzWNuBobjnRAvCurrv "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

The Hotel Brosundet in Alesund was once a warehouse for local fisheries, and was one of the few wooden buildings that did not burn down. A warehouse that once stored fish might not seem like an inspired idea for a hotel location, but it is actually everything that you could possibly hope for if you were to conjure up an image of chic Nordic hospitality. The large reception area has an open [fireplace](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-faux-fireplace-brings-warmth-and-cosiness-to-this-minimal-living-room-in-dubai){: target="_blank"} big enough to host a large gathering of Vikings, with a chimney flue rising dramatically through the centre of the building. A fire is exactly what should be at the heart of a Norwegian hotel, where the cold is a constant for many months of the year. There are huge chunky beams that support the lower floors, instantly making the hotel feel solid and grounded in its heritage. Tucked into the corner of the reception is a small but beautifully curated shop selling handmade sweaters in creams, greys and taupe by the brand O.A.



[#image: /photos/68c2ce2636ccf055d63c3bb7]

Rooms are light and airy, filled with carefully chosen furniture, with windows looking out over the iconic Brosundet channel at one end and Art Nouveau architecture at the other. On the lower-ground floor is a beautifully designed gym and wellness area, including a large outdoor balcony with carved stone baths filled with hot water and bubbles, the perfect remedy after a dip in the Harbour, which can be accessed by a wooden jetty. In the adjoining building to the hotel is a beautiful deli called Butikken with a selection of Norwegian delicacies all beautifully displayed on wide wooden shelves. There are also rooms for wine tastings and private dinners.



[#image: /photos/68c2ce7836ccf055d63c3bb9]

Navigating the West Coast is easiest with a boat on hand, and this is all part of the service at 62 Nord. There really isn’t a more beautiful way to travel between hotels, a cosy rug on the lap, and a country revealing itself as you glide past. The rust coloured wooden summer houses clustered on rocky peninsulas, rugged mountains rising straight up from deep fjord waters.

## Union Øye Hotel


[#image: /photos/68c3de12d02ce634a26f900d]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/2kPDMaDggM5J7QcMdmQpCe23AZw8Qbrm9LAPvrmBLViJRSUkLm9R51onSuVv4hhYJw8ftE5tCLV9urAeZRY3kG12n9GXyv8HWsJ9f33hCpLUYsjjP1fJFVBQg2 "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

Waterfalls tumbling over grey rock as the snow melts, and the wild beauty of Norway revealing itself through every window. In a secluded hamlet at the end of the fjord surrounded by the craggy wild mountains, the Union Øye Hotel appears, a turreted, pale pink [fairytale](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-fairytale-castles-in-germany-that-seem-right-out-of-a-disney-movie){: target="_blank"} house. It has been here since 1891, a refuge for tweed-clad mountaineers who came to Norway to practice for expeditions to the Himalayas. Each of the bedrooms is named after the writers, mountaineers, artists and members of the Royal family who have stayed here. From the suit of armour that greets you in reception, to the cosy library filled with first edition leather-bound books, the Union Øye feels like the country home of a very well-read Norwegian aristocrat. Parked outside the hotel is a Morgan; guests can hire the car for a scenic drive through the mountains, with a picnic hamper packed for lunch.



[#image: /photos/68c3de7c9cdbfc06554a2280]



[#image: /photos/68c3de917707303d2921061d]

The hotel has been exquisitely decorated, the interiors a mix of wood panelled walls, dusty pink velvet sofas, and bedrooms wallpapered in the most perfect floral patterns. The colour palette is a blend of sage green, mushroom and vintage raspberry pink. There are tapestry rugs and cushions with fringed brocade – but there is nothing ‘fussy’ or contrived about this hotel. The large and light-filled conservatory, [painted in a beautiful sage green](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/green-room-decor-ideas-how-to-use-green-in-interior-design){: target="_blank"}, is a new addition where meals are served. Even in here, the cane chairs, oak leaf metal chandeliers and cleverly designed wooden chequered floor sit harmoniously with the rest of the building. Surrounding this jewel box of a hotel are mountains; some of the biggest and most challenging hiking paths in the country are found here. There is also e-biking, which is an excellent way to explore some of the countryside if you don't fancy a vertical ascent but a rather more relaxed road trip. Union Øye also has one of the most beautiful saunas you will ever visit. At the end of a long wooden jetty, there is a charcoal black house with a huge glass window overlooking the snow-capped mountains. Birds dive into the water, catching fish, and the waterfalls tumble through the Nordic pines, and the silence isn’t deafening; it is magnificent.

## Storfjord


[#image: /photos/68c3dd839cdbfc06554a227e]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/3Hvxm9czoAnBvsqu6eN8yzhCpJRg8pXux565DubbLEyoLua2jocRppAFGp5sM5a71C2QCfVLk4zAMDE7mpp4YcFg4Vn8VyNWhmyanMWjnRFi6v6fo5xAjkabXEZN84PtubscUZ7rgbv2LSyTwcU6MjKro6FgyaNrNCpwy7g "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

The third hotel in the trio of the best hotels in Norway is Storfjord, situated in the remote hamlet of Glomset, and the journey here is by car and electric ferry; again, the views are so spectacular that the drive is over too quickly. At Storfjord, the mood changes again; surrounded by trees, the smell is of freshly cut timber, pine needles and damp earth. There is a word in Norwegian, ‘Friluftsliv’, which translates as open-air living, first popularised by the playwright Henrik Ibsen. This hotel, like a very glamorous Winter cabin, with its partly turfed roof, has outstanding views over the fjords below. There is a cosy dining room, with another roaring fire and a sitting room decorated with traditional painted wooden bowls, and lined with more books. The libraries in each of the hotels are exceptional and are a complete passion of the owners. There are sofas upholstered in just the right shades of linen, with armchairs positioned everywhere to just sit and quietly read. The rooms, with underfloor heating, are decorated simply and beautifully, a peaceful, cosy retreat after a hike or a dip in the hot tub.



[#image: /photos/68c3ddc5d02ce634a26f900b]

This wild and wonderful coastline is one of the most spectacular locations in the world. The hotels that 62 Nord has created achieve the almost impossible. Each of the hotels sits in a landscape of unrivalled natural beauty, and yet each rests quietly in its position, as if it had always been there, waiting for guests to discover the freedom of ‘*Filuftsliv*’.

13 Most Beautiful Islands in the World To Visit – and the Books To Read While You’re There: **From the Maldives and Hawaii to Madagascar and Sir Bani Yas Island, discover the most beautiful islands in the world, places where rare wildlife, untamed landscapes, exceptional [hotels](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-hotels-in-the-world-2025-design-experts-share-their-favourite-stays){: target="_blank"} and pristine beaches converge to create visions of paradise.**

## 1\. Socotra, Yemen


[#image: /photos/699d910bf106a095cbec5304]

Floating in the Arabian Sea, closer to the Horn of Africa than [mainland Yemen](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/welcome-to-the-worlds-steepest-villages){: target="_blank"}, Socotra is often described as the most alien-looking island on Earth. Isolated for millions of years, it has evolved its own visual language: forests of umbrella-shaped Dragon’s Blood trees, limestone plateaus, and blindingly white dunes spilling into turquoise waters. Nearly a third of its plant life exists nowhere else, earning the archipelago UNESCO World Heritage status. Despite its otherworldly appearance, life on Socotra follows ancient rhythms, with small fishing villages, frankincense trees, and untouched beaches stretching for kilometres without interruption. It is not a place of resorts or spectacle, but of raw, elemental beauty – a reminder of what the natural world looked like before it was shaped by tourism.

**Our favourite hotel in Socotra:** Socotra Eco-Lodge, offering simple stays immersed in nature  
**Books to read while you're there:** _The Monk of Mokha_ by Dave Eggers – Follows a young Yemeni-American attempting to revive the Yemeni coffee industry amidst the escalating civil war, offering a portrait of resilience.  
[*welcometosocotra.com*](https://www.welcometosocotra.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 2\. Sir Bani Yas Island, United Arab Emirates


[#image: /photos/699d8f7af106a095cbec529e]

Off the coast of Abu Dhabi, Sir Bani Yas Island offers a rare proposition: a safari experience unfolding entirely on an island. Once the private nature reserve of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founding father of the UAE, the island today protects over 17,000 free-roaming animals, from Arabian oryx and gazelles to giraffes roaming freely against a backdrop of rugged mountains and coastal plains. Covering nearly half the island, the Arabian Wildlife Park sits alongside secluded beaches, mangroves, and archaeological remains, including the ruins of a 7th-century Christian monastery, offering a glimpse into the region’s layered past. Visitors can explore by 4x4, kayak through calm coastal waters, or simply retreat into the stillness of its desert-meets-sea landscape. Remote yet accessible, Sir Bani Yas represents a vision of conservation, hospitality, and heritage in balance.

**Our favourite hotel in Sir Bani Yas Island:** Anantara Desert Islands Resort, set between savannah and sea  
**Books to read while you're there:** *The Ring of the Dove* by Ibn Hazm – A classic treatise on the nature of love and human relationships.  
[*sirbaniyas.ae*](https://www.sirbaniyas.ae/){: target="_blank"}

## 3\. Madagascar


[#image: /photos/699d97dbf106a095cbec5414]

The fourth-largest island in the world, Madagascar drifts off the southeast coast of Africa like a forgotten continent, its landscapes shaped by millions of years of isolation. This separation has given rise to extraordinary biodiversity found nowhere else on Earth: forests of towering baobab trees, limestone pinnacles known as tsingy, and rare wildlife such as lemurs, chameleons and fossas. Along its coastline, deserted beaches meet warm turquoise waters, while inland, rainforests, canyons and highlands reveal constantly shifting terrain. Both raw and poetic, Madagascar offers a rare sense of discovery.

**Our favourite hotel in Madagascar:** Miavana by Time \+ Tide, set on a private island off the northeast coast  
**Books to read while you're there:** *The Aye-Aye and I* by Gerald Durrell – Documenting Durell's last expedition to this mystical country, The Aye-Aye treks the reader through Madagascar's glorious wildlife and the animals on the verge of extinction.    
[*madagascar-tourisme.com*](https://madagascar-tourisme.com/en/){: target="_blank"}

## 4\. Barbados
In the heart of the Caribbean, this coral island – the only one in the archipelago – blends exotic flavours with wild coastlines and 100 kilometres of beaches (over 80 beaches, all public). On land, you'll discover nature reserves – and its national mascot, the Green Verbena, a mischievous little monkey – sugar cane fields, coconut groves, refreshing waterfalls, as well as golf, polo and cricket, a direct legacy of the Commonwealth, from which the island gained independence in 2021. On the seaside, the wind conditions are perfect for surfing and kitesurfing, the reefs for diving, sea caves specific to its geology, beaches for watching turtles lay their eggs between April and November, and the flying fish offshore. In terms of typical Caribbean architecture, the sublime colonial residences in the [UNESCO-listed](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/new-unesco-world-heritage-site-southern-ohio-hopewell-ceremonial-earthworks){: target="_blank"} historic centre of the capital, Bridgetown, are a must-see, as are the famous coral stone villas along the coast designed by British theatre designer Oliver Messel and now a national heritage site.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def55]

**Our favourite hotel in Barbados**: the recently renovated Fairmont Royal Pavilion  
**Books to read while you're there**: *Even in paradise* by Elizabeth Nunez – An epic tale of family betrayal and manipulation couched in terrifically engaging prose and peopled with sketchy characters.  
[*visitbarbados.org*](http://visitbarbados.org/){: target="_blank"}

## 5\. Jamaica
Jamaica is the second Caribbean destination on AD's list of the most beautiful islands in the world, and for good reason. The island's north coast boasts some of the most stunning hidden beaches, including Frenchman's Cove – the legendary beach featured in the 1988 film *Cocktail* starring [Tom Cruise](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/where-does-tom-cruise-live-inside-the-mission-impossible-stars-real-estate-portfolio){: target="_blank"} – and *Seven Mile Beach*. Fine sand, turquoise water, palm trees: all the ingredients are there. But every stop between Montego Bay and Port Antonio is a delight to discover. Stroll through the spicy, multicoloured nugget markets, the local plantations, the fried, smoked or barbecued specialities on the street corner, always to the sound of music... Get off the beaten track and opt for a zip-line through the canopy, a dive in the Blue Lagoon (it was the setting for the famous Brooke Shields movie), visit a historic rum factory and its vertiginous stills in the middle of the jungle, and dip into its [heavenly waterfalls](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/most-spectacular-waterfalls-from-around-the-world){: target="_blank"}. Climb to an altitude of 2,000 meters to sip a cup of coffee at Blue Mountain, one of the world's finest, whose estate is a UNESCO World Heritage site. Inland, take a pirogue along the Rio Grande, and a little further on, take a break at the Goldeneye Hotel, where Ian Fleming wrote his first novel with an emblematic character: [James Bond](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/james-bond-villains-moroccan-villa-from-spectre-is-for-sale-for-dollar29-million){: target="_blank"}.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def56]

**Our favourite hotel in Jamaica**: Geejam, with its jungle huts  
**Books to read while you're there:** _The Lunatic_ by Anthony Winkler – A humorous and poignant look at life in rural Jamaica.  
[*fr.visitjamaica.com*](http://fr.visitjamaica.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 6\. Corsica
The wildest of the [Mediterranean islands](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/modern-and-minimal-holiday-home-in-menorca-spain){: target="_blank"} boasts an astonishing diversity of landscapes, from north to south, along its almost 1,000 km of unspoilt coastline. From the pink granite rocks of the Calanques de Piana to the immaculate limestone cliffs of Bonifacio and the [black sandy beach](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/9-beautiful-beaches-around-the-world-that-are-just-as-bizarre-as-they-are-stunning){: target="_blank"} of Nonza, the landscapes are majestic and never the same. Just as pleasant is the inland, bursting with freshwater, torrents, rivers and lakes to cool off in summer. Discover the hot springs at Caldane, Taccana or Baracci – which is due to reopen with its historic hotel. With 240 days of sunshine a year, the beaches of the Isle of Beauty are consistently ranked among the best in the world. In summer, when the island is saturated, people seek absolute peace in its typical villages – a local tradition while travellers enjoy the coastline – in search of shade and traditional inns, like Chez Séraphin, in Péri. With the refurbishment of the Domaine du Mouflon d'Or in Zonza, at the foot of the breathtaking Aiguilles de Bavella, Corsica's mountain side is slowly coming back to life... In the meantime, its popular beach clubs, private villas and ecolodges continue to be the stuff of dreams.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def57]

**Our favourite hotel in Corsica**: Le Domaine de Murtoli, in the south of the island  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Amalfi Secret* by Dean & Catherine Reineking – A fast-paced mystery thriller featuring a family vacation turned into a dangerous hunt for secrets across the Amalfi Coast and Rome.  
[*visit-corsica.com*](http://www.visit-corsica.com/){: target="_blank"}



[#externallink: /externallinks/65030450bb917946d989f4c2]

## 7\. The Seychelles
The Indian Ocean boasts many of the most beautiful islands in the world, listed here, but the Seychelles are truly special: Off the coast of East Africa, between Madagascar and Reunion Island, the Seychelles is an A-list island paradise unlike any other. A stopover on the Spice and Silk Routes, the archipelago was discovered by Vasco da Gama in 1503. With no fewer than 115 granite and coral islands – spread over more than 1,000 km – the Seychelles boast some of the most beautiful beaches in the world: Praslin, Anse Georgette, North Island, La Digue... Distinguishable from the rest by their polished rocks that seem to have fallen randomly from the sky onto white sandy beaches, the Seychelles are the ultimate version of paradise on Earth. You can easily island hop, discover giant tortoises, dive with sharks for the ultimate thrill and enjoy water sports. On land, you can discover mangroves, waterfalls and rare animals such as tiger chameleons and black parrots, between two sessions of golf or climbing on the famous flat rocks. Then it's time to *relax*. After all, it's 29°C all year round… and the island is protected from cyclones.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def58]

**Our favourite hotel in The Seychelles**: [Cheval Blanc](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-cheval-blanc-seychelles-what-its-like-to-stay-at-lvmhs-lush-indian-ocean-getaway){: target="_blank"}, the latest addition to the prestigious collection designed by architect Jean-Michel Gathy  
**Books to read while you're there**: *A Psalm for the Wild-Built* by Becky Chambers– A story about a tea monk (like a traveling therapist who serves tea) who meets a robot in the wilderness, and they converse about the differences in human and robot society and what it means to have a life's purpose.  
[*seychelles.fr*](http://www.seychelles.fr/){: target="_blank"}

## 8\. Mauritius
A gentle lifestyle and a tropical rhythm: Mauritius is an exotic, historic destination whose excellent service is an absolute benchmark in the world of hospitality. In the Indian Ocean, 800km east of Madagascar, Mauritius boasts picture-perfect beaches, often flanked by a panorama of the vertiginous Morne Brabant mountain in the south-west, with an average annual temperature of 25°C and 280 days of sunshine a year. On the agenda: golf, hiking, water sports, basta. Neither a show-off nor exhausting, the island has nothing left to prove about its paradisiacal appeal, which it deploys with grace and consistency. Its ultra-lush landscape allows you to discover grandiose ancestral forests and fascinating fauna. One of the most unforgettable experiences is to cross the Black River National Park, the largest national park and a veritable [sanctuary of biodiversity](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/beautiful-sustainable-buildings-green-architecture-thats-healing-our-planet){: target="_blank"}. Botany reigns supreme here, as in the Pamplemousse gardens, a legacy of the island's colonial past. What to pack: vanilla, rum, spices and black tea.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def59]

**Our favourite hotel in Mauritius**: The Royal Palm Beachcomber Luxury, an island institution  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The White Tiger* by Aravind Adiga (2008) A Booker Prize-winning novel that uses the term "white tiger" metaphorically to represent a unique individual in Indian society.  
[*mauritiusnow.com*](http://www.mauritiusnow.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 9\. Hawaii
With 137 [volcanic](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/how-cappadocia-turkeys-underground-cave-city-was-built-design-lovers-travel-bucket-list){: target="_blank"} islands, the Hawaiian archipelago is the only US state outside the North American continent. In the heart of Oceania, a 6-hour flight from Los Angeles, it's an unforgettable destination for thrill-seekers and Zen-cravers alike. Steep cliffs, sometimes crashing waves, the scent of tiare, black sandy beaches, and so many climates that accommodate a super-powerful natural environment, from tropical forests to lunar beaches. People come here to get married, facing the ocean and celebrate weddings, anniversaries, rebirths, in short, an exceptional moment. They bring back pink Hawaiian salt, flowery shirts, pearly shells and unforgettable souvenirs. In winter, you can watch humpback whales and soak up the diversity of this legendary archipelago.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5a]

**Our favourite hotel in Hawaii**: Four Seasons Lanai, totally exclusive  
**Books to read while you're there**: *Hula* by Jasmine Iolani Hakes – A novel set in Hilo in the 1970s that delves into the culture and history of hula.  
[*gohawaii.com*](https://www.gohawaii.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 10\. The Maldives
With 1,200 islands in its archipelago, spread over almost 800 km, the [Maldives](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/i-stayed-at-soneva-secret-in-the-maldives-heres-what-its-really-like){: target="_blank"} are a real head-turner. Of course, each island, almost privatised by a hotel brand – and all the big names are there – is an experience in itself, with villas on stilts, on the beach, with private swimming pools, a cinema with your feet in the sand and dinner for two on a tiny desert island. The Maldives are the perfect destination for honeymooners and, on some islands, families. On the postcard, an atoll, coral galore, crystal-clear water, face-to-face with manta rays, baby sharks and all manner of unknown exotic beasts, sushi dinners and fresh papaya for breakfast, a phantasmagorical spa with a view, total relaxation in front of sparkling lagoons. Irresistible. The Maldives is the terrestrial incarnation of a tropical paradise, which is developing ever more sustainable experiences to protect its flora and fauna: restoration of the coral reef, protection of sea turtles, meetings with marine biologists, etc.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5b]

**Our favourite hotel in The Maldives**: One & Only Reethi Ra  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Soul of an Octopus* by Sy Montgomery – A Surprising Exploration into the Wonder of Consciousness.  
[*visitmaldives.com*](https://visitmaldives.com/en){: target="_blank"}

## 11\. Zanzibar
Nicknamed the Spice Island, just 35 km east of the African coast, Zanzibar is a paradise-like interlude. Made up of three main islands (Unguja, Pemba, Mafia), a former sultanate and then British colony, the archipelago boasts an equatorial climate, influences from Africa, the [Arabian Peninsula](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/inspired-by-moroccan-palaces-this-home-in-mangaluru-india-reframes-islamic-art-traditions-for-a-new-generation){: target="_blank"} and [India](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-sprawling-safari-lodge-in-india-aramness-is-a-modern-day-sanctuary){: target="_blank"}, and is the cradle of Swahili culture. A kite spot and a national forest, the archipelago is a lush sanctuary inhabited by free-living red monkeys and blue vervet monkeys. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, its capital – the birthplace of Freddy Mercury – is a city steeped in history. Stroll through the spice market, its narrow streets with carved doors and its old fort. Discover the mangroves, the seaweed aquaculture traditionally managed by the women, the lagoons with their thousand shades of turquoise, everything has an exotic flavour in Zanzibar.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def53]

**Our favourite hotel in Zanzibar**: White Sand, Tanzania's only Relais & Châteaux  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Famished Road* by Ben Okri – Known for his magical realism, Okri (of Nigerian descent) became the youngest winner of the Booker Prize in 1991 for the novel.   
[*zanzibar.com*](https://www.zanzibar.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 12\. Tuamotu, French Polynesia
If there's a paradise far away from it all, it's French Polynesia. The 76 Tuamotu atolls are part of this timeless archipelago, 4,000 km – and many hours on a plane – from land. A journey well worth making... We could draw with our eyes closed its idyllic scenery, like those once painted by Gauguin. Coral islands stretching for almost 2000 km, the Tuamotu (*"distant islands"* in Tahitian) are famous for their treasures, exported to the other side of the world, such as *monoi* – made from tiare flowers and coconut oil – fresh fish, salt and the region's treasure, Tahitian black pearls. With your feet in the sand or in the crystal-clear water, levitating in [a villa on stilts](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/incredible-overwater-bungalow-resorts-to-book-around-the-world){: target="_blank"} or on a boat, but always far from the crowds, this is island living at its best in the heart of the Pacific Ocean. Absolute rest and total disconnection are almost mandatory in the face of these insolently beautiful lagoons and unreal coral rings.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5c]

**Our favourite hotel in Tuamotu, French Polynesia**: The newly renovated Tikehau by Pearl Resorts  
**Books to read while you're there**: *Entangled Life* by Merlin Sheldrake – A mystical, scientific search into mycorrhizal fungi, which form vast, interconnected networks in the soil, linking plants and trees to transfer nutrients and information, often referred to as the "wood wide web".  
[*tahititourisme.fr*](https://www.tahititourisme.fr/){: target="_blank"}

## 13\. Saint-Barthélemy
Rounding up our list of the most beautiful islands in the world is a tiny Caribbean island (25 square-kilometres) that isn't exactly deserted anymore, except when you find yourself at dawn on one of its sublime beaches (Saint-Jean, Lorient, Gouverneur, Colombier, etc.). Saint-Barthélémy is home to 65 restaurants and 12 hotels, sublime, uncluttered residences with infinity pools, and boutiques by the biggest luxury brands. The clientele is mainly American, followed by the French. This island, discovered by Christopher Columbus and formerly Swedish, is a dream come true for its sumptuous end-of-year festivities, the highlight of its summer season, welcoming a flurry of private jets and [XXL yachts](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/discover-7-superyachts-designed-by-famous-architects){: target="_blank"} to its marina in Gustavia. On the beach, there's surfing at Toiny, hiking trails and the natural pools of Grand Fond, but above all, the national sport of *idleness*, cocktail in hand. St. Barth has become the quintessential *socialite* island paradise, where people come to see and be seen. Beyond its bling lifestyle, Saint-Barth remains a mysterious island in the French West Indies that continues to fascinate, and rightly so, with its legends and pirates, ancient... and contemporary.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5d]

**Our favourite hotel in Saint-Barthélemy**: L'Eden Rock, with its feet in the water  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Adventures of Amina al-Sirafi* by Shannon Chakraborty – A modern tale of a retired pirate captain returning for one last adventure.  
[*saintbarth-tourisme.com*](http://www.saintbarth-tourisme.com/){: target="_blank"}

13 Most Beautiful Islands in the World To Visit – and the Books To Read While You’re There: **From the Maldives and Hawaii to Madagascar and Sir Bani Yas Island, discover the most beautiful islands in the world, places where rare wildlife, untamed landscapes, exceptional [hotels](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-hotels-in-the-world-2025-design-experts-share-their-favourite-stays){: target="_blank"} and pristine beaches converge to create visions of paradise.**

## 1\. Socotra, Yemen


[#image: /photos/699d910bf106a095cbec5304]

Floating in the Arabian Sea, closer to the Horn of Africa than [mainland Yemen](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/welcome-to-the-worlds-steepest-villages){: target="_blank"}, Socotra is often described as the most alien-looking island on Earth. Isolated for millions of years, it has evolved its own visual language: forests of umbrella-shaped Dragon’s Blood trees, limestone plateaus, and blindingly white dunes spilling into turquoise waters. Nearly a third of its plant life exists nowhere else, earning the archipelago UNESCO World Heritage status. Despite its otherworldly appearance, life on Socotra follows ancient rhythms, with small fishing villages, frankincense trees, and untouched beaches stretching for kilometres without interruption. It is not a place of resorts or spectacle, but of raw, elemental beauty – a reminder of what the natural world looked like before it was shaped by tourism.

**Our favourite hotel in Socotra:** Socotra Eco-Lodge, offering simple stays immersed in nature  
**Books to read while you're there:** _The Monk of Mokha_ by Dave Eggers – Follows a young Yemeni-American attempting to revive the Yemeni coffee industry amidst the escalating civil war, offering a portrait of resilience.  
[*welcometosocotra.com*](https://www.welcometosocotra.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 2\. Sir Bani Yas Island, United Arab Emirates


[#image: /photos/699d8f7af106a095cbec529e]

Off the coast of Abu Dhabi, Sir Bani Yas Island offers a rare proposition: a safari experience unfolding entirely on an island. Once the private nature reserve of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founding father of the UAE, the island today protects over 17,000 free-roaming animals, from Arabian oryx and gazelles to giraffes roaming freely against a backdrop of rugged mountains and coastal plains. Covering nearly half the island, the Arabian Wildlife Park sits alongside secluded beaches, mangroves, and archaeological remains, including the ruins of a 7th-century Christian monastery, offering a glimpse into the region’s layered past. Visitors can explore by 4x4, kayak through calm coastal waters, or simply retreat into the stillness of its desert-meets-sea landscape. Remote yet accessible, Sir Bani Yas represents a vision of conservation, hospitality, and heritage in balance.

**Our favourite hotel in Sir Bani Yas Island:** Anantara Desert Islands Resort, set between savannah and sea  
**Books to read while you're there:** *The Ring of the Dove* by Ibn Hazm – A classic treatise on the nature of love and human relationships.  
[*sirbaniyas.ae*](https://www.sirbaniyas.ae/){: target="_blank"}

## 3\. Madagascar


[#image: /photos/699d97dbf106a095cbec5414]

The fourth-largest island in the world, Madagascar drifts off the southeast coast of Africa like a forgotten continent, its landscapes shaped by millions of years of isolation. This separation has given rise to extraordinary biodiversity found nowhere else on Earth: forests of towering baobab trees, limestone pinnacles known as tsingy, and rare wildlife such as lemurs, chameleons and fossas. Along its coastline, deserted beaches meet warm turquoise waters, while inland, rainforests, canyons and highlands reveal constantly shifting terrain. Both raw and poetic, Madagascar offers a rare sense of discovery.

**Our favourite hotel in Madagascar:** Miavana by Time \+ Tide, set on a private island off the northeast coast  
**Books to read while you're there:** *The Aye-Aye and I* by Gerald Durrell – Documenting Durell's last expedition to this mystical country, The Aye-Aye treks the reader through Madagascar's glorious wildlife and the animals on the verge of extinction.    
[*madagascar-tourisme.com*](https://madagascar-tourisme.com/en/){: target="_blank"}

## 4\. Barbados
In the heart of the Caribbean, this coral island – the only one in the archipelago – blends exotic flavours with wild coastlines and 100 kilometres of beaches (over 80 beaches, all public). On land, you'll discover nature reserves – and its national mascot, the Green Verbena, a mischievous little monkey – sugar cane fields, coconut groves, refreshing waterfalls, as well as golf, polo and cricket, a direct legacy of the Commonwealth, from which the island gained independence in 2021. On the seaside, the wind conditions are perfect for surfing and kitesurfing, the reefs for diving, sea caves specific to its geology, beaches for watching turtles lay their eggs between April and November, and the flying fish offshore. In terms of typical Caribbean architecture, the sublime colonial residences in the [UNESCO-listed](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/new-unesco-world-heritage-site-southern-ohio-hopewell-ceremonial-earthworks){: target="_blank"} historic centre of the capital, Bridgetown, are a must-see, as are the famous coral stone villas along the coast designed by British theatre designer Oliver Messel and now a national heritage site.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def55]

**Our favourite hotel in Barbados**: the recently renovated Fairmont Royal Pavilion  
**Books to read while you're there**: *Even in paradise* by Elizabeth Nunez – An epic tale of family betrayal and manipulation couched in terrifically engaging prose and peopled with sketchy characters.  
[*visitbarbados.org*](http://visitbarbados.org/){: target="_blank"}

## 5\. Jamaica
Jamaica is the second Caribbean destination on AD's list of the most beautiful islands in the world, and for good reason. The island's north coast boasts some of the most stunning hidden beaches, including Frenchman's Cove – the legendary beach featured in the 1988 film *Cocktail* starring [Tom Cruise](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/where-does-tom-cruise-live-inside-the-mission-impossible-stars-real-estate-portfolio){: target="_blank"} – and *Seven Mile Beach*. Fine sand, turquoise water, palm trees: all the ingredients are there. But every stop between Montego Bay and Port Antonio is a delight to discover. Stroll through the spicy, multicoloured nugget markets, the local plantations, the fried, smoked or barbecued specialities on the street corner, always to the sound of music... On the postcard, an atoll, coral galore, crystal-clear water, face-to-face with manta rays, baby sharks and all manner of unknown exotic beasts, sushi dinners and fresh papaya for breakfast, a phantasmagorical spa with a view, total relaxation in front of sparkling lagoons. Irresistible. The Maldives is the terrestrial incarnation of a tropical paradise, which is developing ever more sustainable experiences to protect its flora and fauna: restoration of the coral reef, protection of sea turtles, meetings with marine biologists, etc.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5b]

**Our favourite hotel in The Maldives**: One & Only Reethi Ra  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Soul of an Octopus* by Sy Montgomery – A Surprising Exploration into the Wonder of Consciousness.  
[*visitmaldives.com*](https://visitmaldives.com/en){: target="_blank"}

## 11\. Zanzibar
Nicknamed the Spice Island, just 35 km east of the African coast, Zanzibar is a paradise-like interlude. Made up of three main islands (Unguja, Pemba, Mafia), a former sultanate and then British colony, the archipelago boasts an equatorial climate, influences from Africa, the [Arabian Peninsula](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/inspired-by-moroccan-palaces-this-home-in-mangaluru-india-reframes-islamic-art-traditions-for-a-new-generation){: target="_blank"} and [India](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-sprawling-safari-lodge-in-india-aramness-is-a-modern-day-sanctuary){: target="_blank"}, and is the cradle of Swahili culture. A kite spot and a national forest, the archipelago is a lush sanctuary inhabited by free-living red monkeys and blue vervet monkeys. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, its capital – the birthplace of Freddy Mercury – is a city steeped in history. Stroll through the spice market, its narrow streets with carved doors and its old fort. Discover the mangroves, the seaweed aquaculture traditionally managed by the women, the lagoons with their thousand shades of turquoise, everything has an exotic flavour in Zanzibar.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def53]

**Our favourite hotel in Zanzibar**: White Sand, Tanzania's only Relais & Châteaux  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Famished Road* by Ben Okri – Known for his magical realism, Okri (of Nigerian descent) became the youngest winner of the Booker Prize in 1991 for the novel.   
[*zanzibar.com*](https://www.zanzibar.com/){: target="_blank"}

## 12\. Tuamotu, French Polynesia
If there's a paradise far away from it all, it's French Polynesia. The 76 Tuamotu atolls are part of this timeless archipelago, 4,000 km – and many hours on a plane – from land. A journey well worth making... We could draw with our eyes closed its idyllic scenery, like those once painted by Gauguin. Coral islands stretching for almost 2000 km, the Tuamotu (*"distant islands"* in Tahitian) are famous for their treasures, exported to the other side of the world, such as *monoi* – made from tiare flowers and coconut oil – fresh fish, salt and the region's treasure, Tahitian black pearls. With your feet in the sand or in the crystal-clear water, levitating in [a villa on stilts](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/incredible-overwater-bungalow-resorts-to-book-around-the-world){: target="_blank"} or on a boat, but always far from the crowds, this is island living at its best in the heart of the Pacific Ocean. Absolute rest and total disconnection are almost mandatory in the face of these insolently beautiful lagoons and unreal coral rings.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5c]

**Our favourite hotel in Tuamotu, French Polynesia**: The newly renovated Tikehau by Pearl Resorts  
**Books to read while you're there**: *Entangled Life* by Merlin Sheldrake – A mystical, scientific search into mycorrhizal fungi, which form vast, interconnected networks in the soil, linking plants and trees to transfer nutrients and information, often referred to as the "wood wide web".  
[*tahititourisme.fr*](https://www.tahititourisme.fr/){: target="_blank"}

## 13\. Saint-Barthélemy
Rounding up our list of the most beautiful islands in the world is a tiny Caribbean island (25 square-kilometres) that isn't exactly deserted anymore, except when you find yourself at dawn on one of its sublime beaches (Saint-Jean, Lorient, Gouverneur, Colombier, etc.). Saint-Barthélémy is home to 65 restaurants and 12 hotels, sublime, uncluttered residences with infinity pools, and boutiques by the biggest luxury brands. The clientele is mainly American, followed by the French. This island, discovered by Christopher Columbus and formerly Swedish, is a dream come true for its sumptuous end-of-year festivities, the highlight of its summer season, welcoming a flurry of private jets and [XXL yachts](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/discover-7-superyachts-designed-by-famous-architects){: target="_blank"} to its marina in Gustavia. On the beach, there's surfing at Toiny, hiking trails and the natural pools of Grand Fond, but above all, the national sport of *idleness*, cocktail in hand. St. Barth has become the quintessential *socialite* island paradise, where people come to see and be seen. Beyond its bling lifestyle, Saint-Barth remains a mysterious island in the French West Indies that continues to fascinate, and rightly so, with its legends and pirates, ancient... and contemporary.



[#image: /photos/6842ff022201b76e6d4def5d]

**Our favourite hotel in Saint-Barthélemy**: L'Eden Rock, with its feet in the water  
**Books to read while you're there**: *The Adventures of Amina al-Sirafi* by Shannon Chakraborty – A modern tale of a retired pirate captain returning for one last adventure.  
[*saintbarth-tourisme.com*](http://www.saintbarth-tourisme.com/){: target="_blank"}

How A Small Home Office Can Still Be Charming, Even in a Tiny Apartment: A small home office could be the smartest [home makeover project](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/top-diy-trends-that-will-define-home-renovations-in-2025){: target="_blank"} you take on in 2025. Not only will it add to your home's potential resale value, it will make life so much better in the meanwhile with your work life not taking over your home life.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab570a6f2dfa698d480]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab66a663b2da95cc822]

+++

This [small apartment](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/small-apartment-ideas-this-tiny-studio-is-big-on-style){: target="_blank"} was completely redesigned by the interior design agency NOWW Studio. "The owners are young and have professions in the creative industry," confides the architects. So they appreciate ideas that defy convention, and they value their private lives as much as their professional lives: this apartment had to be like them."

### A tiny living room becomes a small home office
Here, a real bedroom has taken the place of the old kitchen, and only the small living room, which serves as an office, remains unchanged. It has, of course, been completely redesigned. Colour is everywhere in the apartment, and that's what makes this interior so interesting, both visually and graphically.

### A tip to remember
***Combine contemporary woodwork, retro inspiration and designer pieces. The large storage unit in burr wood veneer with its dark border replicates a vintage swivel desk chair produced by MIM, Mobili Italiani Moderni, in the 1960s.***



[#externallink: /externallinks/6503041abb917946d989f4c1]

***
## A dialogue of styles and eras


[#image: /photos/67b77ab670a6f2dfa698d481]

In Paris, this pied-à-terre is the work of Le Cann's interior designers. The interior is inspired by the owner's personality, his story - "a very elegant young man, very classical, Parisian but whose adopted country was Italy" – thus favouring cross-influences between Paris and Milan. After organising the layout around the sources of natural light provided by the bay windows and skydome, Le Cann sculpts the volumes with a rhythmic play of moldings and symmetries.

### Tips to remember
***Combine eras, materials, geometries and styles to create a unique charm. Here, on the brushed stainless steel desk, a Les Yeux Noirs sculpture in bone and black pearls, a Chandigarh hand in bronze, and Olivier Thomé's Absence, in gouache on cardboard. A wrought-iron chair completes the ensemble.***

***
## An abundance of storage space
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab770a6f2dfa698d482]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab770a6f2dfa698d483]

+++

The challenge of layout and optimisation was the compass for interior designer Alice Lacherez's project. "We're in Neuilly, so we had to be consistent with the local environment," she explains. We brought in some southern French codes, while preserving the building's 1970s style," she explains. Various species of wood are used in the furniture and decor, right down to the bamboo sconces, a nod to the Mediterranean.

### Tips to remember
***Incorporate clever, designer storage in the office area. Specially carved niches replace unused service doors. The multiplicity of storage units in no way detracts from the apartment's design. When done right you can make a [small space appear bigger](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/how-to-make-small-rooms-look-bigger){: target="_blank"}.***

***
## Separating spaces with a module
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67b77ab74e1507eef299189b]

[#image: /photos/67b77ab86a663b2da95cc823]

+++

To appreciate this small apartment's transformation, you have to understand what it was before. “You have to imagine yourself, in 24sqm, with old wallpaper, carpet on the floor, floral curtains...” From a dark, partitioned cube with a dubious mezzanine, the place has become a pleasant single apartment under the aegis of Aurélien Duny. For the architect, functionality means opening up the rooms. "All we kept of the existing space were the exposed ceiling beams. We knocked down the walls in favor of semi-high partitions that let light in everywhere."

### Tips to remember
***Separate spaces with a module that seems to float overhead, like a cloud with its rounded corners. We love its Mediterranean influences, reminiscent of the organic lines of a Menorcan finca.***

***
## A small office thanks to 100% made-to-measure furniture


[#image: /photos/67b77ab870a6f2dfa698d484]

When interior designers Elina and Diana Mussakulova were asked to design this small 55-square-meter apartment, they decided to make the most of its precious square meters. For this reason, many of the furnishings were custom-made, as in the bedroom, where the small desk fits in perfectly.

### A tip to remember
***Furniture designed entirely to measure – as in the case of the wardrobe with desk area and the bed.***

***
## An *"inhabited wall"* in plywood
+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/66cd641b6ec82447a1a7695f]

[#image: /photos/66cd641c0c92e7f11fddc0c0]

[#image: /photos/66cd641b0c92e7f11fddc0bf]

+++

"I found it very compartmentalised, with a small kitchen and storage units everywhere that made it hard to understand its high ceilings... in short, getting around this apartment was a journey full of pitfalls", says interior architect and designer Olivier Debin, referring to this bright 47 square-meter apartment. His intervention was therefore twofold: optimising the space and writing a unique story about the place.

### A tip to remember
***The large cabinet or "living wall". Plywood guarantees structural homogeneity and allows the interior designer to play with the effects of different levels, openings, solids, voids and heights between a work surface and a desk. The all-over birch veneer gives the impression of a cabin as much as a laboratory.***
