## 18\. A Cabinet of Wonders, A Celebration of Art in Nature
### Museo di Palazzo Grimani, Venezia, dal 15 dicembre 2024 all’11 maggio 2025


[#image: /photos/676d652df8c420a5fcc4b5e5]|||Museo di Palazzo Grimani, *A Cabinet Of Wonders*. Installation view. Ph. Matteo De Fina|||

I Musei Archeologici Nazionali di Venezia e della Laguna sono lieti di presentare *A Cabinet of Wonders: A Celebration of Art in Nature*, una inedita mostra della George Loudon Collection (Regno Unito), curata dal **Dr. Thierry Morel**. Ambientata nella splendida **sede di Palazzo Grimani** — uno straordinario esempio di architettura rinascimentale toscano-romana a Venezia — la mostra include anche la ricostruzione di una Wunderkammer del XVII secolo. Questo allestimento dà vita a un poetico dialogo tra la passione storica della famiglia Grimani per il collezionismo e la visione contemporanea di George Loudon. Prodotta dal Ministero della Cultura, dalla George Loudon Collection e da Venetian Heritage, la mostra si sviluppa nel \*\**piano nobile* \*\*di Palazzo Grimani dal 15 dicembre 2024 all’11 maggio 2025. I visitatori ne attraverseranno le magnifiche stanze decorate, tra cui la Sala di Psiche, il Camerino di Apollo, il Camerino di Callisto, la Cappella e il suo vestibolo, la sala da pranzo e la sala Neoclassica — in un percorso progettato per stimolare i sensi.

## 20\. Birgit Jürgenssen e Cinzia Ruggeri, Lonely Are All Bridges
### Fondazione ICA, Milano, dal 17 gennaio al 15 marzo 2025


[#image: /photos/676d63368029412d16f97655]|||Birgit Jürgenssen, Untitled (Improvisation) © Birgit Jürgenssen, Estate Birgit Jürgenssen Bildrecht Vienna, 2024; Courtesy Galerie Hubert Winter.|||

**Fondazione ICA Milano** inaugura la programmazione espositiva del 2025 con un progetto espositivo bipersonale, con la eccezionale curatela dell’artista **Maurizio Cattelan** e **Marta Papini**.  
La mostra si intitola ***Lonely Are All Bridges. Birgit Jürgenssen e Cinzia Ruggeri*** e sarà aperta al pubblico da **giovedì 16 gennaio** a **sabato 15 marzo 2025**. L’esposizione celebra il lavoro di due artiste iconiche, **Birgit Jürgenssen (Vienna, 1949 – 2003)** e **Cinzia Ruggeri (Milano, 1942 – 2019)**, mai incontratesi di persona, ma idealmente in dialogo attraverso le loro opere, visioni e riflessioni. Il **titolo** della mostra è tratto da un verso della poetessa austriaca Ingeborg Bachmann, *“lonely are all bridges”*, che sintetizza **lo spirito sperimentale di due artiste** il cui lavoro si spinge oltre le convenzioni, sfida i confini tra arti e costruisce ponti in grado di attraversare discipline differenti, trasformando il quotidiano in un racconto dalla forte dimensione critica. Nate alla fine degli anni Quaranta e formatesi negli anni Sessanta, entrambe hanno affrontato il ruolo della donna nella società, intrecciando tematiche legate al corpo, all’identità e alla trasformazione. La comune fascinazione per **l’ornamento e l’accessorio**, interpretati come un’estensione del corpo e strumento di conquista del proprio spazio, traccia il filo rosso di **un’esposizione che invita a riflettere sulla relazione tra estetica, politica e identità** che innerva tuttora il nostro orizzonte quotidiano. L’esposizione riunisce le ricerche di due figure fondamentali dell’arte contemporanea, interpretate per l’occasione dalla **visione curatoriale di Maurizio Cattelan e Marta Papini** e raccontate al pubblico con un’inedita chiave di lettura. La mostra è resa possibile dalla collaborazione dell’Estate di Birgit Jürgenssen, Vienna e dall’archivio Cinzia Ruggeri, Milano.

[#image: /photos/676d63a18029412d16f97659]|||Cinzia Ruggeri, Scarpe Scale, Courtesy Archivio Cinzia Ruggeri, Milano|||

## 21\. Daniel Buren

Non una ma ben tre sedi espositive  - **Palazzo Buontalenti, Palazzo de’ Rossi** e **Antico Palazzo dei Vescovi** \- per l’attesa, grande mostra che la città dedica a Daniel Buren. Con opere originali create per **Pistoia Musei**, un’ampia selezione di lavori e studi, la personale ripercorre la carriera del maestro, esplorando il suo legame con l’Italia. Altri lavori saranno installate sul territorio circostante. L’artista concettuale, pittore e scultore francese ha vinto numerosi premi, tra cui il Leone d’oro per il miglior padiglione alla [**Biennale di Venezia**](https://www.labiennale.org/it) (1986), il Premio internazionale per il miglior artista a Stoccarda (1991) e il prestigioso **Premium Imperiale** per la pittura a **Tokyo** nel 2007.

## 22\. Bruce Davidson/Zabriskie Point
### Spazio Antonioni, Ferrara, dal 14 dicembre 2024 al 4 maggio 2025
Un nuovo appuntamento allo Spazio Antonioni racconta l’incontro sul set del film **Zabriskie Point** del grande fotografo con uno dei padri della cinematografia moderna. Approdato negli **Stati Uniti** nel 1968 per ritrarre un paese che incarnava l’essenza del suo tempo, **Michelangelo Antonioni** sceglie **Bruce Davidson** come fotografo di scena. Ad accomunare il loro lavoro è la vocazione a scrutare nelle pieghe della realtà senza preconcetti, regalando all’osservatore uno sguardo meravigliato e al tempo stesso consapevole su un mondo denso di luci e ombre. Accanto ad Antonioni, che considera «il più grande regista con cui abbia mai collaborato», Davidson trova ispirazione per alcuni dei suoi scatti più memorabili.

## 23\. Thomas Schütte. Genealogies
### Punta della Dogana, Venezia, dal 6 aprile al 23 novembre 2025


[#image: /photos/676ac60d90930331da1fdee2]|||Thomas Schütte, Innocenti, 1994.|||

Il focus della personale è la circolazione dei motivi nei lavori dell’artista tedesco dagli anni Settanta a oggi. Esposte vedremo svariate sculture dalla collezione Pinault, opere su carta (con molti inediti), e prestiti dell’artista. Caricaturale, talvolta maltrattata, sempre commovente, la figura umana indagata da **Schütte**, centrale nel nucleo di opere della Pinault Collection, prende vita attraverso l’argilla, la cera, la ceramica, il vetro, l’acciaio o il bronzo, nelle figure intere o nelle teste di personaggi, sempre in relazione con il disegno e il ritratto in due dimensioni. Combinando violenza e ingegno, intimità e teatralità, serietà e umorismo, il singolare universo dell'artista lo ha consacrato come una delle figure di spicco dell'arte contemporanea.



[#image: /photos/676ac61ddd2b19bbdf24ecac]|||Thomas Schütte, Efficiency Men, 2005.|||

## 24\. Maurizio Cattelan
### Palazzo della Ragione, Bergamo, dall’8 febbraio all’8 gennaio 2026
La **GAMeC** porta a Bergamo **Maurizio Cattelan**, protagonista della prossima mostra nella storica sede estiva della Galleria d’Arte Moderna e Contemporanea della città. Eccezionalmente nel 2025, nell’ambito di **“Pensare come una montagna”**, il progetto espositivo del celebre artista italiano si estenderà oltre le mura del Palazzo grazie alla collaborazione con il Comune di Bergamo, portando l’artista italiano più noto in ambito internazionale, conosciuto per il suo approccio audace e innovativo, a confrontarsi con lo spazio pubblico. ## 25\. Chiara Dynys. Private Atlas
### Buildingbox, Milano, dal 15 gennaio 2025 al 6 gennaio 2026


[#image: /photos/677e5aa43950e551fb6a10a2]|||Chiara Dynys, *Sante subito* (2023).|||

12 installazioni in 12 mesi. Private Atlas è un progetto espositivo dal taglio antologico, articolato in dodici episodi e curato da **Alessandro Castiglioni**, una mostra personale diffusa nel tempo che ripercorre i trentacinque anni di ricerca e attività dell’artista. La mostra rappresenta un viaggio composto da una serie di appuntamenti che svelano e pongono l’attenzione su alcuni aspetti poetici dell’opera dell’artista, attraversando la sua biografia, le sue ossessioni e la sua ricerca. Concepita come un atlante privato, si articola in tre macro-capitoli (**La disseminazione della memoria**, **Attraversamenti** e **Viaggio in Italia**), che presentano - a cadenza mensile - opere storiche e nuove produzioni dell’artista con allestimenti site-specific pensati appositamente per lo spazio di **BUILDINGBOX**.

## 26\. Il ritratto dell’artista
### Museo civico di San Domenico, Forlì, dal 23 febbraio al 29 giugno 2025


[#image: /photos/676ac748dd2b19bbdf24ecae]|||Alberto Sironi, Autoritratto.|||

Un nuovo lungo viaggio tra capolavori, che ricostruisce al Museo di San Domenico la progressiva definizione della consapevolezza di sé dell’artista nella storia dell’arte. Il ritratto dell’artista è un autografo esistenziale. Segno, traccia, memoria, riflesso da tradurre in un’immagine definitiva, giocata nel tempo, contro il tempo, oltre il tempo. Nell’autoritratto il pittore si sdoppia nel duplice ruolo di modello e di artista. L’occhio si posa sull’immagine riflessa per ritrarsi e l’immagine ritratta è un alter da sé ed è un sé. Spesso ne viene fuori una maschera. Personaggio più che persona. Per molti artisti è così, dal Quattrocento al Novecento. Opere di **Giovanni Bellini, Tintoretto, Lavinia Fontana, Sofonisba Anguissola, Lotto, Pontormo, Parmigianino, Rembrandt, Tiziano, Hayez, Böcklin, De Chirico, Balla, Sironi, Bacon fino a Bill Viola** e **Chuck Close**.



[#image: /photos/676ac755f548545bd62ef88f]|||Tintoretto, Narciso alla fonte.|||

Le luxueux portefeuille immobilier de Kendrick Lamar: L’enfance de Kendrick Lamar à Compton, en [Californie](https://www.admagazine.fr/galerie/etonnante-maison-architecte-californie), a toujours exercé une forte influence sur son travail – il lui a même consacré un album conceptuel entier. Près de 14 ans après la sortie de son premier album studio, *Section.80*, il cumule des réalisations artistiques impressionnantes : un prix Pulitzer, 17 Grammy Awards, un Emmy, 37 Hip Hop Awards (un record), 11 MTV Video Music Awards, et une reconnaissance quasi unanime comme l’un des plus grands artistes de tous les temps. Malgré ce succès phénoménal, l’interprète de *« Not Like Us »* est resté, pour l’essentiel, fidèle à sa Californie natale. À une exception près, le [rappeur](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/bali-villa-musicien-personne-connait-visage) n’a investi que dans des propriétés situées dans son État d’origine.

*« Je suis le premier de ma famille à connaître ce type de succès, alors j’ai pris sur moi de le gérer avec sagesse, car je voulais que mes proches réussissent eux aussi »*, confiait Kendrick Lamar à *Vanity Fair* en 2018. Il n’est donc pas surprenant que le rappeur ait investi dans des propriétés pour sa famille, gardé le silence sur sa vie privée et pris son temps avant d’acquérir des biens immobiliers. Ce n’est que ces dernières années que l’artiste, longtemps fidèle au statut de locataire, a commencé à se tourner vers des résidences de plus en plus [luxueuses](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/monaco-luxueux-30m2-bois). Découvrez ce que nous savons du discret portefeuille immobilier de la star.

## La maison d’Eastvale
Le premier achat immobilier connu de Kendrick Lamar remonte à décembre 2013, lorsqu’il a acquis une [maison](https://www.admagazine.fr/video/watch/la-maison-californienne-de-jennifer-garner) d’environ 325 mètres carrés, comprenant quatre chambres et trois salles de bains, située à Eastvale, en Californie, à environ une heure de Los Angeles. Le rappeur a déboursé 510 000 euros pour cette propriété, à peu près à l’époque où il terminait son rôle d’ouvreur sur la tournée *« Yeezus »* de Kanye West.

Selon certains rapports, Kendrick Lamar a acheté cette maison pour y loger ses parents et ses frères et sœurs. *« J’aime acheter des propriétés, qu’il s’agisse d’appartements, d’immeubles ou de maisons »*, a-t-il confié lors d’une interview à la radio. *« Je vis toujours à [Los Angeles](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/batiments-incendies-los-angeles), dans un petit appartement. Mais j’aime investir mon argent dans des choses durables, plutôt que d’acheter des biens éphémères. Quelque chose qui peut durer toute une vie. »* L’artiste a vendu cette propriété en 2021 pour 800 650 euros.

## La propriété d’investissement à Calabasas
En novembre 2017, Kendrick Lamar a réalisé son prochain achat immobilier notable : une maison à Calabasas pour 2,58 millions d’euros. La résidence de 500 mètres carrés, construite en 1998, semble être une autre propriété d’investissement. Lorsque l’interprète de *« Humble »* a conclu l’achat de la maison, elle comptait six chambres, six salles de bains, une salle multimédia, plusieurs cheminées à bois, une vaste suite principale, ainsi qu’un jardin impressionnant doté d’une piscine aux lignes organiques, d’un espace barbecue et d’un salon ombragé offrant une vue sur le [canyon](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/mvrdv-canyon-san-francisco). Selon les archives, Kendrick Lamar reste propriétaire de cette maison, où certains membres de sa famille auraient résidé.

## Le manoir de Manhattan Beach
+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/67812eecb4b3bd8ed1cd5326]|||Kendrick Lamar (à droite) avec sa partenaire de longue date Whitney Alford en 2018.|||

+++

Kendrick Lamar a ensuite investi 9,4 millions d’euros dans un manoir contemporain à Manhattan Beach, en Californie. La transaction, conclue hors marché en 2019, coïncide avec la naissance de son premier enfant avec sa fiancée de longue date, Whitney Alford. Les informations sur la propriété restent limitées, mais elle s’étend sur 497 mètres carrés répartis sur trois étages, comprenant quatre chambres et sept salles de bains. Bien qu’elle ne soit pas située directement sur la plage, les étages supérieurs offrent une [vue sur l’océan](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/a-vendre-villa-basco-landaise-vue-ocean). Un garage pouvant accueillir trois voitures et un petit patio complètent cette résidence. À ce jour, le rappeur semble toujours en être le propriétaire.

## La maison de Bel Air
Quelques mois après la sortie de son album *Mr. Morale & the Big Steppers* en 2022, Kendrick Lamar a fait un nouvel investissement immobilier en achetant pour 15,4 millions d’euros une maison de style ranch midcentury à Bel Air, dont il est toujours propriétaire. Nichée sur un terrain d’environ 3 035 mètres carrés dans la section prisée d’East Gate, la propriété s’étend sur 743 mètres carrés et comprend également une maison d’amis à deux étages. Ensemble, les deux structures totalisent sept chambres et huit salles de bains.

Construite en 1951 par l’architecte [Edward Fickett](https://www.admagazine.fr/adadresses/galerie/8-airbnb-pour-une-escapade-parfaite-en-californie), la maison se distingue par ses plafonds voûtés et ses nombreux puits de lumière, ce qui permet à la lumière naturelle d’inonder l’intérieur. Parmi ses espaces remarquables figurent un salon lumineux agrémenté d’une cheminée en briques, une cuisine rénovée, une salle de sport et une salle de cinéma. À l’extérieur, un étang à carpes koï, une piscine et un patio aménagé pour les repas s’intègrent dans un cadre de verdure luxuriante.

## Le penthouse de New York
En 2023, le rappeur a marqué un tournant dans ses investissements immobiliers en réalisant son premier achat sur la côte Est. À l’automne, il a acquis un pied-à-terre à New York pour 8,3 millions d’euros. Ce [penthouse](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/penthouse-amsterdam-touche-francaise) en triplex se trouve dans le Pierhouse, un immeuble en copropriété conçu par l’architecte Jonathan Marvel en 2016 et idéalement situé en bord de l’eau dans le Brooklyn Bridge Park. Ce bâtiment de 10 étages est prisé des célébrités : Ed Sheeran, Amy Schumer et Matt Damon y ont également possédé des résidences.

Le penthouse de Kendrick Lamar s’étend sur 290 mètres carrés, avec 185 mètres carrés d’espace extérieur répartis entre un toit-terrasse paysagé et une terrasse au niveau inférieur. Composé de quatre chambres et trois salles de bains, l’espace offre amplement de place pour que le rappeur, sa compagne et leurs deux jeunes enfants puissent y séjourner confortablement lors de leurs passages à [New York](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/greenwich-village-maison-ville-theatrale). La hauteur des plafonds et les vues panoramiques du sol au plafond sur le fleuve et la ligne d’horizon de Manhattan en font un lieu de séjour remarquable.

## Le manoir de Brentwood
Le dernier achat immobilier de Kendrick Lamar est également le plus cher. En mai dernier, l’artiste a dépensé 41 millions d’euros pour une propriété à Brentwood, Los Angeles. Selon les registres, la propriété se compose de deux parcelles adjacentes : l’une abrite une ferme moderne de 1 500 mètres carrés, tandis que l’autre, plus petite, comprend une habitation de 315 mètres carrés, vraisemblablement utilisée comme maison d’hôtes. Le rappeur a acquis ces terrains voisins à l’ancien responsable juridique de Fox Corp., Viet Dinh, dans le cadre d’une transaction hors marché, ce qui laisse peu d’informations sur la demeure. Nous savons toutefois que lorsque Dinh a acheté la maison principale en 2019, elle comptait huit chambres et 15 salles de bains. Cet achat a permis au musicien de figurer sur notre liste des [10 transactions immobilières de célébrités les plus chères de 2024](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/transactions-celebrites-plus-cheres-2024). (Fait amusant : Gwyneth Paltrow possède une maison voisine, dont le prix est actuellement fixé à 24,3 millions d’euros.)

Article initialement publié dans [*AD* US](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/where-does-kendrick-lamar-live-unpacking-the-rappers-real-estate-portfolio){: target="_blank"}.

Moody Masculine Interiors Set the Scene at This Egyptian Architect’s Contemporary Cairo Penthouse: **[Dramatic, yet effortlessly liveable](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/comedian-mohanad-alhattab-serene-villa-in-dubai-brings-a-moody-masculine-style-to-home-decor){: target="_blank"}, this contemporary Cairo penthouse is a masterful composition of opposites: of light and dark, of moody, masculine interiors and earthy accents.**

For architects, designing their own homes represents the purest distillation of their design philosophy. It’s an opportunity and a challenge: a chance to experiment boldly without client feedback or market considerations, while creating a personal sanctuary. For Fathy Ibrahim, founder and lead architect of Egypt architecture studio Insignia Design Group, that challenge called for deliberate theatricality.



[#image: /photos/681586f1aa67175ec681b514]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6815870e883cad4e07ddc045]

[#image: /photos/68158712883cad4e07ddc047]

+++

It’s a quality you can feel immediately upon entering his contemporary Cairo penthouse in the Sodic Villette compound where a dramatic entrance – clad entirely in textured black tiles and a mirrored ceiling – create an infinity-like effect. It’s a bold statement that sets the tone for what lies beyond.



[#image: /photos/6815871b883cad4e07ddc049]

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/681586fbaa67175ec681b516]

+++

“When I first stepped into the empty apartment, I saw endless possibilities,” recalls Ibrahim. “The lack of finishing meant I could design freely, without having to be controlled by existing layouts or styles.” This blank canvas offered Ibrahim, an architect accustomed to bringing others’ visions to life, the rare opportunity to explore his own design vocabulary without compromise.

Unlike typical Egyptian residences that often favour bright, open entrances, Ibrahim’s choice of a moody, theatrical threshold creates a compelling transition. “The entrance serves as a powerful statement,” he explains. “The dark tiles on the walls create a sophisticated backdrop that contrasts beautifully with the warm tones of the interiors.”

This interplay between shadow and light continues throughout the 240-square-meter residence. Moving from the dramatic entrance, the space opens up into an airy reception area where Spanish marble, rich walnut accents, and a carefully calibrated palette of earth tones create a warm, sophisticated atmosphere. The living area seamlessly connects to an open kitchen, establishing the heart of this Cairo penthouse as a space designed for connection and hospitality.



[#image: /photos/681586f0883cad4e07ddc041]

“Among all the spaces in my home, the reception area and the open [kitchen hold a special place](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/cool-kitchen-ideas-by-2024-ad100-designers){: target="_blank"} in my heart,” Ibrahim shares. “This space beautifully embodies the spirit of connection and hospitality, making it the heart of my home.” At every point, Ibrahim’s architectural background is evident in his meticulous approach to materiality, showcasing a harmonious dialogue between contrasting textures: slick marble surfaces play against plush fabrics, while reflective elements balance the solidity of natural stone.



[#image: /photos/681587170195b572a3822b1e]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/681586f1274ac810407c6094]

[#image: /photos/681587070195b572a3822b18]

+++

“The decision to use black Spanish marble was inspired by its elegance and timeless appeal,” he explains. For the TV wall, Ibrahim selected a unique marble that serves as a focal point, while Patagonia marble (renowned for its vibrant patterns) frames a sculptural piece in the living area.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/681586fd05d531b698f5e110]

[#image: /photos/681586fc0b0ec64e6da3f35b]

+++

## Must Read: [This Moscow Home With Its Own Hammam Is the Ultimate Wellness Flex](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-moscow-home-with-its-own-hammam-is-the-ultimate-wellness-flex){: target="_blank"}
In the main bathroom, antique brown porcelain and open-book marble create a refined, spa-like retreat. The marble’s dramatic veining has been carefully matched to form a mirrored pattern that evokes the feel of a contemporary artwork, elevating the everyday into an experience of visual pleasure. This attention to detail continues throughout the penthouse, where a careful balance is struck between open, communal areas and more intimate, personal spaces. While the reception and kitchen are designed for gathering and connection, the bedrooms retreat into quieter atmospheres.



[#image: /photos/68158715a541756600abbd7a]

“The principal bedroom is a true sanctuary, embodying my unique style through the use of black colours, marble, mirrors, and wood accents,” Ibrahim says. Here, he continues a nuanced approach to masculine interiors, with dark tones creating [a sophisticated, cocoon-like atmosphere](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-nature-inspired-interiors-palette-cashmere-blanket-effect-for-minimal-apartment-in-lisbon){: target="_blank"}. A custom headboard made of black stone serves as a dramatic focal point, while wood-clad walls introduce warmth and texture.

## Must Read: [Masculine Bedroom Design Anchors This Art-Filled New York Loft](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/masculine-bedroom-design-ideas-neal-thomas){: target="_blank"}


[#image: /photos/681586ffa541756600abbd78]

In contrast, the guest room presents a lighter aspect of Ibrahim’s design sensibility. “The guest room is designed with modern light colours that promote a sense of freshness and tranquillity,” he explains, “making it a perfect space for visitors to unwind.”

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68158715381020cde4ad3910]

[#image: /photos/6815871b274ac810407c6096]

+++

Perhaps the most subtle yet impactful element of this contemporary penthouse in Cairo is its lighting design. Ibrahim has used indirect illumination to sculpt the atmosphere of each space, particularly through the strategic placement of strip lights that highlight architectural details. “In the reception area, I used LED strips along the ceiling to draw attention to the unique design,” he explains. “This indirect light creates a soft, diffused glow that highlights the elegance of the design without being overpowering.”

##  Milan Diary: [The 13 Best New Lighting Designs from Euroluce 2025 You Need To Know](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-13-best-new-lighting-designs-from-euroluce-2025-you-need-to-know){: target="_blank"}

Hygge’s Out, *Lagom* Is In – but What’s This New Nordic Style Designers Are Swearing By?: ***Lagom* is latest Nordic design philosophy we are loving right now. Think of it as Danish h*ygge*'s more sophisticated Swedish cousin.**

The quiet, calm, and nature-inspired Danish concept of *hygge* (pronounced “hoo-ga”) has influenced home interiors for decades, fostering a serene and tranquil environment that [cocoons dwellers in wellness](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/therme-dubai-everything-we-know-about-the-dollar545-million-wellness-resort){: target="_blank"} and [peacefulness](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/why-forest-bathing-is-the-hottest-wellness-trend-right-now){: target="_blank"}. While this style remains relevant and widely practised, a complementary design concept, *lagom*, has been charming designers worldwide. Coined by the Swedes, *lagom* is all about simplicity, serenity, and function, all in perfect equilibrium. More than just an aesthetic, it is a philosophy aimed at making both your interiors and your life more manageable.

Instead of focusing on instant pleasures, *lagom* encourages finding long-term happiness in one's surroundings by incorporating natural materials and high-quality objects that stand the test of time, [creating an enduring sense of well-being](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/this-wellness-resort-in-spain-promises-the-ultimate-off-grid-experience){: target="_blank"}.

**We asked *lagom*\-practising experts to shed more light on the concept and share tips on how to incorporate this style into your interiors.**

## **What is *Lagom*?**
“*Lagom*, the Swedish philosophy of ‘just the right amount’, is about balance, moderation, and practicality,” shares Anam Taufik of Mumbai-based [AT Studio](https://www.instagram.com/anam.taufik.studio/?hl=en). “It’s less about indulgence and more about sustainability and finding contentment in simplicity. It’s about designing a home that breathes and allows you to do the same. There's something deeply calming about that balance; it feels effortless and functional, yet beautiful.”



[#image: /photos/67e3a3c5f33433895bbfe2c2]



[#externallink: /externallinks/650303f6bb917946d989f4c0]

At its core, the concept revolves around curating a minimalist home where every piece serves a purpose and complements the others. Simplicity – whether in materials, textures, or tones – lays the foundation of *lagom*. Every element works in synergy, helping to create a unified, cohesive interior that doesn’t “pop” or create “drama.”



[#image: /photos/67e3a3c5863b71850ba39cb4]

“Many ancient cultures across the globe have long preached the notion that simplicity leads to inner peace and contentment – that the human mind needs quiet to [focus on what is truly meaningful](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/how-energy-mapping-in-feng-shui-could-be-your-answer-to-a-prosperous-home-heres-how-to-do-it-properly){: target="_blank"} and joyful,” says Jonas Bjerre-Poulsen, partner at Copenhagen-based [Norm Architects](https://normcph.com/). “Over thousands of years, the steady progression from modest village life to populous and hierarchical civilisations has increased the value of the material, arguably at the expense of the spiritual. We’ve been taught to prioritise temporary pleasures and acquisitions repeatedly until our lives are anything but simple. This path has led to a de-prioritisation of basic, immaterial human needs – such as a connection to others, a sensory relationship with nature, a feeling of security as well as autonomy, and clarity about our role within the wider community.”



[#image: /photos/67e3a3d2f33433895bbfe2c8]

At its essence, *lagom* brings back the age-old concept of simple living – an approach to life and a state of being, without being overly frugal or bland. Its main pillars are sustainability, quality, and balance.

## **How is *lagom* different from *hygge*?**
The Danish and Swedish concepts share several commonalities, as both aim to create relaxed, calming homes that celebrate craftsmanship and natural materials. However, at a more technical level, *hygge* is about creating a cosy, comfortable home, whereas *lagom* is about achieving a more balanced and manageable space – sometimes even veering towards minimalism. And this latter concept is now being embraced more by designers as it not only helps create more long-lasting spaces but also positively influences the way you live.

“*Lagom* and *hygge* are both beautiful concepts, and I think the world is embracing them because they feel like a breath of fresh air in this chaotic, fast-paced lifestyle we all live today,” says Taufik. “*Lagom* is about balance and moderation – having what you need to live a harmonious life without excess. *Hygge*, on the other hand, is about the feeling of cosiness and warmth, sometimes even leaning into indulgence. It’s more emotional, while *lagom* feels practical and understated.”

“Hygge is more abstract in an interior application, in my opinion,” says Pernille Lind of London-based [Pernille Lind Studio](https://www.pernillelindstudio.co/). “*Lagom* supports the Danish aesthetic of minimalism very well, but I believe it has another layer to it – such as textiles, objects, and minimalist colour schemes.”



[#image: /photos/67e3a3e7863b71850ba39cb8]

## **How to achieve a *lagom*\-style interior**
**The true aim of *lagom* is to strike a balance between materiality, colour, and patterns, ensuring a cohesive and serene connection between elements within each room and throughout the home.** “Carefully consider this balance, and that means sometimes intentionally removing certain details or elements to avoid clutter and over-design,” says Lind. “The concept embodies a sense of restraint and the judicious selection of what is essential, ensuring a harmonious balance.”

## **Key elements of *lagom*:**
### 
### **Balanced colour and material scheme**
**A unified colour palette of soft, earthy tones and tactile materials that evoke nature is essential to achieving the *lagom* look**. The design scheme focuses on the perfect ‘Goldilocks’ balance – not too much, not too little. Colours should flow seamlessly from one room to the next, fostering a sense of equilibrium. Neutral tones such as cream, beige, brown, taupe, and light grey work best. *Lagom* also favours noble materials like wood, stone, organic cotton, linen, and rattan – timeless choices that do not seek attention but endure for years.



[#image: /photos/67e3a3cff33433895bbfe2c4]

### **Functional craftsmanship**
“Achieving simple yet sensuous spaces requires a sincere devotion to craftsmanship and the ambition to work with high-quality materials and detailing. Every joint, connection, and detail should be intentional and finely tuned – each contributing to a larger whole that engages us on both an emotional and practical level,” adds Bjerre-Poulsen. Beauty for beauty’s sake is not what *lagom* stands for. Every furniture piece or decorative element should serve a purpose and earn its place in the home.



[#image: /photos/67e3a3d1f33433895bbfe2c7]

### **Clutter-free interiors**
A truly calm and restorative interior cannot be achieved if it is cluttered. *Lagom* advocates against overloading your home with decorative elements. To maintain the perfect balance, you should aim to have as few items as possible. Bring in only what is essential, and your home will naturally [remain calm and clutter-free](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/how-to-organise-your-home-to-be-in-control-of-your-life-according-to-the-experts){: target="_blank"}. Aside from the bare essentials, invest in a select few pieces that bring you joy, stand the test of time, and are easy to maintain.



[#image: /photos/67e3a3ce863b71850ba39cb6]

Inside Prince William and Kate Middleton's $22 million new home, Forest Lodge: After months of speculation, Prince William and Kate Middleton are finally on the move, but they're not venturing too far from their current home, Adelaide Cottage: the Prince and Princess of Wales's new home, Forest Lodge, is also in Windsor, and many have already billed it as the young royal family's “forever home.”

William, Kate and their children have faced a series of challenges since they moved into Adelaide Cottage three years ago: Queen Elizabeth II's death soon after their settled in, Catherine's cancer treatment and King Charles' cancer diagnosis. Earlier this year there were reports about a security breach following a break-in on the estate grounds while the Waleses were in residence. Given the circumstances, it is easy to understand the family's desire for a change of scene and like it is for any family, William and Kate's new home will mark [a fresh start](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cpwyk1v0p8yo){: target="_blank"}. Here is everything we know about Forest Lodge.

## Where is William and Kate’s new home?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa732]

Nestled in a private and calm corner of Windsor Great Park, Forest lodge is just four miles from William and Kate's current home, Adelaide Cottage. Built in the 1770s, it is a Georgian mansion with eight bedrooms, a massive upgrade for the couple. It is also a practical choice for the hands-on couple who are often seen accompanying their children on the school run: Prince George, Princess Charlotte and Prince Louis attend Lambrook School which remains just a short drive away.



[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa733]

## Who designed Forest Lodge?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa734]

Surrounded by woodlands in the 4,800-acre Windsor Great Park, Forest Lodge was designed by Humphry Repton. Its red-brick facade conceals a half-barrel vaulted entrance hall and all the trimmings of a grand Georgian-era mansion: ornate plasterwork and cornicing, marble fireplaces and venetian windows that overlook the verdant setting. Respectfully enlarged and restored over the years, it was acquired by the Crown in 1829 and since March 1972 has enjoyed Grade II-listed status.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa735]

[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa736]

+++

## How big is William and Kate’s new home?
Compared to the four-bedroom Adelaide Cottage, William and Kate Middleton's new home, Forest Lodge, has eight bedrooms. It features six chimneys and nine bay windows. And while their new home has the space, as is the case with their [family set-up](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-family-home-in-goa-where-a-courtyard-and-100-year-old-columns-add-to-its-charm-studio-415/){: target="_blank"} at Adelaide Cottage, William and Kate will have no live-in staff.

## How much is Forest Lodge worth today?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa737]

After its last renovation in 2001, which costed approximately $2 million, the mansion was valued at $7.45 million. If Forest Lodge were to come on the market today, it would be worth approximately $22 million.



[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa738]

## Who lived at Forest Lodge before William and Kate?
Notable former occupants of Forest Lodge include Sir Malcom Murray, Sir William Fremantle and Sir John Aird. In 1998, you could rent it for $82,000 a year. After it was announced that the Prince and Princess of Wales were relocating to Forest Lodge, news circulated that two families who lived in cottages nearby were asked to vacate. To offer context, these families rented the cottages (once Forest Lodge's stables) from the Crown Estate and have been offered similar accommodations elsewhere in Windsor Great Park.

## Who will pay the rent for William and Kate’s new home, Forest Lodge?
William and Kate will pay the rent for Forest Lodge personally, meaning, they will not be using public funds. Any minor [renovations](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ad-revisits-old-school-homes-that-remind-us-of-our-nani-ka-ghar/){: target="_blank"} will also be paid for by the couple.

## Why are William and Kate moving to Forest Lodge?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa739]

A chance for a new start following a stressful few years, William and Kate's new home is also a strategic move. After the family has settled into life in Windsor, Forest Lodge is being billed as their forever home: a place where Prince George, Princess Charlotte and Prince Louis will grow up, and as importantly, where they may [continue to live](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cpwyk1v0p8yo){: target="_blank"} when the Prince and Princess of Wales become King and Queen. In that respect, Forest Lodge is regal enough without being ostentatious, a line William and Kate toe so effortlessly.

While [Buckingham Palace](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/gallery/the-worlds-most-expensive-home-the-buckingham-palace/){: target="_blank"} has remained the British monarch's formal home for generations, in her final years Queen Elizabeth II primarily resided at Windsor Castle. King Charles continues to maintain residence at Clarence House, where he lived with Camilla before he acceded to the throne in September 2022. Currently undergoing phased restoration, Buckingham Palace is used for formal events, receptions and other business of the state. Prince William and Kate Middleton's move to Forest Lodge is an indication that they plan to continue this new tradition.

## When will William and Kate move into their new home?
It is expected that William and Kate will move their family into Forest Lodge before Christmas this year.

Timeless by Design: The Rise of Ōpaal Interiors: Across the Middle East, design is undergoing a shift, from statement-making glamour to interiors shaped by authenticity, calm and emotional depth. Increasingly, clients are seeking spaces that mirror their identity, elevate their daily rituals and honour a more responsible way of living.



[#image: /photos/693286ca73ea71f36e4c1d6f]|||Residential project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

Leading this evolution is Ōpaal Interiors, an Emirati design studio that has steadily become one of the region’s most thoughtful design success voices. As it approaches its 10-year anniversary in 2026, Ōpaal’s influence can be felt in some of the region’s most refined homes and hospitality projects, where restraint take precedence.

“Not all design makes noise. Some simply stay with you,” says Ōpaal Interiors CEO & Founding partner Noor Al Muhaideb. This sentiment forms the backbone of the studio’s creative ethos: creating interiors that balance serenity with strength, warmth with precision, and beauty with meaning.



[#image: /photos/69244ee2335d173ca68f81cf]|||Ōpaal Interiors CEO & Founding partner Noor Al Muhaideb|||

What began as Al Muhaideb’s bold vision has evolved into a practice with a global footprint, spanning the Middle East, Europe and other international markets. Its portfolio ranges from expansive private residences to boutique hospitality escapes, as well as commercial and office environments. Projects include Dubai’s Mandarin Oriental Jumeira and the Kempinski Sky Garden Rafal in Saudi Arabia.

The studio’s growing influence has been recognised by the industry’s foremost institutions, with accolades including Interior Design Firm of the Year at the Design Middle East Awards and honours from *Arabian Business* for innovation and leadership.

Ōpaal’s philosophy is anchored by three pillars: longevity, sustainability and personalisation. Instead of chasing the momentum of trends, Ōpaal has committed to permanence. Its interiors blend classical structure with contemporary nuance, resulting in spaces that maintain their relevance over decades.



[#image: /photos/6932843a897ab921547a90bc]|||Residential project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

Sustainability, too, has long formed part of Ōpaal’s design DNA. Long before environmental responsibility became a regional mandate, the studio embraced locally sourced materials, conscious detailing, and earth-sensitive strategies. For Ōpaal, ecological responsibility does not constrain luxury, it refines it.

Perhaps the practice’s most defining quality is its bespoke rigour. Each commission begins with a conversation that uncovers a client’s aspirations, rhythms and relationship to space. The result is interiors that are deeply individual. No two projects are alike, yet all share a language of calm, considered refinement.

Behind every space is a multidisciplinary team of architects, artisans, materials specialists and sustainability experts, united under Al Muhaideb’s direction. A shared commitment to materiality and intention lends Ōpaal spaces a quiet power. These are interiors that feel grounded from the moment they are completed.



[#image: /photos/693286c158519187a495834f]|||Hospitality project by Ōpaal Interiors|||

As it plans to enter its second decade, Ōpaal Interiors continues to help chart a more nuanced definition of luxury in the Middle East. For Al Muhaideb, the future of design lies in authenticity, craftsmanship and consciousness, principles that will guide the studio as it expands its creative horizons.

Gemmyo dévoile son nouveau flagship parisien, un écrin taillé sur mesure: ## Gemmyo dévoile son nouveau flagship parisien, un écrin taillé sur mesure
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/687616da6b5c1e3cee25db04]|||La devanture de la boutique, rue de Seine.|||

[#image: /photos/687616d0112d2e593436316b]|||La devanture de la boutique, rue de Seine.|||

+++

Inspiré des jardins du Luxembourg, la boutique parisienne nichée à Saint-Germain-des-Prés a été pensée comme une orangerie bucolique. Fondée en 2011 par Pauline Laigneau et son mari Charif Debs, la maison s’est imposée en quelques années comme l’étendard d’une joaillerie contemporaine, accessible et singulière. Derrière une façade aux lignes haussmanniennes, la boutique se transforme en [salon](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/guide-salon-assises-adopter) intime où les clients prennent le temps, échangent et essayent les pièces de joaillerie, dans une scénographie chaleureuse et feutrée. Découverte de l’écrin sur mesure.

### **Une adresse pensée comme un hôtel particulier à Saint-Germain-des-Prés**


[#image: /photos/687613fa823b84357852ec67]|||L'entrée aux allures de jardin parisien.|||

Après le [rénovation](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/espaces-abraxas-rehabilites-travaux-millions-euros) de la boutique rue Courcelles, Gemmyo dévoile un nouvel espace de 134 mètres carrés dans le quartier historique du VIe arrondissement de Paris. Pour l’occasion, la maison a de nouveau fait appel au duo d’architectes Stephan Bidoux et Julien Villeneuve pour repenser l’espace comme un lieu décontracté tout en faisant référence à l’art, la culture et les personnalités qui ont marqué l’histoire de ce lieu. « *De Juliette Gréco à [Serge Gainsbourg](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/maisons-jane-birkin-serge-gainsbourg), en passant par Simone de Beauvoir et Jean-Paul Sartre, la boutique se nourrit de l’héritage culturel du quartier* » rappelle la maison.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/687614076b5c1e3cee25db00]

[#image: /photos/68761407de8bfefa7c5689fc]

+++

À l’intérieur, le raffinement discret du lieu dialogue avec une inspiration végétale assumée. Sol en terrazzo moucheté, arches arrondies, marbre, verre de Murano, teintes douces oscillant entre vert sauge, nude et ivoire… Chaque élément contribue à créer une atmosphère apaisante, presque résidentielle. Un mobilier sur mesure en bois blond et laiton brossé côtoie des pièces design dans une harmonie subtile entre tradition et modernité. Un clin d'œil élégant à l’esprit des orangeries du XVIIIe siècle, réinterprété ici dans une grammaire contemporaine.

### **Un lieu d’échange et de création**
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6876140a16f4664f402b43c5]

[#image: /photos/687614016b5c1e3cee25dafe]

+++

Plus qu’un simple point de vente, le nouveau flagship parisien de Gemmyo se veut un lieu d’expérience. Dans l’arrière-boutique, une alcôve confidentielle accueille les rendez-vous personnalisés et les créations sur mesure. Un espace feutré, pensé comme un boudoir joaillier, où l’on prend le temps d’imaginer la pièce parfaite, alliance, bague de fiançailles ou bijou unique, accompagné par les experts de la maison. Une parenthèse précieuse, à l’image de la relation que Gemmyo entretient avec sa clientèle : personnalisée, chaleureuse et libre. Cette volonté de réenchanter l’expérience joaillière se retrouve dans chaque détail. À mi-chemin entre la galerie, le salon privé et l’atelier contemporain, la boutique brouille les frontières entre les univers. Des objets d’art curatés par Amélie Maison d’Art ponctuent le parcours, tandis que la lumière naturelle, filtrée par les ouvertures façon atelier, baigne l’ensemble d’une clarté douce et enveloppante.



[#image: /photos/6876141b9a4b6cfec6672d52]|||Boudoir privé pour les essayages de bijoux, avec aux murs les oeuvres de chez Amélie Maison d'Art.|||

En plein cœur de Saint-Germain-des-Prés, le 74 rue de Seine devient bien plus qu’une simple adresse : un lieu de vie, de transmission et de création, à l’image d’une maison joaillière qui réinvente avec finesse les codes du genre.

Hermès and artist Nigel Peake look to cartography for the maison’s latest porcelain service: What does music have to do with a 33-piece kaolin white porcelain table service? Everything, according to Charlotte Macaux Perelman and Alexis Fabry, artistic directors of Hermès Maison. Under their direction, Irish artist [Nigel Peake](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/why-you-should-visit-homo-faber-in-venice-before-september-ends/) created the art for the new collection of plates, trays, tart platters, soup spoons, soy bowls and so much more.

“It’s just how I like to draw—with music,” says Peake. “It’s not intentional... I also collect old vinyl records. Before it became cool,” the artist quips at the launch of the collection in Paris this January. The creative trio, who worked together on the A Walk in the Garden collection in 2018, reunited three years ago to work on Hermès En Contrepoint, the new porcelain service from the maison. Initially inspired by cartography, the project took a surprising turn when Peake began sketching in the margins of the maritime maps. Borders of colourful ink drawings that framed the maps like a meticulous doodle became the highlight. “The borders around the maps were fascinating—they represented scale, rhythm and tempo,” explains Macaux Perelman. These playful margins, an improvisation at first, became the heart of the collection.



[#image: /photos/67c6e1d25a2f131a9e98a342]|||
*Scenography from the launch event at The Conservatoire National des Arts et Métiers, a former abbey in Paris’ third arrondissement (photo: Maxime Verret).*
|||

“This collection elevates Nigel’s work as a [colourist](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/meet-malene-bach-the-colourist-behind-many-interesting-architectural-projects-in-india/),” explains Fabry. “There is something very precise and regular in the way he works. It is a pattern and yet he has managed to imbue it with some kind of poetry.” Light and airy, each plate, platter, tray and tea cup is unique with friezes of circles, squares and diamonds in hues of pink, violet, blue, green and orange. During the launch, a choreographed dance showcased how each plate would find its match in a different pairing—where pink merges with green and orange with blue, a colour wheel with limitless combinations. “It’s like a game, or a puzzle,” says Macaux Perelman. “The pieces come together in different permutations, much like an orchestra where every note has its place.”



[#image: /photos/67c6e1d91e1722289637b52c]|||
*Scenography from the launch event at The Conservatoire National des Arts et Métiers, a former abbey in Paris’ third arrondissement (photo: Maxime Verret).*
|||

Even with the precision, the human touch is ever-present. “Despite it being made to the perfect Hermès standards, there are variabilities that show you it is artisanal work,” explains Fabry. The interplay of hands, as they work through the pieces in the collection, creates an intimacy that feels timeless. “I love the idea that a granny would cherish this, and so would someone younger. Objects like these outlive us,” says Peake.

The launch of the collection at the National Conservatory of Arts and Crafts in Paris—an evocative space with an old-world charm—was a deliberate choice to emphasise its universal appeal. “It can feel at home anywhere,” Macaux Perelman affirms. Perhaps that is its most enduring charm—an invitation to cherish a zesty accessory at mealtimes for now and for time to come.

The Creators of Craft Design Challenge for Emerging Creatives: Vogue Arabia joins forces with IQOS and the Italian design house Seletti to introduce Creators of Craft, a new design challenge created to spotlight emerging regional talent and foster innovation in contemporary object design. The initiative brings together two distinct creative perspectives united by an interest in how design shapes everyday life.

IQOS develops its products with an emphasis on usability and how design shapes everyday interactions. This approach also extends to its creative collaborations, including a recent project with Kuwaiti artist Abrar Zenkawi for the IQOS ILUMA i launch. She customised the device with Sadu-inspired patterns and sea motifs that capture design in Kuwait through a contemporary lens. Projects like this allow the brand to explore regional perspectives within its wider design language.

Seletti has long stood apart in the design landscape for its ability to turn the ordinary into something expressive. Established in 1964, the brand has built an extensive catalogue that includes lighting, tableware and furniture defined by concept driven thinking. Its products often reference pop culture and industrial design, resulting in objects that are functional and imaginative. By challenging conventional ideas of form, Seletti encourages designers to see familiar materials from a fresh angle.

**About the challenge**

Creators of Craft unites the perspectives of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti in a regional design call. The challenge invites emerging creatives to design a modern accessory for IQOS that draws on the region’s craft heritage through a contemporary lens. The brief asks participants to present an original idea that mirrors the values of innovation and artistic integrity. This opportunity underscores the commitment of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti to nurturing talent and contributing to the design dialogue of the Middle East.

**How to participate**

Residents of the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Lebanon, and Egypt are required to submit a sketch or digital render of their design alongside a short explanation of the idea. Entries will be evaluated by a jury comprising Manuel Arnaut, Head of Editorial Content at Vogue Arabia; Yann Marois, Vice President of Brand Design and Creative at Philip Morris International; Stefano Seletti, Founder and Creative Director of Seletti; and Nadine Kanso, Founder and Creative Director of Bil Arabi. Up to five finalists will receive an IQOS-funded budget to build a prototype for Jury review before the Creators of Craft event in Dubai (17–19 April 2026), where one winner will be chosen.

**The Award**

The winner will be revealed at the April 2026 Creators of Craft event in Dubai and will earn a commissioned IQOS design project, an all-expenses-paid trip to Milan Design Week, and an exclusive Vogue Arabia print feature. They will also receive access to masterclasses led by Nadine Kanso and Stefano Seletti.

*Apply today before the deadline of 9 January 2026. To participate, click [here](https://www.voguearabia.com/sponsored/article/creatorsofcraft).*

*IQOS is a brand of smoke-free product that is not risk-free and provides nicotine, which is addictive.*

Liaigre takes over a gallery in Mumbai for a one-of-a-kind immersive showcase: At first glance, France and India might seem like two disparate countries that have little in common with each other. Yet, French design house Liaigre’s first showcase in Mumbai reveals a shared appreciation for refined craftsmanship. This exhibition transforms the gallery into a typical Liaigre home, allowing all those who enter to truly experience what it means to be a part of the brand’s ethos. “We wanted to display a mix of historical pieces, iconic pieces, and recent pieces,” explains Christophe Caillaud, the CEO of Liaigre. “This display creates an environment that is very close to what our inspiration for a space in India would look like.”

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68dbc7b9ae8bc385db05c81b]|||*The Galet bookcase with movable parts.*|||

[#image: /photos/68dbc48606dacd007b238db4]|||*The structure of the ochre ⁠Basse Terre II Sofa was intended to be cubical yet comfortable.*|||

+++

*Also read: [Christian Liaigre’s first home in India is a contemporary take on the traditional bungalow](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/magazine-story/influences-from-mughal-architecture-and-indian-palaces-meld-inside-this-charming-delhi-home/#s-cust0){: target="_blank"}*

Indeed, that is one of the core tenets of Liaigre as a brand— to fit the context of the space they are in, and work with clients to create something that is fit for their home. Some of the highlights of the show include the Galet bookcase—which means pebble in French. The Galet is a stunning library piece that has moving ‘pebbles’ fitted between the shelves, adding a playful element to the design. Right next to the library is a signature lounge chair that is not on display in Liaigre showrooms anymore. This historic piece is the Latin daybed, and displays exquisite workmanship with its double stitching.



[#image: /photos/68dbc389af7ca2bdf1d6b5db]|||*The living room set up features the Sienne floor lamp in the far left corner, the iconic wooden Nagato stool in front of the mirror, and the bronze Citron lamp adorns the side table near the Augustin sofa. The composition is grounded by the Saint-Germain armchairs in the foreground.*|||

“Of course, we could not display Liaigre without showing some of our pieces in bronze,” shares Caillaud, talking about the iconic Saint-Germain armchairs designed by Christian Liaigre himself. The structure is cast in bronze, and has a certain sculptural quality that is reminiscent of artist Alberto Giacometti’s work. Another iconic piece that forms the living area of the exhibition is the Augustin sofa. This is also one of Christian Liaigre’s own designs, and is the first of its kind to introduce low, casual seating. The structure of it is still very formal, yet the seating allows for easy comfort and conversations.

*Also read: [Christian Liaigre’s interiors for Vertigo up the ante for superyacht style](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/content/christian-liaigres-interiors-for-vertigo-up-the-ante-for-superyacht-style/){: target="_blank"}*

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68dbc916ae8bc385db05c81f]|||*The dining room features a mix of formal and informal seating.*|||

[#image: /photos/68dbca60ae8bc385db05c821]|||*This outdoor section features the darker iteration of the Naja table created by Eric Schmitt for Liaigre.*|||

+++

As we move to the dining area of the exhibition, the brand’s philosophy of comfort, craftsmanship, and contrast coalesces. A large formal [dining table](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/dining-table-vastu-8-major-red-flags-to-watch-out-for/){: target="_blank"} is illuminated by signature lighting above, yet the seating that surrounds it is casual— a mix of benches and chairs. Here too the contrast comes out, with the textured wooden backrest of the chairs complimenting the smooth leather seating of the bench.

The most striking throughline that connects Liaigre to India is a deep reverence for craftsmanship. Each piece is crafted to perfection, with many subtle details in texture and material shining through upon closer inspection. Many Liaigre collections are made in collaboration with seminal artists like Eric Schmitt, Karen Swami and others.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68dbca611d135afd3bfd79ad]|||*The outdoor section of the showcase is anchored by the Craie table by Karen Swami. It features a stoneware base and an oakwood top.*|||

[#image: /photos/68dbcaf21d135afd3bfd79af]|||*This section of the showcase has a more relaxed and casual atmosphere.*|||

+++

“I think our way of working with interior architecture and furniture is very compatible with the quality of the architecture that we have found in India,” Caillaud explains, “we are excited to continue this dialogue between our craftsmanship and [Indian craftsmanship](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/7-standout-pieces-from-ads-craftsmanship-issue-that-celebrate-the-art-of-handmade-design/){: target="_blank"}.” Liaigre is eager to meet the Indian design community, whether that means taking temporary showcases such as this to other cities, or opening a permanent showroom in Mumbai itself, the possibilities are endless. “At the end of the day, we don't want to be the biggest company, but we do want to be the closest to our clients,” concludes Christophe Caillaud.

*The showcase is on display at Æquo in Mumbai until the 20th of October.*

For the London Jeweller Glenn Spiro, Creating Future Heirlooms Is a Family Affair: **In a city brimming with world-class ateliers, London Jeweller Glenn Spiro has set itself apart with its rare, never-to-be-repeated creations fit for present-day kings and queens.**

Many [buildings in London’s Mayfair](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/what-makes-mandarin-oriental-mayfair-londons-most-luxurious-new-hotel){: target="_blank"} have a hidden history, often hinted at by the blue plaques on their façades that detail previous notable tenants, from the Regency dandy Beau Brummell to Jimi Hendrix. At 26 Bruton Street, built in the 19th century, there’s one for the late royal couturier Sir Norman Hartnell. A lot of the interior detail upstairs is just as it was when Hartnell made Queen Elizabeth II’s coronation dress here in 1953, sewing it with a fantasia of gold bugle beads, diamanté and pearls.



[#image: /photos/678a78c8be9d9e6e580cbb55]|||The mirror-clad interiors of the Mayfair atelier.|||

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/678e27629a39aece015264dd]|||Baoulé gold, antique citrine and diamond earrings.|||

+++

Today, the workshop is the inner sanctum of the father-and-son team behind rarefied high jewellery atelier G by [Glenn Spiro](https://glennspiro.com/){: target="_blank"}. Glenn, the patriarch, launched this address ten years ago (the jeweller has been in the game for over four decades), and was joined by his son Joe in 2017. There’s no street-level shop front, and all business is by appointment only. “We only make about 150 pieces of jewellery a year,” says Joe, standing in front of a display case housing a dazzling pair of Sunrise orange sapphire circular earrings. “Look at these,” he says, taking one out to show me. “Our business is all about finding the most special gems. We couldn’t repeat these. We bought the stones because of how they look in the dark, not in the light – it’s about their colour saturation and luminescence. This kind of jewellery is worn at night.”



[#image: /photos/678a78c1be9d9e6e580cbb52]|||Baoulé gold and diamond earrings.|||

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/678a78ccbe9d9e6e580cbb59]|||Carved wood and gold bracelet with ‘no oil’ Colombian emerald.|||

[#image: /photos/678a78cfb1a541780de915a3]|||Cognac diamond Brioche earrings.|||

+++

+++inset-left

[#image: /photos/678a7a82b1a541780de915a5]|||Gold and diamond Bullet ring.|||

+++

We are buzzed through one of numerous security doors into the office Joe shares with his father, which is furnished with remarkable objects, including a set of 1970s wood-and-leather [Africa dining chairs by Afra and Tobia Scarpa for Maxalto](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/toura-dollar58-millon-belgravia-townhouse-london-with-extraordinary-art-design-collection-by-brigitta-spinocchia-freund){: target="_blank"}. Several green-velvet boxes are piled on a large table, containing pieces from the Spiros’ 2024 Materials of the Old World collection, shown recently at [PAD in London](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/pad-london-2024-how-vikram-goyal-collection-reframed-the-narrative-around-made-in-india){: target="_blank"}, and representing something of a new direction for them. The collection uses eclectic elements, from super-rare vintage citrine diamonds to Mesopotamian carnelian beads, Egyptian ceramics that date back 3,000 years, and individual elements repurposed from 1950s pieces by Cartier. It’s a new way of working for the London jeweller, but also an extension of what the Spiros have always done – they are the Indiana Jones and son of the jewellery world, always on the hunt for the best diamonds in existence, holding on to them until design inspiration strikes. “We buy addictively,” says Joe. “And as my dad always says, the gemstones dictate the design.”



[#image: /photos/678a78c7b1a541780de915a1]|||Diamond Angelus earrings.|||

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/678a7b3ab1a541780de915a7]|||Carved wood and gold ring with ‘no oil’ Colombian emerald.|||

+++

The Spiros are artists as much as jewellers. Their work is bought by a fraction of the one per cent, and now resides in institutions alongside historic masterpieces: the astounding Papillon ring, created in the shape of a butterfly with delicate, fluttering titanium wings bejewelled with green tsavorites and diamonds, ended up in the V&A’s permanent collection after being donated by its owner Beyoncé.



[#image: /photos/678a78c4b1a541780de9159f]|||Cognac diamond earrings.|||

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/678a78cdbe9d9e6e580cbb5b]|||Gold and silver earrings with pink sapphires and diamonds.|||

[#image: /photos/678a78c3b1a541780de9159e]|||Earrings from ‘Materials Of The Old World’ set with a rare collection of amber beads and diamonds.|||

+++

+++inset-left

[#image: /photos/678a7b8fb1a541780de915a9]|||Baoulé gold, vintage citrine and diamond pendant.|||

+++

There’s not really a house style at Spiro. Some of the duo’s pieces look ancient or tribal; some look futuristic, with diamonds in amorphous ceramic settings that can’t be resized. “With those, it’s a Cinderella moment for someone if they like it and it fits,” says Glenn. “It feels like fate.” I ask what the wildest thing they’ve executed is, and Glenn replies immediately. “A set of huge Nigerian buttons with ivory, coral and diamonds everywhere, on a big chain.” Joe quickly adds: “I’d say it was the five-carat black-diamond nipple rings we were commissioned to do. I love that they were for a woman who is super-fun and wild, and felt like she can do whatever she wants.”  
I ask them if they consider their work avant-garde. Glenn says not. “That’s an easy excuse to make something that’s not timeless,” he says. “We make pieces that cost hundreds of thousands of dollars, but I’m also inspired by costume jewellery from the 1960s through to the 1980s. I always want to find the depth in something. I loved what Paul Flato did between the 1920s and 1940s – it was big, unapologetically funky jewellery.”



[#image: /photos/678a78c9be9d9e6e580cbb56]|||Sculptural vintage furniture dots Glen Spiro's Mayfair atelier.|||

The atelier on Bruton Street couldn’t be a more fitting space for the Spiros. English Heritage was active in ensuring the 1930s mirror-lined walls and glass chimneypiece were given protected status, so they remain in situ. You can feel the history as you sit on any of the sofas in the atelier, surrounded by sculptures in diamonds, inhaling [air perfumed by Diptyque’s Figuier-scented candles](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-scented-candles-for-christmas-2024-to-gift-or-light){: target="_blank"}. There’s some personal history here, too. “When we took the place over, it had been sort of forgotten about,” says Joe. “It was being used as a storeroom for Juicy Couture. But my father had been here a long time before; while working in a junior role as a teenager at the start of his career, at the Cartier-owned English Art Works, he accompanied a client to a Norman Hartnell show.”



[#image: /photos/678a78cfbe9d9e6e580cbb5c]|||The Mayfair atelier's reception room, with all its period interior details faithfully preserved.|||

Now Hartell’s one-time home and studio is the Spiros’ HQ, and their work is adding to the history of the building. “We are old school,” says Glenn. “Most of the big brands aren’t run by jewellers anymore. We make what we want, and only sell directly to people who want it.” Like the late monarch’s shimmering, iconic gown, the one-offs created by the Spiros take on a life of their own when they leave their vault. “We make everything out of passion,” says Joe. “It’s a strange feeling when pieces sell, because then we go back to the drawing board to start something new.” Glenn, who likes to reference the glam of Halston and disco as much as antique design principles, puts his own spin on it. “We finish something, and it sits there in a box until a beautiful woman puts it on her neck and brings it to life. And that’s why we do it.”

*This feature first appeared in [the latest issue of AD Carat](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/ad-carat-discover-exceptional-watches-and-jewellery-that-are-future-heirlooms){: target="_blank"}, AD Middle East's watches and jewellery special.*

7 *AD*\-approved sofa designs that will still feel fresh in 2026: In a design [landscape](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/10-architects-and-designers-in-surat-reimagining-the-built-landscape-with-purpose/){: target="_blank"} obsessed with what’s new, the most modern move is restraint. These timeless sofa designs endure because they prioritise proportion, texture, and tactility over trend—proof that true modernity never rushes.

## The Mid-Century Modern Couch: An Icon Reimagined


[#image: /photos/68cbb361915b28381cd498f4]|||*Pro-Tip: Add one curve nearby, a round coffee table or arc floor lamp balances the sofa’s linear geometry, as Studio Espaazo does in this Ahmedabad home. Keep the legs visible—exposed teak or metal legs create visual air and maintain that signature mid-century lightness.*|||

Born in post-war America, the [mid-century](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-multi-generational-villa-by-muselab-gives-the-motifs-of-kutch-a-mid-century-makeover/){: target="_blank"} modern sofa embodied a new kind of optimism: light, democratic, and human-scaled, when renowned designers such as Charles and Ray Eames, Florence Knoll, and George Nelson replaced heavy, carved furniture with clean horizontals, organic curves, and slender wooden legs. Eight decades on, its appeal endures because it balances form and function with quiet confidence: compact proportions which fit urban apartments, honest materials like teak, wool, or linen which age gracefully, and a geometry that harmonises with almost any style—from Scandinavian calm to Indian tropical modernism.

## The Sectional Sofa: For Stylish Ease


[#image: /photos/6863a2fe01e928c8f7030977]|||*Pro-Tip: As Concepto Studio does in this Bengaluru home, position the sectional away from walls; it looks sculptural and frames open-plan zones beautifully.*|||



[#image: /photos/6908518fe0d04a711165639b]|||*Pro-Tip: Rather than bulky end tables, opt for nested surfaces or built-in side ledges to complement the piece. Keep the side and centre tables low height, as seen in art collector Karan Bhagat’s Mumbai home—originally designed as a show flat by Rajiv Saini and reimagined by architect Siddharth Garimella.*|||

The sectional sofa has evolved from its suburban origins into a symbol of intelligent design—adaptable, sculptural, and unapologetically generous. Conceived in the mid-20th century to suit open layouts and shifting lifestyles, its genius lies in its modularity: each segment can transform a space, from an [intimate](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-designers-home-in-juhu-is-an-intimate-portrait-of-heritage-and-crafted-modernity-interior-designer-tania-shah/){: target="_blank"} reading corner to a sprawling social zone. Contemporary designers treat it as architecture rather than furniture—anchoring living areas, defining circulation, and softening modern geometries. With low profiles, tonal upholstery, and deep, sink-in seats, the sectional embodies relaxed luxury that doesn’t age. It’s as much a place to gather as it is to pause.

*Also read: [5 best sofa colours to breathe life into a neutral home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-best-sofa-colours-to-breathe-life-into-a-neutral-home/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Modular Marvel: Where Form Meets Flexibility


[#image: /photos/683f428af160cc375552caa9]|||*Pro tip: With modular sofas, try to keep the palette monochrome to maintain visual calm. Let its sculptural form, not colour, create rhythm across the room.*|||

The modular sofa can be described as even a philosophy ofmodern living, beyond its life as furniture, and shares roots with the sectional sofa. Born in the late 1900s and popularised by icons like Mario Bellini’s *Camaleonda*, it challenged the static idea of a traditional couch by letting users compose their own configurations. Today, its appeal endures because it reflects how we actually inhabit our homes fluidly. Each unit can shift from intimate nook to social centrepiece with ease, embodying a kind of democratic design that feels both playful and poised. With soft edges, comfortable upholstery, and sculptural volume, the modular sofa is a versatile and [artistic](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/anish-kapoors-monte-santangelo-turns-everyday-commute-into-an-artistic-experience/){: target="_blank"} shape-shifter.

## The Bouclé Sofa: Elevating Texture


[#image: /photos/680f60f3d7037b1705713a37]|||*Pro-tip: Maintain the tones of the bouclé, as the texture works best in neutral, ivory or oat shades, as seen in this home by AT Studio. Pair it with tonal contrasts —dark wood, marble, or brushed metal—to ground its softness and keep the room from feeling too ethereal.*|||

The instantly recognizable bouclé sofa designs have achieved cult status for a reason—they turn simplicity into seduction. Once favoured by mid-century modernists for its soft tactility, *bouclé* (French for “looped yarn”) resurfaced in contemporary interiors as the textile of quiet luxury. Its tactile, cloud-like texture diffuses light, softens architectural lines, and invites touch—an antidote to the hard-edged [minimalism](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/in-this-5100-square-foot-bengaluru-penthouse-minimalism-gets-a-mediterranean-twist-pomtini-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} of recent decades. Designers now favour it not for novelty, but for its ability to balance sculptural form with comfort, making even the most minimal space feel warm and inhabited. Its versatility allows it to effortlessly find its place in all designs, from a pared-back Japandi apartment to a richly layered art deco interior. Whether curved, modular, or perched on slender legs, the bouclé sofa embodies modern, fluid comfort.

## The Reimagined Chesterfield: Tradition With A Twist


[#image: /photos/686e2c0bebca66233ec8ed90]|||*Pro-tip: A matte or suede finish modernises the traditional high-gloss look instantly, as seen in the library lounge of Goa’s Verve Estate, by Paul Rodrigues.*|||



[#image: /photos/65d44e14f3a9d1555ccb3647]|||*Pro-tip: Let the Chesterfield breathe—keep it off rugs with heavy patterns, and pair it with visually minimal pieces (such as the marble centre piece in this Chennai home by Shreya Krishnan Design Office) to offset its stateliness.*|||

The Chesterfield sofa has travelled centuries—from British drawing rooms to modern lofts—without losing its quiet command. Born in 18th-century England and once synonymous with men’s clubs and cigar smoke, it has since been redefined by designers who replaced excess with elegance. Today’s Chesterfields shed their ornate scrolls for slimmer arms, matte leather, or even linen and velvet, achieving heritage without heaviness. What makes it timeless is its architecture: deep tufting that adds texture, symmetry that calms the eye, and a sense of permanence rare in fast-turn design. It anchors contemporary spaces with [history](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/bombay-deco-is-the-ultimate-chronicle-of-mumbais-art-deco-history/){: target="_blank"}, bringing gravitas to the lightest rooms and restraint to the grandest ones.

*Also read: [Poliform's Owen collection blends couture finesse with modern Italian craftsmanship](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/poliforms-owen-collection-blends-couture-finesse-with-modern-italian-craftsmanship/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Built-In Sofa: Seating As Architecture


[#image: /photos/68c28ba871e6a8c79d088741]|||*Pro-tip: Layer fixed seating with loose cushions and textiles in varied tones; the contrast of softness against masonry keeps it human, not austere. You can also experiment with other couch style pairings to add variety, as seen in this Mumbai home by RAD co \+ lab.*|||



[#image: /photos/67a9c0a9a0d263d60280eda7]|||*Pro-tip: You can get creative with your seating. In this Mumbai home by STaND Design, the deck flooring of the balcony extends as seating into the living room, creating an enticing play of levels.*|||

The built-in sofa blurs the line between furniture and structure—part of the room, not merely placed in it. Emerging from Mediterranean and [Japanese](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-2000-square-foot-home-in-bandra-blends-indian-and-japanese-design-sensibilities-pill-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} traditions where space was both precious and poetic, it has become a hallmark of architectural minimalism today. Cast in lime plaster, stone, or tadelakt, it offers permanence in an age of the temporary. Its beauty lies in proportion and restraint: seamless planes that invite lounging without visual noise. Built-ins work best when continuity is key—extending from plinth to wall to window in a single gesture. They turn corners into contemplative nooks, steps into seating, and architecture into atmosphere.

## The Low-Slung Lounge: Grounding Grace


[#image: /photos/67cfe1a75f709ec2a12b9048]|||*Pro-tip: Orient the low-slung sofa towards windows or courtyards—the low height extends the landscape. The surrounding furniture should be low and the lighting diffused as well, to allow the sofa to fully express its horizontal emphasis, as seen in this Bengaluru home by Ashni Kumar.*|||

The low-slung sofa captures the easy elegance of spaces that breathe—homes where air, light, and pause carry as much weight as design. Drawing from Japanese tatami culture and India’s own tradition of floor seating, it sits closer to the ground, promoting a slower rhythm and visual calm. Its elongated proportions and deep cushions create an atmosphere of ease, while wide arms and open bases invite lounging that feels instinctive rather than staged. Designers love it for how it grounds a space—anchoring large [living rooms](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/9-living-room-decor-ideas-thatll-make-this-space-the-highlight-of-your-home/){: target="_blank"} without interrupting sightlines or scale.

In conclusion, across all silhouettes, the most timeless sofa isn’t defined by style but by spatial awareness and sensitivity—to proportion, texture, and light. Keep upholstery tactile, colour palettes grounded, and silhouettes human in scale. Let materials breathe, allow light to move, and balance every softness with structure. Above all, invest in craftsmanship and comfort; trends fade, but good design—like a well-made sofa—only gets better with time.
