The modular sofa can be described as even a philosophy ofmodern living, beyond its life as furniture, and shares roots with the sectional sofa. Born in the late 1900s and popularised by icons like Mario Bellini’s *Camaleonda*, it challenged the static idea of a traditional couch by letting users compose their own configurations. Today, its appeal endures because it reflects how we actually inhabit our homes fluidly. Each unit can shift from intimate nook to social centrepiece with ease, embodying a kind of democratic design that feels both playful and poised. With soft edges, comfortable upholstery, and sculptural volume, the modular sofa is a versatile and [artistic](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/anish-kapoors-monte-santangelo-turns-everyday-commute-into-an-artistic-experience/){: target="_blank"} shape-shifter.

## The Bouclé Sofa: Elevating Texture


[#image: /photos/680f60f3d7037b1705713a37]|||*Pro-tip: Maintain the tones of the bouclé, as the texture works best in neutral, ivory or oat shades, as seen in this home by AT Studio. Pair it with tonal contrasts —dark wood, marble, or brushed metal—to ground its softness and keep the room from feeling too ethereal.*|||

The instantly recognizable bouclé sofa designs have achieved cult status for a reason—they turn simplicity into seduction. Once favoured by mid-century modernists for its soft tactility, *bouclé* (French for “looped yarn”) resurfaced in contemporary interiors as the textile of quiet luxury. Its tactile, cloud-like texture diffuses light, softens architectural lines, and invites touch—an antidote to the hard-edged [minimalism](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/in-this-5100-square-foot-bengaluru-penthouse-minimalism-gets-a-mediterranean-twist-pomtini-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} of recent decades. Designers now favour it not for novelty, but for its ability to balance sculptural form with comfort, making even the most minimal space feel warm and inhabited. Its versatility allows it to effortlessly find its place in all designs, from a pared-back Japandi apartment to a richly layered art deco interior. Whether curved, modular, or perched on slender legs, the bouclé sofa embodies modern, fluid comfort.

## The Reimagined Chesterfield: Tradition With A Twist


[#image: /photos/686e2c0bebca66233ec8ed90]|||*Pro-tip: A matte or suede finish modernises the traditional high-gloss look instantly, as seen in the library lounge of Goa’s Verve Estate, by Paul Rodrigues.*|||



[#image: /photos/65d44e14f3a9d1555ccb3647]|||*Pro-tip: Let the Chesterfield breathe—keep it off rugs with heavy patterns, and pair it with visually minimal pieces (such as the marble centre piece in this Chennai home by Shreya Krishnan Design Office) to offset its stateliness.*|||

The Chesterfield sofa has travelled centuries—from British drawing rooms to modern lofts—without losing its quiet command. Born in 18th-century England and once synonymous with men’s clubs and cigar smoke, it has since been redefined by designers who replaced excess with elegance. Today’s Chesterfields shed their ornate scrolls for slimmer arms, matte leather, or even linen and velvet, achieving heritage without heaviness. What makes it timeless is its architecture: deep tufting that adds texture, symmetry that calms the eye, and a sense of permanence rare in fast-turn design. It anchors contemporary spaces with [history](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/bombay-deco-is-the-ultimate-chronicle-of-mumbais-art-deco-history/){: target="_blank"}, bringing gravitas to the lightest rooms and restraint to the grandest ones.

*Also read: [Poliform's Owen collection blends couture finesse with modern Italian craftsmanship](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/poliforms-owen-collection-blends-couture-finesse-with-modern-italian-craftsmanship/){: target="_blank"}*

## The Built-In Sofa: Seating As Architecture


[#image: /photos/68c28ba871e6a8c79d088741]|||*Pro-tip: Layer fixed seating with loose cushions and textiles in varied tones; the contrast of softness against masonry keeps it human, not austere. You can also experiment with other couch style pairings to add variety, as seen in this Mumbai home by RAD co \+ lab.*|||



[#image: /photos/67a9c0a9a0d263d60280eda7]|||*Pro-tip: You can get creative with your seating. In this Mumbai home by STaND Design, the deck flooring of the balcony extends as seating into the living room, creating an enticing play of levels.*|||

The built-in sofa blurs the line between furniture and structure—part of the room, not merely placed in it. Emerging from Mediterranean and [Japanese](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-2000-square-foot-home-in-bandra-blends-indian-and-japanese-design-sensibilities-pill-design-studio/){: target="_blank"} traditions where space was both precious and poetic, it has become a hallmark of architectural minimalism today. Cast in lime plaster, stone, or tadelakt, it offers permanence in an age of the temporary. Its beauty lies in proportion and restraint: seamless planes that invite lounging without visual noise. Built-ins work best when continuity is key—extending from plinth to wall to window in a single gesture. They turn corners into contemplative nooks, steps into seating, and architecture into atmosphere.

## The Low-Slung Lounge: Grounding Grace


[#image: /photos/67cfe1a75f709ec2a12b9048]|||*Pro-tip: Orient the low-slung sofa towards windows or courtyards—the low height extends the landscape. The surrounding furniture should be low and the lighting diffused as well, to allow the sofa to fully express its horizontal emphasis, as seen in this Bengaluru home by Ashni Kumar.*|||

The low-slung sofa captures the easy elegance of spaces that breathe—homes where air, light, and pause carry as much weight as design. Drawing from Japanese tatami culture and India’s own tradition of floor seating, it sits closer to the ground, promoting a slower rhythm and visual calm. Its elongated proportions and deep cushions create an atmosphere of ease, while wide arms and open bases invite lounging that feels instinctive rather than staged. Designers love it for how it grounds a space—anchoring large [living rooms](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/9-living-room-decor-ideas-thatll-make-this-space-the-highlight-of-your-home/){: target="_blank"} without interrupting sightlines or scale.

In conclusion, across all silhouettes, the most timeless sofa isn’t defined by style but by spatial awareness and sensitivity—to proportion, texture, and light. Keep upholstery tactile, colour palettes grounded, and silhouettes human in scale. Let materials breathe, allow light to move, and balance every softness with structure. Above all, invest in craftsmanship and comfort; trends fade, but good design—like a well-made sofa—only gets better with time.

A Timeless Kitchen Design Sits at the Heart of This Renovated 19th-Century Stone House: Boasting a beautifully [timeless kitchen design](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/5-timeless-kitchen-design-ideas), Stone Crop in the rolling green countryside near Elora, Ontario, is a rare example of early [settler architecture](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/colonial-architecture-everything-you-need-to-know) that has been carefully updated. Built in 1833 by Thomas Dow, a Scottish mason who helped shape the town’s earliest buildings, the property has recently been renovated for a young couple working in tech who relocated to the area from the city to raise a family.



[#image: /photos/681f8bce9fbf11ed3f5f7277]

“This project was about finding a balance between respecting the building’s history and making it practical for everyday life,” says interior designer [Keri MacLellan](https://www.west-grove.co/about), founder of [Westgrove](https://www.west-grove.co/), the studio that led the renovation. “We wanted to keep what made the house special while opening it up and bringing in more light and flow.”



[#image: /photos/681f8bf4d0e469ea6da51cc7]

MacLellan chose to celebrate the bones of the building rather than mask them. “We exposed all the original stonework throughout the existing structure,” she says. “There’s a richness to those textures that tells the story of the house better than any object could.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c193f60d2e393b1cda1]

One of the more complex challenges lay in the connection between the original home and a newer extension, added in the early 2000s. “The [original stone walls](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/mallorcan-house-by-rock-and-villa-is-a-lesson-in-refined-mediterranean-luxury) are nearly two feet thick,” says MacLellan. “Rather than try to disguise that transition, we decided to expose it.” The team cut a dramatic passageway through the stone, creating an 18-inch-deep threshold that now forms a striking portal between the old cottage and the contemporary living spaces beyond. “It’s like stepping through time,” she adds.



[#image: /photos/681f8c329fbf11ed3f5f7279]



[#image: /photos/681f8c44d0e469ea6da51cc9]

The interiors reflect the refined sensibility of its owners – both passionate [collectors of ceramics](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/gallery/see-how-stunning-ceramics-can-transform-a-space), design and art. “They wanted something calm and grounded but not too minimal,” explains the designer. [Natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/sustainable-house-mallorca-ernest-bordoy) anchor the palette. Take, for example, the timeless kitchen design: pale plaster walls, oiled white oak, woven cane, and slubby linens complement the texture of the exposed historic stone walls.



[#image: /photos/681f8c59d0e469ea6da51ccb]

To add colour and visual interest, the couple commissioned a series of artworks from local painter [Alex Moore-Gibson](https://www.alexmooregibsonart.com/). Her layered canvases enliven the otherwise neutral interiors while remaining in keeping with their spirit. “We wanted the art to feel like part of the home, not something layered on top,” MacLellan notes.



[#image: /photos/681f8c76d0e469ea6da51ccd]

Bespoke ceramic lighting – pendants, sconces and mobiles – created in collaboration with [Sara Wood](https://www.sarawoodbyhand.com/), a local designer known for her organic forms and earthy glazes, is also peppered throughout the cottage. “The client is an accomplished ceramicist herself, so this partnership was particularly meaningful,” says the designer. “The ceramics added softness and helped tie everything together.”



[#image: /photos/681f8c919fbf11ed3f5f727b]

Built-in cabinetry was designed to [maximise storage](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/10-clever-kitchen-storage-ideas-to-maximise-your-space) without overwhelming the rooms. Underpinning the timeless kitchen design, a cane-fronted island blends traditional references with a clean, contemporary shape. “The idea was to use [natural materials](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-modern-cote-dazur-home-lays-down-the-blueprint-for-chic-indoor-outdoor-living) and simple forms that would last,” MacLellan explains. “Nothing here is about trends.”



[#image: /photos/681f8cb83f60d2e393b1cda3]

The renovation was completed in six months and, despite the challenges of working within the constraints of a heritage home, the result feels perfectly coherent. Original features like architraves and stone walls remain untouched, but they now sit alongside new interventions that add light and ease of use. “The owners were really happy with how the original and new elements came together,” says MacLellan, ““There’s a sense that this house has history but it’s also ready for the future.”

7 tendances de revêtement pour la cuisine en 2025: La cuisine reste le cœur de la maison en 2025, reflétant, notamment par son revêtement, des tendances axées sur l’authenticité et le bien-être. L’aménagement évolue avec des designs uniques, intégrant des finitions audacieuses et un mobilier remarquable. Cette transformation fait de la cuisine un espace où cuisiner, partager, socialiser, et prend ainsi une dimension toute particulière. **Porcelanosa** propose une multitude d'inspirations modernes pour sublimer votre intérieur, en mêlant esthétisme et fonctionnalité. Ces sept tendances de revêtement vous aideront à repenser votre cuisine avec des éléments décoratifs et pratiques, en phase avec les aspirations actuelles, pour un espace chaleureux et personnalisé.

+++button-group

[En savoir plus](https://www.porcelanosa.com/fr/ "En savoir plus"){: target="_blank"}

+++

## 1\. Le revêtement en all-over


[#image: /photos/67b5f4d6d82ae62fac99dfdc]|||Le revêtement *Taranto Marfil* de Porcelanosa, effet marbre.|||

En 2025, le revêtement se fait pluriel : sur les murs, les sols, il pare une cuisine dans son entièreté, donnant une impression d'unité et d'harmonie agréables à l'œil. Grâce aux technologies contemporaines, les revêtements combinent les fonctions. Plus résistants, plus versatiles, sans transiger sur le design, ils deviennent, comme le béton ciré ou la peinture, envisageables (et recommandés \!) en all-over dans la cuisine. *« Cette esthétique sans faille est idéale pour les cuisines qui nécessitent beaucoup d’entretien et de nettoyage,* explique Porcelanosa. *Moins il y a de joints, plus le résultat est hygiénique ».*

## 2\. Le papier-peint décoratif
Dans la même démarche de résistance aux exigences techniques de la cuisine, le papier-peint s'y intègre également. L'enjeu, apporter une touche décorative, sophistiquée ou minimaliste, pour insuffler davantage d'originalité dans sa cuisine. Une cuisine désormais personnalisable aux goûts de chacun, qui s'autorise la fantaisie des papiers-peints les plus divers, sortant du classique duo peinture-carrelage. *« Les tendances en matière de design pour les cuisines 2025 incluent des murs décorés de motifs luxuriants, de surfaces texturées, de combinaisons de couleurs organiques et de motifs géométriques »*, indique la marque.

## 3\. L'affirmation du dosseret de cuisine


[#image: /photos/67b5f4ebd82ae62fac99dfde]|||Ici, le dosseret de cuisine est marqué par la pose d'un revêtement *XTONE Navona* effet travertin.|||

Espace dévolu à la cuisson et à l'évier, le dosseret de cuisine devient la pièce maîtresse de la pièce. Pour cela, on le distingue visuellement du reste de la cuisine grâce aux matériaux : des placards en béton ciré ou en laque font ainsi ressortir le marbre, le travertin ou le carrelage d'un dosseret affirmé. La structuration de l'espace peut également s'effectuer par la couleur. *« Cette esthétique est plus frappante avec des étagères flottantes, évitant les casiers en hauteur, pour faire du tableau de bord un élément marquant du mur »,* conseillent les experts de Porcelanosa.

## 4\. Les textures naturelles


[#image: /photos/67b5f3a63b0f3607e830906c]|||Le revêtement *Xlight AGED CLAY* s'exprime en 120x270 au mur et 120x120 au sol.|||

Cette année, les matières organiques continuent leur percée dans les intérieurs, notamment dans les cuisines qui intègrent l'argile, le grès cérame ou le marbre pour davantage de naturalité. Des matériaux permettant, en outre, d'insuffler une délicate tactilité dans les cuisine, à rebours des surfaces lisses et hygiénistes peu sensorielles. *« De plus, l’utilisation de ces surfaces qui recréent des pierres naturelles telles que le travertin comme revêtement de sol les rend plus faciles à entretenir*, souligne Porcelanosa. *Les tendances de la cuisine pour 2025 incluront également des carreaux décoratifs avec des reliefs subtils qui ajouteront de la dimension et de l’intérêt visuel. »*

## 5\. Les finitions métalliques


[#image: /photos/67b5f504d82ae62fac99dfe0]|||Le revêtement *Xtone* terre greige de Porcelanosa se marie admirablement avec les touches métallisées.|||

Ultra contemporain, le métal s'intègre dans la cuisine. Une finition argentée pour un rendu high-tech, une touche de laiton pour un effet raffiné… ces détails peuvent prendre la forme d'un encadrement de mobilier, de poignées, de luminaires ou encore de notes métallisées purement décoratives. L'objectif ? Dépoussiérer la cuisine traditionnelle pour lui insuffler un vent de modernité. *« Actuellement, les métaux chauds tels que le cuivre, le laiton et le titane sont les plus courants,* précise la marque. *En général, deux ou trois métaux sont combinés dans le même espace, avec un matériau principal et les autres complémentaires ».*

## 6\. Les céramiques artisanales


[#image: /photos/67b5f4c6d82ae62fac99dfda]|||La mosaïque *Kronos Jade* de Porcelanosa, au vert raffiné, s'allie au carrelage *CUZCO* au sol.|||

À l'inverse, là encore, des cuisines en carrelage classique, Porcelanosa propose des revêtements en céramique à la main, à la fois élégants et singuliers. Ils apportent une note artisanale bienvenue, une ambiance légèrement plus rustique, qui confère à la pièce davantage de richesse matérielle. *« La cuisine comprendra des pièces telles que du zellige, des mosaïques et même des carreaux peints à la main »*, prévoit la marque.

## 7\. Couleurs : les tons organiques et nordiques


[#image: /photos/67b5f4f5ed2990ec470e5da4]|||Le revêtement *Xtone Amazonite* mêle les tons vert et gris.|||

En 2025, deux familles de couleurs sont privilégiées :

- **Les tonalités nordiques issues de la nature**, comme les verts forêt ou sauge, ici traduits dans un revêtement de cuisine en marbrures minérales, mêlant un vert intense à un gris très naturel. *« Le bois, surtout dans les tons moyens et foncés, sera de plus en plus visible. »*
- **Les nuances organiques chaleureuses**, comme le carrelage rose-brique chaud présenté ci-dessous. Un apport de lumière et de convivialité idéal dans la pièce du partage par excellence. *« L’augmentation de la combinaison des tons bruns et verts est due au fait qu’ils sont plus élégants et naturels dans la lumière de l’hémisphère nord »*, conclut Porcelanosa.



[#image: /photos/67b5f4e1ed2990ec470e5da2]|||Le carrelage de la collection *KENT* au ton enveloppant apporte de la chaleur dans la cuisine, en mélangeant deux types de carreaux de la collection : _Kent Warm Gloss_ et *Kent Deco Warm Gloss*.|||

+++button-group

[En savoir plus](https://www.porcelanosa.com/fr/ "En savoir plus"){: target="_blank"}

+++

The Creators of Craft Design Challenge for Emerging Creatives: Vogue Arabia joins forces with IQOS and the Italian design house Seletti to introduce Creators of Craft, a new design challenge created to spotlight emerging regional talent and foster innovation in contemporary object design. The initiative brings together two distinct creative perspectives united by an interest in how design shapes everyday life.

IQOS develops its products with an emphasis on usability and how design shapes everyday interactions. This approach also extends to its creative collaborations, including a recent project with Kuwaiti artist Abrar Zenkawi for the IQOS ILUMA i launch. She customised the device with Sadu-inspired patterns and sea motifs that capture design in Kuwait through a contemporary lens. Projects like this allow the brand to explore regional perspectives within its wider design language.

Seletti has long stood apart in the design landscape for its ability to turn the ordinary into something expressive. Established in 1964, the brand has built an extensive catalogue that includes lighting, tableware and furniture defined by concept driven thinking. Its products often reference pop culture and industrial design, resulting in objects that are functional and imaginative. By challenging conventional ideas of form, Seletti encourages designers to see familiar materials from a fresh angle.

**About the challenge**

Creators of Craft unites the perspectives of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti in a regional design call. The challenge invites emerging creatives to design a modern accessory for IQOS that draws on the region’s craft heritage through a contemporary lens. The brief asks participants to present an original idea that mirrors the values of innovation and artistic integrity. This opportunity underscores the commitment of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti to nurturing talent and contributing to the design dialogue of the Middle East.

**How to participate**

Residents of the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Lebanon, and Egypt are required to submit a sketch or digital render of their design alongside a short explanation of the idea. Entries will be evaluated by a jury comprising Manuel Arnaut, Head of Editorial Content at Vogue Arabia; Yann Marois, Vice President of Brand Design and Creative at Philip Morris International; Stefano Seletti, Founder and Creative Director of Seletti; and Nadine Kanso, Founder and Creative Director of Bil Arabi. Up to five finalists will receive an IQOS-funded budget to build a prototype for Jury review before the Creators of Craft event in Dubai (17–19 April 2026), where one winner will be chosen.

**The Award**

The winner will be revealed at the April 2026 Creators of Craft event in Dubai and will earn a commissioned IQOS design project, an all-expenses-paid trip to Milan Design Week, and an exclusive Vogue Arabia print feature. They will also receive access to masterclasses led by Nadine Kanso and Stefano Seletti.

*Apply today before the deadline of 9 January 2026. To participate, click [here](https://www.voguearabia.com/sponsored/article/creatorsofcraft).*

*IQOS is a brand of smoke-free product that is not risk-free and provides nicotine, which is addictive.*

Tour a Modern Dubai Villa Where a Sculptural Staircase Sets the Scene: There's a particular moment when visitors first step into this modern Dubai villa that captures the essence of what designers Meher Sahney and Bani Singh of GroundedDesign have achieved. As eyes travel upward, following the [sculptural sweep of a staircase that ribbons](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/a-spiral-staircase-is-the-star-attraction-in-this-theatrical-naples-apartment){: target="_blank"} through the entrance, the villa's interior concept reveals itself: this is a home where clean architectural precision meets organic fluidity, where each curve and contour has been meticulously considered.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67ee32f9931c635e2e7652f2]

[#image: /photos/67ee32ff931c635e2e7652f4]

+++

[Set within Dubai's prestigious Jumeirah Golf Estates](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-most-beautiful-homes-in-dubai){: target="_blank"}, the villa’s angular structural elements are softened by flowing lines, pristine white volumes warmed by textural finishes, and dramatic statement features balanced with intimate, cocooning spaces. “The vision was to create a seamless blend of modern minimalism and luxury,” explain the designers.

The property was redeveloped for sale, but the design was carefully tailored to meet the lifestyle of its future residents. “We envisioned discerning buyers who value both style and functionality – individuals who prioritize family connections and social gatherings while enjoying a serene living environment,” they explain. Spacious hosting areas were designed to encourage connection and flow, with open-plan living spaces [flooded with natural light](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/manhattan-apartment-exposed-brick-ceiling-transformation-cosy-cocoon-power-of-lighting){: target="_blank"} that create smooth transitions between indoor and outdoor environments. The result is a home that feels elevated yet welcoming – a space where residents can truly feel connected to both their surroundings and the people they hold dear.



[#externallink: /externallinks/6503041abb917946d989f4c1]



[#image: /photos/67ee34db22de03245a8912f3]

Completed in late 2024, it spans 10,200 square meters, including a grand entrance to announce the property’s grandeur. A custom-crafted pivot door leads into a dramatic [double-height vestibule where the staircase reveals itself](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/warm-minimalism-design-ideas-greg-natale){: target="_blank"}. Handmade in Finland by Secto Design, a cascade of pendant lighting fixtures hangs at varying heights above the staircase, accentuating the vertical volume while filling the space with warm illumination.



[#image: /photos/67ee350322de03245a8912f5]

“One of the biggest challenges in executing the curvilinear elements was integrating them with the angularity of the villa's structural shell,” shares Singh. “We collaborated closely with contractors and craftsmen to develop custom templates, allowing us to achieve smooth, [uninterrupted curves](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-new-york-penthouse-fuses-craft-with-urban-style-for-a-cosy-chic-sanctuary){: target="_blank"} that felt intentional rather than out of place.”



[#image: /photos/67ee3526931c635e2e7652fc]



[#image: /photos/67ee356b22de03245a8912f7]

The villa achieves biophilic harmony without resorting to the expected tropes of living walls or overt greenery. Instead, it's the architectural language itself – the organic curves, the natural flow of spaces – that creates a profound connection to natural principles. At the heart is [a Zen-inspired](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/zen-interior-design-joanna-parol){: target="_blank"} courtyard – a grounding visual axis visible from key vantage points throughout. It serves as a conceptual anchor that connects disparate areas of the home while reinforcing the property's connection to nature.



[#image: /photos/67ee35bc22de03245a8912f9]

“We saw the courtyard as an opportunity to [soften the villa's architectural framework](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-modern-farmhouse-look-home-in-dubai-jumeirah-golf-estates-contemporary-meets-rustic-interior){: target="_blank"},” explains Sahney. “The planting scheme was deliberately restrained, focusing on texture, height, and movement. The layering of plants creates a quiet rhythm, while subtle lighting highlights their form, adding depth throughout the day.” The result is a courtyard that feels integrated yet impactful – a serene focal point from which the villa’s architectural components “grow”.



[#image: /photos/67ee35e522de03245a8912fb]

Every aspect of the interior was meticulously curated to create a symbiotic relationship between light and space. Natural light floods the interior through expansive glazing, strategically positioned to maximize views of the surrounding landscape. The carefully considered placement of windows allows light to reflect off textured surfaces, creating a dynamic play of shadow and illumination that transforms spaces throughout the day. Outside, the organic, freeform pool echoes the villa's curvaceous architecture, creating a cohesive visual identity that extends beyond the built environment.



[#image: /photos/67ee361e931c635e2e7652fe]



[#image: /photos/67ee34a7931c635e2e7652fa]

When asked about their favorite design element, both designers highlight the master bathroom. “Positioned at the back of the property, it could have easily been overlooked, but we intentionally designed it as a hidden sanctuary,” they explain. “Upon entering, you're immediately enveloped in a serene, cave-like atmosphere that evokes the calm of a private spa.” The material palette is soft yet rich in texture, with natural stone surfaces that feel grounding and elegant. Floor-to-ceiling glazing enhances the sense of calm while maintaining privacy. “The interplay of light and shadow throughout the day creates a dynamic yet tranquil energy – a space that feels restorative and indulgent without being ostentatious.”



[#image: /photos/67ee367f22de03245a8912ff]



[#image: /photos/67ee366422de03245a8912fd]

The selection of materials speaks to a refined, tactile luxury. Interior flooring is clad in wide-plank Khars timber, lending warmth and elegance to the spaces. In contrast, textured microcement walls in the bathrooms and hallways create a monolithic feel. Porcelain tiles from Taf House introduce a play of texture in high-traffic areas, chosen for both durability and aesthetic appeal. Meanwhile, the villa's external façade is treated with a mix of contemporary cladding materials and soft stucco finishes, providing a subtly textured appearance that blends with the surrounding landscape.



[#image: /photos/67ee36a422de03245a891301]

The interior furnishings were curated with meticulous attention to both aesthetics and practicality. Standout items include bespoke seating from Tacchini and Ferm Living, which echo the villa's curvilinear architecture. The dining area is anchored by a sculptural, handcrafted table, while luxury accessories from brands like Louis Vuitton and 101 Copenhagen add refined accents throughout the space.



[#image: /photos/67ee36e622de03245a891305]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67ee36b2931c635e2e765300]

[#image: /photos/67ee36d122de03245a891303]

+++

“Each piece was selected or custom-made to fit seamlessly into the villa's design scheme,” notes Singh. “We aimed for an atmosphere of calm elegance, where every corner whispers luxury and refinement.” This philosophy of restrained sophistication extends beyond furnishings to every aspect of the home. Even the technology throughout this modern Dubai villa residence adheres to this principle of elegant discretion. State-of-the-art home automation systems remain virtually invisible, with sophisticated smart controls for lighting, climate, and entertainment integrated so seamlessly that they enhance the living experience without disrupting the visual tranquility.



[#image: /photos/67ee370722de03245a891307]

What stands out is the designers’ ability to balance contradictory elements: structural precision with organic fluidity, minimalist restraint with subtle lavishness, technological sophistication with a feel of complete serenity. “The architectural brief demanded maximizing both vertical and horizontal circulation while maintaining spatial fluidity,” explains Sahney. “Careful detailing ensured that structural elements became part of the aesthetic narrative rather than hindrances to it.”

Moody Masculine Interiors Set the Scene at This Egyptian Architect’s Contemporary Cairo Penthouse: **[Dramatic, yet effortlessly liveable](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/comedian-mohanad-alhattab-serene-villa-in-dubai-brings-a-moody-masculine-style-to-home-decor){: target="_blank"}, this contemporary Cairo penthouse is a masterful composition of opposites: of light and dark, of moody, masculine interiors and earthy accents.**

For architects, designing their own homes represents the purest distillation of their design philosophy. It’s an opportunity and a challenge: a chance to experiment boldly without client feedback or market considerations, while creating a personal sanctuary. For Fathy Ibrahim, founder and lead architect of Egypt architecture studio Insignia Design Group, that challenge called for deliberate theatricality.



[#image: /photos/681586f1aa67175ec681b514]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6815870e883cad4e07ddc045]

[#image: /photos/68158712883cad4e07ddc047]

+++

It’s a quality you can feel immediately upon entering his contemporary Cairo penthouse in the Sodic Villette compound where a dramatic entrance – clad entirely in textured black tiles and a mirrored ceiling – create an infinity-like effect. It’s a bold statement that sets the tone for what lies beyond.



[#image: /photos/6815871b883cad4e07ddc049]

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/681586fbaa67175ec681b516]

+++

“When I first stepped into the empty apartment, I saw endless possibilities,” recalls Ibrahim. “The lack of finishing meant I could design freely, without having to be controlled by existing layouts or styles.” This blank canvas offered Ibrahim, an architect accustomed to bringing others’ visions to life, the rare opportunity to explore his own design vocabulary without compromise.

Unlike typical Egyptian residences that often favour bright, open entrances, Ibrahim’s choice of a moody, theatrical threshold creates a compelling transition. “The entrance serves as a powerful statement,” he explains. “The dark tiles on the walls create a sophisticated backdrop that contrasts beautifully with the warm tones of the interiors.”

This interplay between shadow and light continues throughout the 240-square-meter residence. Moving from the dramatic entrance, the space opens up into an airy reception area where Spanish marble, rich walnut accents, and a carefully calibrated palette of earth tones create a warm, sophisticated atmosphere. The living area seamlessly connects to an open kitchen, establishing the heart of this Cairo penthouse as a space designed for connection and hospitality.



[#image: /photos/681586f0883cad4e07ddc041]

“Among all the spaces in my home, the reception area and the open [kitchen hold a special place](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/cool-kitchen-ideas-by-2024-ad100-designers){: target="_blank"} in my heart,” Ibrahim shares. “This space beautifully embodies the spirit of connection and hospitality, making it the heart of my home.” At every point, Ibrahim’s architectural background is evident in his meticulous approach to materiality, showcasing a harmonious dialogue between contrasting textures: slick marble surfaces play against plush fabrics, while reflective elements balance the solidity of natural stone.



[#image: /photos/681587170195b572a3822b1e]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/681586f1274ac810407c6094]

[#image: /photos/681587070195b572a3822b18]

+++

“The decision to use black Spanish marble was inspired by its elegance and timeless appeal,” he explains. For the TV wall, Ibrahim selected a unique marble that serves as a focal point, while Patagonia marble (renowned for its vibrant patterns) frames a sculptural piece in the living area.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/681586fd05d531b698f5e110]

[#image: /photos/681586fc0b0ec64e6da3f35b]

+++

## Must Read: [This Moscow Home With Its Own Hammam Is the Ultimate Wellness Flex](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-moscow-home-with-its-own-hammam-is-the-ultimate-wellness-flex){: target="_blank"}
In the main bathroom, antique brown porcelain and open-book marble create a refined, spa-like retreat. The marble’s dramatic veining has been carefully matched to form a mirrored pattern that evokes the feel of a contemporary artwork, elevating the everyday into an experience of visual pleasure. This attention to detail continues throughout the penthouse, where a careful balance is struck between open, communal areas and more intimate, personal spaces. While the reception and kitchen are designed for gathering and connection, the bedrooms retreat into quieter atmospheres.



[#image: /photos/68158715a541756600abbd7a]

“The principal bedroom is a true sanctuary, embodying my unique style through the use of black colours, marble, mirrors, and wood accents,” Ibrahim says. Here, he continues a nuanced approach to masculine interiors, with dark tones creating [a sophisticated, cocoon-like atmosphere](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-nature-inspired-interiors-palette-cashmere-blanket-effect-for-minimal-apartment-in-lisbon){: target="_blank"}. A custom headboard made of black stone serves as a dramatic focal point, while wood-clad walls introduce warmth and texture.

## Must Read: [Masculine Bedroom Design Anchors This Art-Filled New York Loft](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/masculine-bedroom-design-ideas-neal-thomas){: target="_blank"}


[#image: /photos/681586ffa541756600abbd78]

In contrast, the guest room presents a lighter aspect of Ibrahim’s design sensibility. “The guest room is designed with modern light colours that promote a sense of freshness and tranquillity,” he explains, “making it a perfect space for visitors to unwind.”

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68158715381020cde4ad3910]

[#image: /photos/6815871b274ac810407c6096]

+++

Perhaps the most subtle yet impactful element of this contemporary penthouse in Cairo is its lighting design. Ibrahim has used indirect illumination to sculpt the atmosphere of each space, particularly through the strategic placement of strip lights that highlight architectural details. “In the reception area, I used LED strips along the ceiling to draw attention to the unique design,” he explains. “This indirect light creates a soft, diffused glow that highlights the elegance of the design without being overpowering.”

##  Milan Diary: [The 13 Best New Lighting Designs from Euroluce 2025 You Need To Know](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-13-best-new-lighting-designs-from-euroluce-2025-you-need-to-know){: target="_blank"}
+++group-2

[#image: /photos/681587110195b572a3822b1a]

[#image: /photos/68158707fcc0bb2316576bbb]

+++



[#image: /photos/68158717a541756600abbd7c]

This approach transforms the penthouse as day turns to night: During daylight hours, natural light washes through the space, emphasising its sculptural qualities and after dark, the carefully choreographed artificial lighting creates distinct moods in each area, from the intimate glow of the dining area (enhanced by custom shelves featuring innovative stretch fabric lighting) to the serene ambience of the bedrooms.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68158708aa67175ec681b518]

[#image: /photos/681586f1a541756600abbd74]

The crowning glory of the penthouse is its roof terrace – an urban sanctuary that provides respite from Cairo’s intensity. “The primary inspiration for the roof area was to bring the essence of nature into an urban setting,” Ibrahim says. Dark wood pergolas provide shade and structure, while [tropical plants in varying heights](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-xxl-tropical-plants-to-get-the-jungle-look-at-home){: target="_blank"} create depth and interest.



[#image: /photos/681586f0fcc0bb2316576bb5]

The roof area extends the [outdoor living space](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/7-brilliant-outdoor-living-room-ideas-to-up-your-alfresco-entertaining-game){: target="_blank"}, offering dining and lounging areas framed by lush greenery. It’s a thoughtful conclusion to a home that seamlessly balances bold statements with intimate moments, showcasing Ibrahim’s ability to create spaces that are both visually striking and deeply livable.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6815870efcc0bb2316576bbf]

[#image: /photos/6815870c83d4563fa3268faa]

+++

“My background as an architect and interior designer means my home should be a true reflection of my character – bold, elegant, and unafraid to embrace modernity,” says Ibrahim. In this Cairo penthouse, he has created not just a home but a personal design statement, and one that demonstrates how thoughtful architectural interventions, careful material selection, and a willingness to embrace contrast can transform a blank canvas into a sophisticated urban retreat. The Creators of Craft Design Challenge for Emerging Creatives: Vogue Arabia joins forces with IQOS and the Italian design house Seletti to introduce Creators of Craft, a new design challenge created to spotlight emerging regional talent and foster innovation in contemporary object design. The initiative brings together two distinct creative perspectives united by an interest in how design shapes everyday life.

IQOS develops its products with an emphasis on usability and how design shapes everyday interactions. This approach also extends to its creative collaborations, including a recent project with Kuwaiti artist Abrar Zenkawi for the IQOS ILUMA i launch. She customised the device with Sadu-inspired patterns and sea motifs that capture design in Kuwait through a contemporary lens. Projects like this allow the brand to explore regional perspectives within its wider design language.

Seletti has long stood apart in the design landscape for its ability to turn the ordinary into something expressive. Established in 1964, the brand has built an extensive catalogue that includes lighting, tableware and furniture defined by concept driven thinking. Its products often reference pop culture and industrial design, resulting in objects that are functional and imaginative. By challenging conventional ideas of form, Seletti encourages designers to see familiar materials from a fresh angle.

**About the challenge**

Creators of Craft unites the perspectives of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti in a regional design call. The challenge invites emerging creatives to design a modern accessory for IQOS that draws on the region’s craft heritage through a contemporary lens. The brief asks participants to present an original idea that mirrors the values of innovation and artistic integrity. This opportunity underscores the commitment of Vogue Arabia, IQOS and Seletti to nurturing talent and contributing to the design dialogue of the Middle East.

**How to participate**

Residents of the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Lebanon, and Egypt are required to submit a sketch or digital render of their design alongside a short explanation of the idea. Entries will be evaluated by a jury comprising Manuel Arnaut, Head of Editorial Content at Vogue Arabia; Yann Marois, Vice President of Brand Design and Creative at Philip Morris International; Stefano Seletti, Founder and Creative Director of Seletti; and Nadine Kanso, Founder and Creative Director of Bil Arabi. Up to five finalists will receive an IQOS-funded budget to build a prototype for Jury review before the Creators of Craft event in Dubai (17–19 April 2026), where one winner will be chosen.

**The Award**

The winner will be revealed at the April 2026 Creators of Craft event in Dubai and will earn a commissioned IQOS design project, an all-expenses-paid trip to Milan Design Week, and an exclusive Vogue Arabia print feature. They will also receive access to masterclasses led by Nadine Kanso and Stefano Seletti.

*Apply today before the deadline of 9 January 2026. To participate, click [here](https://www.voguearabia.com/sponsored/article/creatorsofcraft).*

*IQOS is a brand of smoke-free product that is not risk-free and provides nicotine, which is addictive.*

7 sustainable architecture insights for India from BIG's iconic projects: “In *Formgivi*ng. *An Architectural Future History*, Bjarke Ingels writes that as architects, we must give something back to society,” begins Kai-Uwe Bergmann, partner at the globally renowned Bjarke Ingels Group (BIG). The ethos of sustainable architecture (an overused and often abused encapsulation of a branch of architecture that leaves much to interpretation) is being delightfully reshaped by BIG—moving away from a paradigm of limitation to one of opportunity. From hybrid power plants to community-driven [public parks](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ahmedabads-parimal-garden-offers-a-poignant-reflection-of-the-city-since-the-1950s-prabhakar-bhagwat-landscape-architecture/){: target="_blank"}, the design studio has long championed the idea that sustainability should not only be functional—but also joyful, social, and deeply contextual.



[#image: /photos/67f4d7af2f0b5ef9e021a55e]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

In conversation with *AD* India, Bergmann elaborates on this understanding of the social and sustainable architecture that drives BIG’s iconic projects, each of them offering not only a design strategy but a philosophical framework that can be meaningfully applied across the globe, and particularly to India's diverse, rapidly urbanising context.

## Make Sustainability Stick
Before studying their projects, it is important to note how BIG’s understanding of social sustainability guides them. As Bergmann points out, the classical understanding of the term innately involves some sense of limiting oneself, or spelling out a reduction in the quality of life for the greater good. BIG’s approach of enhancing through sustainability is different, one they have aptly termed ‘Hedonistic Sustainability’, and it begins with a simple question: *In what world do we actually want to live?*



[#image: /photos/67f4d81462d1bebe58149915]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark.*|||

“There is a greater chance that people will change their habits if sustainability contributes to improving everyone’s lives. I believe this is also true in India, where the greater populace would rather move towards something that increases the quality of their daily life than limiting their choices,” Bergmann rightly points out. It is this sense of collective, communal joy that BIG seeks to channel through the power of design.

## CopenHill, Copenhagen: Infrastructure as an Educational Experience


[#image: /photos/67f4d64562d1bebe5814990f]|||*Copenhill in Copenhagen presents an industrial plant reimagined as a leisure hub.*|||

In the outskirts of Copenhagen, CopenHill is a power plant designed to incinerate waste and convert it into clean energy for 150,000 homes, while simultaneously offering a [lush green rooftop](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-vastu-compliant-garden-home-in-mumbai-with-a-2200-square-foot-terrace/){: target="_blank"} replete with community functions—presenting a hybrid industrial plant and leisure park. “The concept includes a didactic strand: once onsite, visitors find out how waste incinerator plants function, an important part of a functioning society,” Bergmann elaborates.



[#image: /photos/67f4d63e62d1bebe5814990d]|||*Copenhill, Copenhagen*|||

With India’s pace of growth, there exists an enormous need for future energy sources. While these should primarily come from renewables, Bergmann speaks about the possibility of using recycling, upcycling and new technologies to create additional energy loops. Urban India urgently needs to shift public perception around waste and energy and projects like CopenHill show that essential infrastructure doesn’t need to be hidden—it can be celebrated, raising environmental literacy through integrating learning and leisure opportunities.

## Google Bay View Campus: Harnessing Solar Power


[#image: /photos/67f4d69e0dddd9765880c120]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

The shimmering, dragon-scale roof of the sprawling Google Bay View Campus in Silicon Valley, California (designed by BIG—Bjarke Ingels Group and [Heatherwick Studios](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/you-wont-believe-work-gets-done-at-these-three-google-offices/){: target="_blank"} in close collaboration with Google) might resemble a sprawling piece of futuristic origami, but actually boasts a photovoltaic roof made of 50,000 solar modules that ripple across the surface, expected to produce 90% of the campus’ energy requirements. “When it comes to choosing a project’s structural material, we carefully examine in advance what makes the most structural sense onsite, precisely with regard to the energy concept or the building’s life cycle,” Bergmann explains.



[#image: /photos/67f4d69c0dddd9765880c11f]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

In India, the abundance of sunlight during the summer months or rainwater during the monsoon season opens doors to immense possibilities. With the still-scarce adoption of techniques that harness these elemental powers, projects like the [Google Campus](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/content/googles-new-1-billion-london-hq-massive-dubbed-landscraper/){: target="_blank"} serve as a prescient reminder that technology can be reframed not as a clunky afterthought, but as an aesthetic gesture.

## Superkilen: Creation through Public Participation


[#image: /photos/67f4d6f262d1bebe58149911]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark brought to life through public participation.*|||

“Architecture must not be despotic, but represent a dialogue with context, history, and tradition. It should be an osmotic process of exchange and reciprocal enrichment,” says Bergmann. A large proportion of BIG’s socially sustainable projects employ public participation as a driving force for change. In Superkilen, an urban landscape park in an ethnically diverse neighbourhoods in Denmark with 60 different nationalities, BIG invited citizens to nominate objects from their home countries—Moroccan fountains, Russian neon signs, Indian benches, etc—to incorporate into the park’s identity, lending a sense of ownership to this [communal space](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-indian-courtyards-that-truly-exemplify-indoor-outdoor-living/){: target="_blank"}.



[#image: /photos/67f4d6e12f0b5ef9e021a55c]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark*|||

“By transforming public procedure into proactive proposition, we curated a park for the people, by the people—peer-to-peer design,” he says. In Indian cities teeming with plural, informal voices left out of formal planning, this serves as a stark reminder that participatory design can reclaim ideas of true democratic urbanism.

## BIG U: Climactic Resilience to Cultural Richness
In response to the devastation of Hurricane Sandy, BIG proposed the BIG U: a 16-kilometre-long flood protection system wrapping around Lower Manhattan. But it doesn’t just block water; it creates greenways, markets, [amphitheatres](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-pune-design-studio-marries-art-food-music-and-mindful-living-experiences/){: target="_blank"}, and open spaces for residents to use year-round. “Every place has a soul, a genius loci that must be listened to, understood, and interpreted,” emphasises Bergmann, that goes beyond function in architecture to the culture of the city.

With coastal cities in India facing increased climatic threats as development continues, planning goals need to go beyond utilitarian protection to adaptable, human-centric urban systems. The BIG U model offers a compelling reframing: climate responses as an opportunity for cultural activation.

## Telosa: Building Future Cities Consciously


[#image: /photos/67f4d7af62d1bebe58149913]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

How does one go about building a city from scratch? [Telosa](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/step-into-a-dollar500-billion-city-of-the-future-dreamed-up-by-an-american-billionaire/){: target="_blank"}, a proposed city for five million residents in the American desert, designed with walkability, renewable energy, clean air, and shared equity as its founding principles serves as an example where even the economic model upon which the city is founded was rethought. The founder Marc Lore is laying the groundwork for an equitable urban environment, bolstered by BIG’s vision for accessible and healthy urban spaces.



[#image: /photos/67f4d8650dddd9765880c122]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

“In India, the sheer magnitude of city-making that is necessary to keep up with its population growth means that entire neighbourhoods and cities are being planned informally, and don’t always take into consideration the many systems needed to sustain them. This means that they grow at such a speed that mobility systems can break down,” Bergmann cautions. Clean water resources, public transportation systems, and [renewable energy](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/sponsored/story/discover-how-this-brands-advanced-technology-is-shaping-contemporary-spaces/){: target="_blank"} sources are at the root of Telosa’s thinking and should also be considered India - as any project, no matter what its size, will tax the country’s already exhausted systems.

## Drawing Lessons from the Past
Bergmann reminds us that sustainability was inherent to Indian architecture (shaded courtyards, generously laid-out [stepwells](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-stepwell-in-sudi-will-be-resorted-to-its-former-glory-by-the-deccan-heritage-foundation/){: target="_blank"}, evaporative cooling through public ponds of water benefiting the society, not just the patron who built them, and more). He also references dharamshalas in India. “I was always taken by these structures, which are simple, pragmatic, and well placed in the centres of towns to offer any passing traveler a place to rest their feet and meet local inhabitants,” he reveals. For India, where social sustainability was once such an intrinsic part of local architecture, it might just be a better matter of getting back to the roots.



[#image: /photos/67f4da230dddd9765880c124]|||*Bjarke Ingels Group’s monograph, Formgiving. An Architectural Future History.*|||

Bergmann’s observations and subsequent insights, from his research and time in India as a collaborator at [Conscious Collective](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/conscious-collective-by-godrej-and-boyce-heralds-innovations-in-sustainable-design/){: target="_blank"} by Godrej & Boyce, reveal that in the race to build bigger and better, there exists a prescient reminder for India that sustainable architecture isn’t limited to certifications and boxes to check—it needs to be a mindset that intersects imagination, empathy and equity.



[#cneembed: script/video/67dbd81942662d4a29f09882.js?aspectRatio=16x9]

In response to the devastation of Hurricane Sandy, BIG proposed the BIG U: a 16-kilometre-long flood protection system wrapping around Lower Manhattan. But it doesn’t just block water; it creates greenways, markets, [amphitheatres](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-pune-design-studio-marries-art-food-music-and-mindful-living-experiences/){: target="_blank"}, and open spaces for residents to use year-round. “Every place has a soul, a genius loci that must be listened to, understood, and interpreted,” emphasises Bergmann, that goes beyond function in architecture to the culture of the city.

With coastal cities in India facing increased climatic threats as development continues, planning goals need to go beyond utilitarian protection to adaptable, human-centric urban systems. The BIG U model offers a compelling reframing: climate responses as an opportunity for cultural activation.

## Telosa: Building Future Cities Consciously


[#image: /photos/67f4d7af62d1bebe58149913]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

How does one go about building a city from scratch? [Telosa](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/step-into-a-dollar500-billion-city-of-the-future-dreamed-up-by-an-american-billionaire/){: target="_blank"}, a proposed city for five million residents in the American desert, designed with walkability, renewable energy, clean air, and shared equity as its founding principles serves as an example where even the economic model upon which the city is founded was rethought. The founder Marc Lore is laying the groundwork for an equitable urban environment, bolstered by BIG’s vision for accessible and healthy urban spaces.



[#image: /photos/67f4d8650dddd9765880c122]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

“In India, the sheer magnitude of city-making that is necessary to keep up with its population growth means that entire neighbourhoods and cities are being planned informally, and don’t always take into consideration the many systems needed to sustain them. This means that they grow at such a speed that mobility systems can break down,” Bergmann cautions. Clean water resources, public transportation systems, and [renewable energy](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/sponsored/story/discover-how-this-brands-advanced-technology-is-shaping-contemporary-spaces/){: target="_blank"} sources are at the root of Telosa’s thinking and should also be considered India - as any project, no matter what its size, will tax the country’s already exhausted systems.

## Drawing Lessons from the Past
Bergmann reminds us that sustainability was inherent to Indian architecture (shaded courtyards, generously laid-out [stepwells](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-stepwell-in-sudi-will-be-resorted-to-its-former-glory-by-the-deccan-heritage-foundation/){: target="_blank"}, evaporative cooling through public ponds of water benefiting the society, not just the patron who built them, and more). He also references dharamshalas in India. “I was always taken by these structures, which are simple, pragmatic, and well placed in the centres of towns to offer any passing traveler a place to rest their feet and meet local inhabitants,” he reveals. For India, where social sustainability was once such an intrinsic part of local architecture, it might just be a better matter of getting back to the roots.



[#image: /photos/67f4da230dddd9765880c124]|||*Bjarke Ingels Group’s monograph, Formgiving. An Architectural Future History.*|||

Bergmann’s observations and subsequent insights, from his research and time in India as a collaborator at [Conscious Collective](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/conscious-collective-by-godrej-and-boyce-heralds-innovations-in-sustainable-design/){: target="_blank"} by Godrej & Boyce, reveal that in the race to build bigger and better, there exists a prescient reminder for India that sustainable architecture isn’t limited to certifications and boxes to check—it needs to be a mindset that intersects imagination, empathy and equity.
